data.topic: Global game-development employment (headcount LEVELS), 2015-2026, with US and Japan breakdowns. Distinguishes DIRECT game-industry employment from TOTAL-supported jobs (incl. indirect/induced) and from studio/developer-only subsets. US figures are the strongest (ESA/TEConomy primary series for 2019/2023/2025); Global and Japan are largely estimates with divergent methodologies (bottom-up developer counts vs top-down total-workforce). All layoff figures are kept strictly separate from headcount levels.

series.label: US video game industry — DIRECT jobs (ESA/TEConomy, industry-wide: software+hardware+retail)

points.source: ESA 'Video Games in the 21st Century: 2020 Economic Impact Report' (TEConomy). Exact: 143,045 direct jobs at 11,427 establishments in 2019. https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Video-Games-in-the-21st-Century-2020-Economic-Impact-Report-Final.pdf
points.note: PRIMARY. Basis: direct US employment across software (developers/studios/publishers), consoles/hardware/peripherals, and video-game retail. This is the classic ~143k 'direct' number. Software sector dominates (~74%).

points.source: ESA '2024 Economic Impact Report' (TEConomy), data year 2023. Exact: 104,080 direct jobs. https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/EIR_ESA_2024.pdf
points.note: PRIMARY. Same direct-employment basis as 2019. Not directly comparable to 2019 due to methodology upgrades (LinkedIn/LTI location, tighter small-firm & remote-worker capture, more conservative treatment of diversified/hardware firms) — so the 143k->104k drop is partly definitional, not pure job loss.

points.source: ESA '2026 Economic Impact Report' (TEConomy), data captured Nov-Dec 2025. Exact: 82,930 direct jobs (also stated 82,971) at 8,998 sites. https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/EIR_2026_FINAL.pdf
points.note: PRIMARY. Report explicitly states a decline of >21,100 workers vs the 2023 database, with largest losses in software. Software=63,248; consoles/hardware=3,481; retail=13,099; game-dev services=2,449 (approx). Same direct-employment basis.

series.label: US video game industry — TOTAL jobs supported (direct + indirect + induced, ESA/TEConomy)

points.source: ESA 2020 EIR: 428,646 total US jobs supported (employment multiplier ~3.0). https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Video-Games-in-the-21st-Century-2020-Economic-Impact-Report-Final.pdf
points.note: TOTAL, not direct. Includes supplier (indirect) and spending-driven (induced) jobs. Do NOT compare to developer headcount.

points.source: ESA 2024 EIR: 350,015 total US jobs supported; multiplier 3.36. https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/EIR_ESA_2024.pdf
points.note: TOTAL supported (the widely-quoted '350,000 jobs' headline). $101.4B total economic output.

points.source: ESA 2026 EIR: 250,838 total US jobs supported (~251,000); multiplier 3.02. https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/EIR_2026_FINAL.pdf
points.note: TOTAL supported. $95.8B output. Drop from 350k reflects both lower direct base and lower multiplier.

series.label: US — studio/developer/dev-team employees only (subset of ESA software sector)

points.source: ESA 2024 EIR: 'nearly 36,000 employees working within studios, independent developer teams or home offices'. https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/EIR_ESA_2024.pdf
points.note: DEVELOPERS-ONLY subset (narrowest definition). Software sector total was larger (~77k in 2023); this ~36k is the studio/dev-team slice specifically.

points.source: ESA 2026 EIR: '34,070 employees working within studios, independent developer teams, or home offices' (of 63,248 total software-sector employees). https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/EIR_2026_FINAL.pdf
points.note: DEVELOPERS-ONLY subset. Closest ESA figure to a pure 'game developer' headcount. Rest of the 63,248 software total is developer/publisher + publisher + software-other.

series.label: Global game-DEVELOPMENT workforce — bottom-up 'developers' estimate (Amir Satvat / ASGC)

points.source: Amir Satvat / GamesBeat, 'How many people work in the game industry? Two different views' (Apr 26, 2025). Satvat's original bottom-up figure ~230,000, built by aggregating open-role/job-posting data across ~3,600-5,000 employers and estimating headcount. https://gamesbeat.com/how-many-people-work-in-the-game-industry-two-different-views/
points.note: ESTIMATE. Bottom-up, DEVELOPER-focused (core game dev, excludes much of publishing/marketing/admin and under-covers APAC). Lower bound of the credible range.

points.source: Amir Satvat, revised figure via GamesBeat (updated Jun 18, 2025), after collaboration with Game Industry Coffee Chat: ~350,000 including support functions and better APAC coverage. https://gamesbeat.com/how-many-people-work-in-the-game-industry-two-different-views/
points.note: ESTIMATE. Revised broader count (adds support functions + underrepresented regions). Point-in-time, not a time series; placed at 2025 as the current best bottom-up figure.

series.label: Global game industry workforce — top-down TOTAL estimate (Game Industry Coffee Chat / GICC)

points.source: Game Industry Coffee Chat top-down estimate via GamesBeat (2025): range 740,000-900,000, median ~833,000 across 25,000+ companies. https://gamesbeat.com/how-many-people-work-in-the-game-industry-two-different-views/
points.note: ESTIMATE, BROADEST basis: developers + publishers across PC/console/mobile/AR-VR + external devs/outsourcers, across art/engineering/production/marketing/admin. ~2.4-3.6x Satvat's dev-focused count — illustrates how much the definition drives the number.

series.label: Global 'game developers' incl. hobbyists (definitional outlier — NOT professional workforce)

points.source: Widely-circulated '11.1 million game developers worldwide, 2024' figure (engine/tool ecosystems, e.g. Unity-style counts) reported via Statista-adjacent and trade summaries. https://www.statista.com/statistics/453790/game-developer-employment-status-worldwide/
points.note: ESTIMATE / OUTLIER. Counts anyone who makes games including hobbyists and students; the paid professional workforce is ~1-1.5M by the same summaries, and the paid CORE-DEV workforce far smaller (see Satvat 230-350k). Included only to flag the methodological gap — do NOT treat as employment.

series.label: Japan — total game-RELATED industry workforce estimate (CESA Game Industry Report)

points.source: CESA 'Game Industry Report' (ゲーム産業レポート), reported via GameDeveloper/Automaton/AnimeNewsNetwork summaries (2024-2025): domestic game-related workforce ~200,000 (約20万人), avg age 37, avg tenure ~7 years. https://www.cesa.or.jp/action/industry-research/2024/
points.note: ESTIMATE, BROAD basis: total game-RELATED industry in Japan (not developers-only). Analogous in breadth to a total/adjacent-jobs figure, not a pure dev count. CESA figure is survey-based (members list ~198 companies incl. Nintendo, Sony, Capcom, Bandai Namco, Sega, Square Enix, Koei Tecmo) scaled to the domestic industry.

series.label: Japan — Nintendo consolidated headcount (single-firm anchor, FY end 31 Mar)

points.source: Nintendo Co., Ltd. Annual Report 2025 (yuho equivalent), 'Number of employees As of March 31, 2025: 8,205' (consolidated group, excl. temporary/part-time). https://www.nintendo.co.jp/ir/en/library/annual_report/
points.note: PRIMARY, single-firm. FISCAL YEAR ends 31 Mar 2025 -> calendarYear 2025 for comparability. CONSOLIDATED GROUP basis (global group, not Japan-only, not developers-only). Included as a hard primary anchor for JP scale; a large share of the ~200k CESA total sits across a handful of such publishers plus their supply chain.

data.summary:
There is no single authoritative time series for global game-DEVELOPMENT headcount; the number you get depends almost entirely on the definition, so I built parallel series and kept DIRECT employment, TOTAL-supported jobs, developer-only subsets, and layoffs strictly separate.

US is the best-documented. ESA/TEConomy Economic Impact Reports (primary) give DIRECT US game-industry employment of 143,045 (2019) -> 104,080 (2023) -> 82,930 (2025), and TOTAL supported jobs (incl. indirect/induced) of 428,646 -> 350,015 -> 250,838. The 143k->104k drop is partly a methodology change (2023 report tightened small-firm/remote/hardware capture), but the 104k->83k drop is real contraction: the 2026 report explicitly notes >21,100 fewer workers than 2023, concentrated in software. The narrowest 'pure developer' US figure is the studio/dev-team subset: ~36,000 (2023) -> 34,070 (2025).

Global is estimate-only and methodologically split. Bottom-up (Amir Satvat/ASGC, from job-posting/open-role aggregation) gives ~230,000 core developers, revised up to ~350,000 when adding support functions and better Asia-Pacific coverage (2025). Top-down (Game Industry Coffee Chat) gives 740,000-900,000 (median ~833,000) for the whole industry across all functions. A separate '11.1M developers worldwide' figure includes hobbyists and is not an employment number. So credible PROFESSIONAL global game-dev headcount sits roughly in the 230k-900k band depending on scope.

Japan (CESA Game Industry Report) estimates ~200,000 in the total game-RELATED workforce (broad, not developers-only); a hard single-firm anchor is Nintendo's 8,205 consolidated employees (FY end 31 Mar 2025).

Layoffs are a FLOW, not a level, and are reported separately: Satvat's gross gaming layoffs run ~8,500 (2022), ~10,500 (2023), 15,631 (2024, the peak), 9,053 (2025), with ~7,500 projected for 2026 — a cumulative ~43k+ over 2022-2025 that partly explains the US direct-employment decline. Do not net these against headcount levels.

Data gaps: no direct Newzoo published headcount time series was retrievable (Newzoo's free reports cover revenue/players, not workforce); pre-2019 US and a continuous global annual series do not exist from primary sources. Acquisition distortions do not affect these aggregate industry series (they matter for single-firm counts like Microsoft/Activision Oct 2023 or Take-Two/Zynga May 2022, which are not used here).
findings: US DIRECT game-industry employment (ESA/TEConomy, primary): 143,045 (2019) -> 104,080 (2023) -> 82,930 (2025). This is the industry-wide direct count (software+hardware+retail), NOT total supported jobs.
US TOTAL supported jobs (direct+indirect+induced): 428,646 (2019) -> 350,015 (2023) -> 250,838 (2025). The famous '350,000 US jobs' is this TOTAL figure for 2023, not a developer count.

The 143k->104k US drop (2019->2023) is substantially a METHODOLOGY change (2024 report tightened small-firm, remote-worker, and diversified/hardware-firm capture); the 104k->83k drop (2023->2025) is REAL: 2026 report cites >21,100 fewer workers vs 2023, concentrated in software.
Narrowest US 'pure developer' figure = studio/dev-team employees: ~36,000 (2023) -> 34,070 (2025). Total US software sector was 63,248 in 2025.

Global professional game-dev headcount is definition-driven: Satvat bottom-up ~230,000 core devs (2024), revised ~350,000 with support+APAC (2025); GICC top-down 740,000-900,000 (median 833,000) for the whole industry. The '11.1M developers' stat includes hobbyists and is not employment.

Japan: CESA estimates ~200,000 in the total game-RELATED workforce (broad, survey-based, ~198 member companies scaled up); this is NOT developers-only. Nintendo alone = 8,205 consolidated employees (FY end 31 Mar 2025).
LAYOFFS (gross flow, kept separate from levels; Satvat/ASGC): ~8,500 (2022), ~10,500 (2023), 15,631 (2024 peak), 9,053 (2025), ~7,500 projected (2026); cumulative ~43k+ over 2022-2025.

Fiscal-year note: Nintendo, and JP publishers generally, close 31 Mar; the 8,205 figure is FY2024 ending 31 Mar 2025, mapped to calendarYear 2025. ESA data years are calendar-aligned (2025 data captured Nov-Dec 2025).
caveats: No published Newzoo workforce/headcount time series was retrievable. Newzoo's free Global Games Market Reports cover revenue and player numbers, not employment; the '330,000 employed globally in 2023' figure attributed to Newzoo in secondary blogs could not be verified against a primary Newzoo document and should be treated as unconfirmed.

The four series (DIRECT / TOTAL-supported / developer-only subset / global estimates) are on DIFFERENT bases and must not be plotted as one line. Direct US (~83k, 2025) vs total US supported (~251k) vs global dev estimate (230-350k) vs global total (833k) differ by scope, not just geography.

US ESA figures across years are only partly comparable: the 2024 report changed methodology vs 2020 (LinkedIn/LTI geolocation, better small-firm and remote-worker capture, more conservative hardware/diversified-firm treatment). Treat the 2019 vs 2023 vs 2025 as a broadly consistent but not perfectly like-for-like series.

Global figures for 2024/2025 are point estimates from Satvat/GICC, not annual back-series; there is no credible year-by-year global development-headcount series 2015-2026 from any single primary source. Pre-2019 continuous data does not exist in the sources used.

Japan ~200,000 is CESA's broad game-related figure; the developers-only core is much smaller (analysts cite a core game industry on the order of tens of thousands). The exact CESA developer-only subset and its year were not confirmed from a primary CESA PDF in this pass and should be verified against the CESA Game Industry Report 2024/2025 directly.

Acquisition distortions (e.g., Microsoft/Activision Oct 2023, Take-Two/Zynga May 2022) affect single-firm headcounts but do NOT distort the aggregate industry series used here; the only single-firm anchor included is Nintendo, which had no such distortion.
Layoff counts are GROSS cuts (flow), not net change and not levels. They are provided for context on the US decline but were deliberately excluded from all headcount-level series.

Web search is US-centric; Japanese-language CESA primary PDFs were only reached via secondary summaries (GameDeveloper, Automaton, AnimeNewsNetwork, Famitsu), so JP totals carry more uncertainty than the US ESA numbers.
sources: ESA / TEConomy Partners — 'Video Games in the 21st Century: The 2026 Economic Impact Report' (2025 data): https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/EIR_2026_FINAL.pdf
ESA / TEConomy Partners — '2024 Economic Impact Report' (2023 data): https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/EIR_ESA_2024.pdf

ESA / TEConomy Partners — '2020 Economic Impact Report' (2019 data, 143,045 direct jobs): https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Video-Games-in-the-21st-Century-2020-Economic-Impact-Report-Final.pdf
ESA — Economic Impact / State Impact Map landing: https://www.theesa.com/resources/2026-economic-impact-report/

Amir Satvat via GamesBeat — 'How many people work in the game industry? Two different views' (230k vs 350k vs 740k-900k/833k): https://gamesbeat.com/how-many-people-work-in-the-game-industry-two-different-views/

Amir Satvat via GamesBeat — 'Gaming layoffs slowed from 15,631 in 2024 to 9,053 in 2025' (layoff series 2022-2026): https://gamesbeat.com/gaming-layoffs-slowed-from-15631-in-2024-to-9053-in-2025-amir-satvat/
CESA — Game Industry Report (ゲーム産業レポート) 2024 landing: https://www.cesa.or.jp/action/industry-research/2024/

CESA report summaries (~200k Japan workforce, salaries, AI adoption) via AnimeNewsNetwork: https://www.animenewsnetwork.com/news/2025-09-29/cesa-report-half-of-japanese-game-studios-use-ai-in-development/ and Automaton West / Famitsu
Nintendo Co., Ltd. — Annual Report 2025 (FY ended 31 Mar 2025; 8,205 consolidated employees): https://www.nintendo.co.jp/ir/en/library/annual_report/
Statista — Global game developer employment status/type/region survey series (methodology context, 2021-2023): https://www.statista.com/statistics/453790/game-developer-employment-status-worldwide/

data.topic: The 2017–2022 games-industry hiring wave: how many game-dev jobs were created globally and by region, % growth, and organic vs M&A-driven expansion
data.summary: Between 2017 and 2022 the games industry ran one of the strongest hiring booms in its history, but no single authoritative headcount series spans it cleanly, so the picture must be triangulated. Best available global estimate (Amir Satvat / ASGC, drawing on GICC and public data) puts the total games workforce at roughly 750,000 by 2022, with the 2017–2022 window adding an estimated 150,000–250,000 net new roles (~30,000–50,000/yr) — very roughly +30–45% off a ~530–600k 2017 base. Growth was wildly uneven by region: near-flat/modest in mature North America, but +40% to +200%+ in smaller ecosystems (Canada +48%, UK +58%, Sweden +88%, Poland +150%, Brazil +210%). The clearest hard evidence is publisher headcount from primary filings, which shows large ORGANIC expansion: EA 8,800→12,900 (+47%), Ubisoft 11,907→20,665 (+74%), Take-Two 3,707→7,799 (+110%, and this is BEFORE the ~3,000-person Zynga deal that closed May 2022), Activision Blizzard ~9,800→~13,000 (+33%), Nintendo 5,166→6,717 (+30%). Critical caveat: the ESA U.S. economic-impact series (65k direct 2016 → 143,045 direct 2019 → 104,080 direct 2023) is NOT year-over-year comparable because ESA changed its measurement methodology twice, so it cannot be used to state a clean U.S. 2017→2022 growth rate. Take-Two's headline jump becomes M&A-driven only in FY2023 (Zynga); the truly M&A-distorted step-changes (Microsoft+Activision, Take-Two+Zynga, Sony+Bungie, Embracer's roll-up) all land in 2022–2023 and mostly reshuffle rather than create jobs.

series.label: Global games workforce (all functions; Satvat/ASGC estimate)

points.note: Back-derived: 2022 ~750k minus estimated 150k-250k added over the 5-yr window implies a ~500k-600k 2017 base; midpoint used. NOT an official count.
points.source: Amir Satvat / ASGC via GamesBeat (Apr 2025) — 'global industry likely added 150,000-250,000 roles 2017-2022'

points.note: Satvat/ASGC total games workforce estimate; all job functions (dev, publishing, ops, QA, etc.), not developers-only. Newzoo does not publish a comparable headcount series.
points.source: Amir Satvat / ASGC, GamesBeat 'Global gaming's workforce grew 0.6% in 4 years' (2025)

series.label: Net new games roles created worldwide during 2017-2022 wave

points.note: Midpoint of the stated 150,000-250,000 range added over the five-year window (~30k-50k/yr). This is the direct answer to 'how many jobs were created'. Gross hiring was higher; this is net of churn.

series.label: Electronic Arts — total employees (consolidated group)

points.note: FY end 31 Mar 2017. 'approximately 8,800 regular, full-time employees'. ORGANIC.
FY end 31 Mar 2022. 'approximately 12,900 people globally, 64% located internationally'. +47% vs 2017, essentially all ORGANIC (Codemasters ~700 acq. Feb 2021 is the only notable M&A, small).

series.label: Ubisoft — total Group headcount (team members)

points.note: Headcount at 31 Mar 2017 = 11,907 (total group 12,012 before restating out Hybride's 105). Up 11.6% YoY even then. ORGANIC.
20,665 team members at 31 Mar 2022 (avg FY headcount 20,616). +74% vs 2017. ORGANIC — Ubisoft grew via new/expanded studios, not big acquisitions. Best single illustration of the wave.

series.label: Take-Two Interactive — total employees (consolidated)

points.note: 3,707 full-time employees at 31 Mar 2017 (R&D staff 2,818). ORGANIC.

7,799 full-time employees at 31 Mar 2022 (6,042 in game development). +110% vs 2017. IMPORTANT: this is BEFORE the Zynga acquisition (~3,000 staff) which closed 23 May 2022, i.e. in FY2023 — so the FY2022 figure is ORGANIC. The M&A step-change shows up the following year (~11,580 by FY2023).

series.label: Activision Blizzard — total employees (consolidated)

points.note: At 31 Dec 2017, 'approximately 9,800 total full-time and part-time employees'. Includes King (mobile). FY end 31 Dec.

At 31 Dec 2022, 'approximately 13,000 full-time and part-time non-temporary' employees. +33% vs 2017, ORGANIC. (Acquisition by Microsoft closed Oct 2023 — outside this window; that is the M&A distortion for Microsoft's own headcount, not ABK's here.)

series.label: Nintendo — consolidated employees

points.note: 5,166 consolidated employees at 31 Mar 2017. FY end 31 Mar. ORGANIC.
points.source: Nintendo annual report / financial results FY2017; via GoNintendo/stockanalysis

points.note: 6,717 consolidated at 31 Mar 2022. +30% vs 2017, ORGANIC. Illustrates that even conservative JP first-party expanded materially.
points.source: Nintendo FY2022 financial results; stockanalysis.com (S&P Global data)

series.label: US video game industry — DIRECT jobs (ESA Economic Impact Reports)

points.note: ESA 2017 EIR reported >65,000 direct employees (2016 data). CAUTION: narrower methodology than later reports; NOT comparable to the 2019 figure.

ESA 2020 EIR: 143,045 direct jobs, 428,646 total jobs supported (2019 data). BUT this uses a BROAD NAICS scope incl. distribution, retail and hardware — dev/publishing is only part. The jump from 65k is largely a methodology change, not pure hiring.
points.source: ESA 2020 Economic Impact Report (Video Games in the 21st Century)

points.note: ESA 2024 EIR: 104,080 direct jobs (of which ~75,300 in software development), >350,000 total supported. Methodology tightened again vs 2020. The three data points (65k/143k/104k) are on DIFFERENT bases — do NOT read as a growth-then-decline curve.

series.label: Regional games-workforce GROWTH, 2017-2022 (% change)
series.unit: % growth over the window (levels not published)

points.note: Canada +48%. Regional % from Satvat/ASGC synthesis of national-association data; base/end levels not disclosed.
United Kingdom +58%. Consistent with TIGA/Ukie reporting of rapid UK dev-headcount growth in this period.
Sweden +88%. Nordic cluster (Embracer, Paradox, King, MTG) expansion.
Poland +150%. CD Projekt/Techland/Bloober-era boom.
Brazil +210%. Fastest-growing emerging market in the window (off a small base).

findings: SCALE OF THE WAVE: Best global estimate is ~150,000-250,000 NET new games roles created worldwide 2017-2022 (~30k-50k/yr), lifting the total games workforce to roughly 750,000 by 2022 (Satvat/ASGC). No official body publishes a clean global headcount series, so treat this as a triangulated estimate, not a filing.

ORGANIC, NOT M&A: The five big Western/JP publishers with primary filings all grew organically within the window — EA +47% (8,800→12,900), Ubisoft +74% (11,907→20,665), Take-Two +110% (3,707→7,799), Activision Blizzard +33% (9,800→13,000), Nintendo +30% (5,166→6,717). Aggregate of these five: ~39,400 (2017) → ~60,900 (2022), +55%, ~+21,500 heads, essentially all organic hiring.

M&A DISTORTIONS LAND AFTER THE WINDOW: The famous step-changes are 2022-2023 events and mostly reshuffle rather than create jobs — Take-Two+Zynga (~3,000) closed May 2022 (FY2023, so Take-Two's FY2022 7,799 is still organic); Sony+Bungie (~1,600) Jul 2022; Microsoft+Activision (~13,000) Oct 2023; Embracer's debt-fueled roll-up peaked ~17,000 in 2022 then unwound. Flag any post-2022 headcount jump at Microsoft/Take-Two/Sony/Embracer as acquisition-driven.

US ESA SERIES IS A METHODOLOGY TRAP: ESA EIR direct-jobs figures (65,000 in 2016 → 143,045 in 2019 → 104,080 in 2023) look like a boom-and-bust but are NOT comparable — ESA widened the NAICS scope for the 2020 report (adding distribution/retail/hardware) then re-tightened it. Do not compute a US 2017-vs-2022 growth rate from these. The software-development-only subset was ~75,300 in the 2023 report.

REGIONAL DIVERGENCE IS THE HEADLINE: Growth was concentrated outside mature North America — Canada +48%, UK +58%, Sweden +88%, Poland +150%, Brazil +210% over the window (Satvat/ASGC). North America grew far more slowly, which is why the SAME dataset shows near-flat global headcount 2022-2026 as NA then contracted -11.5%.

JAPAN HAS NO CLEAN INDUSTRY HEADCOUNT: CESA's Games White Papers are revenue-focused and paywalled; they do not publish an industry-wide employment total for 2017-2022. The Japanese picture must be built from major-publisher yuho (有価証券報告書) consolidated counts (Nintendo, Capcom, Square Enix, Bandai Namco, Sega Sammy, Konami), and Bandai Namco/Sega Sammy are conglomerates (toys, arcades, pachinko) so their totals overstate games headcount.

DEVELOPERS-ONLY vs TOTAL: Company figures above are total-group headcount, not developers-only. Where disclosed, Take-Two reported 6,042 of 7,799 (78%) 'working in game development' at FY2022; treat group totals as ~20-25% larger than pure dev/creative headcount.
caveats: No authoritative body (Newzoo, ESA, CESA) publishes a continuous global or US games-EMPLOYMENT headcount series for 2017-2022. Newzoo tracks revenue/players, not workforce. The global totals here rest on Amir Satvat's ASGC estimate and are labelled isEstimate=true.
The global-total and back-derived 2017 base (~570k) are estimates with wide error bars; the honest statement is '150,000-250,000 net new roles added,' not a precise level.

ESA EIR figures (65k/143k/104k) are on three DIFFERENT methodological bases and must not be strung into a trend line. Any 'US direct jobs' growth number derived from them would be an artifact of methodology changes.

Regional % growth figures (Canada +48% … Brazil +210%) come from Satvat/ASGC's synthesis of national-association data; underlying level values (base and end headcount) are not publicly itemized, so they cannot be independently reconstructed to people counts here.

Company headcounts have different fiscal-year ends: EA, Take-Two, Ubisoft, Nintendo, Capcom, Square Enix, Bandai Namco end 31 Mar; Activision Blizzard ends 31 Dec. calendarYear alignment is approximate to within a quarter.

Company totals are consolidated GROUP headcount (all functions, all geographies), not developers-only and not the US-only slice; they include non-game staff where a company is diversified (least for EA/Ubisoft/Take-Two/ABK; more for Nintendo hardware ops, and materially for conglomerates like Bandai Namco and Sega Sammy which were therefore excluded from the aggregate).

Tencent (~108,000 group, 2022) and NetEase are conglomerates where games are only one segment; their group headcounts cannot be attributed to game development and were deliberately excluded from the organic-hiring aggregate.

GROSS hiring during the wave was substantially higher than the ~150k-250k NET figure, because net is after attrition/churn; the sources do not provide a clean global gross-hires number for the window.
sources: ESA — 2017 Economic Impact Report (>65,000 direct jobs, 2016 data): https://www.theesa.com/2017-economic-impact-report/

ESA — 2020 Economic Impact Report 'Video Games in the 21st Century' (143,045 direct / 428,646 total jobs, 2019 data): https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Video-Games-in-the-21st-Century-2020-Economic-Impact-Report-Final.pdf
ESA — 2024 Economic Impact Report (104,080 direct jobs / ~75,300 in software dev, 2023 data): https://www.theesa.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/EIR_ESA_2024.pdf

Amir Satvat / ASGC — 'Global gaming's workforce grew 0.6% in 4 years, but North America's shrank 11.5%', GamesBeat (2025) — global ~750k total, 150k-250k added 2017-2022, regional % growth: https://gamesbeat.com/global-gamings-workforce-grew-0-6-in-4-years-but-north-americas-shrank-11-5-amir-satvat/
Amir Satvat / ASGC community hub: https://asgc.gg/
Electronic Arts FY2017 Form 10-K (8,800 employees, 31 Mar 2017), SEC EDGAR: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/712515/000071251517000035/ea3312017-q410kdoc.htm

data.topic: Gross game-industry layoffs by year (2022-2026), one number per major tracker per year
data.summary: Five commonly-cited trackers report GROSS game-industry layoffs (people cut, not headcount level, not net) for 2022-2026. Two data lineages dominate: (A) Farhan Noor's crowd-sourced videogamelayoffs.com — which the Obsidian "vg-layoffs" publish tracker mirrors/extends, and which Game Developer & GamesIndustry.biz year-end pieces cite — giving 8,500 (2022), 10,500 (2023), 14,600 (2024), and an incomplete ~4,000-5,300 (2025); and (B) Amir Satvat / ASGC, which accesses non-public confirmations from ~5,000 companies and runs higher: 8,500 (2022), 10,500 (2023), 15,631 (2024), 9,053-9,175 (2025), ~10,100 projected 2026. gaminglayoffs.com (Mike Straw Media) is a third, independent aggregation: ~14,900 (2024) and 8,051 confirmed / 9,231 forecast (2025). IMPORTANT: 2022 and 2023 are effectively single-source (Noor's numbers, echoed by everyone). Numbers diverge most in 2024 (14,600 vs 15,631) and 2025 (~4,000 incomplete Noor vs ~9,000+ Satvat/gaminglayoffs), because ~1/3 of layoff events have uncertain headcounts and quiet/contractor cuts go unreported. These are GROSS cuts (headcount reductions plus studio-closure staff), not consolidated-company headcount levels and not net change; no fiscal-year adjustment applies (trackers use calendar-year event dates).
findings: Two of the five 'trackers' are actually one data lineage: Farhan Noor (technical artist) runs videogamelayoffs.com, and the Obsidian 'vg-layoffs' publish site mirrors/extends that same dataset ('obsidian-adjacent'). Game Developer and GamesIndustry.biz year-end tallies quote Noor's numbers rather than counting independently. So for cross-checking, there are really THREE independent estimates: Noor/Obsidian, gaminglayoffs.com (Mike Straw), and Amir Satvat/ASGC.

2022 (~8,500) and 2023 (~10,500) are essentially SINGLE-SOURCE: both trace to Noor's crowd-sourced tracker. Satvat labels these two years 'confirmed' and adopts the same figures; every trade-press year-end piece repeats them. Treat 2022-2023 as one consensus number, not independent corroboration.

2024 is where trackers first diverge: Noor/Obsidian = 14,600; gaminglayoffs.com ~14,900; Satvat/ASGC = 15,631 (Satvat's 'estimate,' higher because he folds in non-public confirmations and gaming-specific slices of broader corporate restructures). Range ~14,600-15,650.

2025 shows the WIDEST spread and is the most methodology-sensitive year. Noor's videogamelayoffs.com/Obsidian sat at only ~4,000 (July snapshot) rising to ~5,300 (Nov 30 snapshot) but was NOT fully updated and notably excluded the July 2025 Microsoft cuts (up to ~9,000, of which several thousand were gaming). gaminglayoffs.com logged 8,051 CONFIRMED (to Nov 11) with a ~9,231 full-year forecast. Satvat/ASGC = 9,053 (later ~9,175). So 2025 = ~5,300 (incomplete Noor) up to ~9,200 (Satvat/gaminglayoffs). The Wikipedia consolidated table adopts 9,200.

2026 is early/partial. Obsidian tracker showed 'Estimated Total in 2026: 4,600' with 22 studio shutdowns as of a 07/01/2026 update. Satvat/ASGC projected ~10,100-10,133 for full-year 2026 (2,579 roles across 48 situations YTD by early July, plus ~7,554 projected). These are forecasts, not settled counts.

Regional detail exists mainly in the Satvat/ASGC dataset: ~58% of layoffs in North America, of which ~43% in California (California = ~25% of global cuts); ~22% Europe; ~81% combined North America + Europe (2026 YTD figures). Noor/Obsidian and gaminglayoffs.com log per-studio locations but do not publish aggregate regional splits.

All five report GROSS layoffs (count of people cut, by event date) and separately track STUDIO CLOSURES (Obsidian: 40+ studio shutdowns in 2024, 22 in 2026 YTD). None of these are consolidated-company headcount LEVELS and none are NET change. No acquisition distortion applies to these tracker totals (that caveat is for company headcount, e.g. Take-Two/Zynga or Microsoft/Activision) — though Microsoft's post-Activision cuts are a major component of the 2024-2025 layoff totals.

Methodology caveat repeated across sources: roughly one-third of commonly-cited layoff events have uncertain headcounts (listed with question marks, ranges, or no figure), and quiet reductions plus contractor cuts often never enter the news cycle — so all published totals are floors. An oft-cited aggregate is '~45,000 jobs lost 2022 to July 2025.'
methodologyNotes: BASIS for every number here = GROSS layoffs (people cut), aggregated by calendar-year event date from public announcements (studio statements, WARN/regulatory filings, LinkedIn, trade press) plus, for Satvat/ASGC, non-public confirmations. NOT company headcount level, NOT net change.

No fiscal-year adjustment needed: these trackers key off calendar-date layoff events, so calendarYear == reporting year. (The 31-Mar FY caveat applies to company filings like EA/Take-Two/Ubisoft/JP firms, not to these event trackers.)

'Confirmed vs estimated': Noor/Obsidian and gaminglayoffs.com totals are sums of individually-logged events but are explicitly labeled 'Estimated Total' because many events carry uncertain headcounts; gaminglayoffs.com separates a 'confirmed' count from a full-year 'forecast.' Satvat's 2022-2023 are 'confirmed'; his 2024-2026 are 'estimates/projections' (he cites a 93% forecast-accuracy record for 2025).

Divergence drivers: (1) inclusion/exclusion of gaming slices within mega-corp restructures (esp. Microsoft July 2025); (2) update lag (Noor's site was stale in 2025); (3) treatment of events with no disclosed headcount; (4) contractor and quiet cuts largely uncaptured by all trackers.

The Game Developer and GamesIndustry.biz 'year-end tallies' are NOT an independent count — they cite Farhan Noor's videogamelayoffs.com figures (8,500 / 10,500 / 14,600). I represent them as a reporting layer over Noor's data, not a fifth independent number.

Ranges given per year reflect genuine cross-tracker disagreement, widest in 2025 (~5,300 stale-Noor to ~9,200 Satvat/gaminglayoffs) and unsettled in 2026 (partial actuals ~2,600-4,600 vs ~10,100 projections).

gaminglayoffs.com (Mike Straw Media) — https://gaminglayoffs.com/ and /tracker (~14,900 in 2024; 8,051 confirmed to Nov 11 2025, ~9,231 forecast)

data.topic: NET headcount change in the games industry by year (total workforce level rising/falling), distinct from gross layoffs — the churn/re-hiring vs structural-decline question

series.label: Global games workforce — NET change YoY (S&P Global head-count analytics, ~75-90 largest listed publishers/platform holders)
series.unit: percent YoY change in aggregate headcount

points.source: S&P Global Market Intelligence 'Video game industry sheds jobs to protect margins' (2025) — hiring surged 2020-21; exact % not disclosed, directional +
points.note: Pandemic hiring boom begins; S&P dataset starts 2010 and had NEVER contracted before 2024. Value is a from-memory directional estimate, not a published figure.
Continued boom. Magnitude estimated/directional only.
Revenue outlook soured mid-2022 but headcount still GREW. Magnitude estimated.
PEAK headcount year. Net still slightly positive/flat DESPITE ~10,500 gross layoffs (Satvat). Classic churn year: gross cuts absorbed by continued hiring. Magnitude estimated.

points.source: S&P Global Market Intelligence, 'Video game industry sheds jobs to protect margins' (Feb 2025): 'the video game industry shed 2% of its workforce in 2024 — the first staffing contraction in the data set, which stretches back to 2010'
points.note: FIRST net annual contraction in 15 years. ~15,631 gross layoffs (Satvat) exceeded hiring — net FELL. Basis: aggregate headcount of ~75 largest gaming companies.

series.label: Global core game-dev workforce — LEVEL and NET (Amir Satvat / ASGC dataset)
series.unit: total employees (headcount level) and net job change

points.source: Amir Satvat / ASGC via GamesBeat 'Global gaming's workforce grew 0.6% in 4 years...' (2026)
points.note: Start-of-window level ~750,000 (basis: broad games-industry employment estimate, wider than S&P's listed-company sample). ~8,500 gross layoffs in 2022.

points.source: ASGC/Satvat via GamesBeat (2026); layoff count from GamesBeat 'Gaming layoffs slowed from 15,631...' (2026)
points.note: Level roughly flat. ~10,500 gross layoffs in 2023 but NET barely moved — re-hiring offset cuts. Interpolated within the 4,000-job / 0.6% four-year net gain.

15,631 gross layoffs (Satvat's worst year; >6,000 in a single quarter early 2024) yet global NET still ~flat because ~13,500/yr core-dev hires continued. This is the crux: huge GROSS churn, near-zero NET globally.

points.source: ASGC/Satvat via GamesBeat 'Gaming layoffs slowed from 15,631 in 2024 to 9,053 in 2025' (2026); 'Hiring may finally be matching the layoff rate' (2025)
points.note: 9,053 gross layoffs (down 42% YoY). Hiring reached BREAKEVEN with layoffs on a 6-week trailing basis in Jan 2025; quarterly layoffs below 700 since Q4 2024. 2025 potentially net-positive-to-flat.

points.source: ASGC/Satvat via GamesBeat (2026): 'grew by 4,000 jobs, or about 0.6% in four years'; 2026 layoff forecast ~7,500-11,580
points.note: End-of-window level ~754,000. NET four-year change 2022→2026 = +4,000 jobs (+0.6%), i.e. ~+1,100/yr. ~48,000 roles eliminated vs ~52,500 hired over the window. Nearly FLAT net despite massive gross churn.

series.label: Global games industry — GROSS layoffs per year (context: to contrast against the flat NET above)

points.source: Amir Satvat via GamesBeat 'Gaming layoffs slowed from 15,631 in 2024 to 9,053 in 2025' (2026)
points.note: GROSS, not net. Satvat estimate; independent Game Industry Layoff Tracker also ~8,500.
GROSS. Net headcount still peaked this year — cuts fully absorbed by hiring.

points.source: Amir Satvat via GamesBeat 'Gaming layoffs slowed from 15,631 in 2024 to 9,053 in 2025' (2026); independent tracker 14,600
points.note: GROSS peak. Only year gross cuts exceeded hiring enough to push global NET slightly negative (S&P: -2% among listed firms).

points.source: Amir Satvat via GamesBeat (2026): 'layoffs slowed from 15,631 in 2024 to 9,053 in 2025'; independent tracker 5,300
points.note: GROSS, -42% YoY. Satvat claims 93% forecast accuracy. Sources disagree (Satvat 9,053 vs tracker 5,300) — range given.

points.source: ASGC live tracker forecast via GamesBeat (2026): full-year projection ~11,580 (YTD Jan-6 Apr actuals 4,674); alt Satvat forecast 7,500 'high, high std dev'
points.note: FORECAST only. Cumulative 2022-2026P gross layoffs ~55,408.

series.label: North America core game-dev workforce — NET change (ASGC/Satvat, most structurally negative region)

points.source: Amir Satvat / ASGC via GamesBeat 'Global gaming's workforce grew 0.6% in 4 years, but North America's shrank 11.5%' (2026)
points.note: 4-year NET decline. 19.2% of the regional workforce was laid off (gross) but only partly rehired → genuine STRUCTURAL net loss. Job-seeker:hire ratio 11:1 (127,000 seekers vs 12,000 hires). This is where 'rollback' fails and 'structural' holds.

series.label: Other regions — 4-year gross layoff intensity (share of regional workforce cut, 2022-2026) for net-decline context
series.unit: percent of regional workforce laid off (gross, 4-yr cumulative)

points.note: Europe. Milder than NA; net closer to flat.
Asia least affected — net likely still positive. NA (19.2%) and South America (11%) worst.

series.label: US games workers reporting a layoff — GDC State of the Game Industry survey (self-reported prevalence, NOT net headcount)
series.unit: percent of surveyed developers laid off

points.source: GDC 2025 State of the Game Industry (survey Jan 2025, >3,000 respondents), via gdconf.com & BusinessWire
points.note: 11% laid off in past 12 months; 41% felt layoff impact; 58% concerned about job security. Prevalence metric, not a headcount level.

points.source: GDC 2026 State of the Game Industry (>2,300 respondents), via PC Gamer & BusinessWire (Jan 2026)
points.note: 28% laid off in the PAST TWO YEARS globally, rising to 33% (one-third) for US respondents; 50% say employer did layoffs in past 12mo; 48% of laid-off workers hadn't found a new job. Cumulative 2-yr metric — evidence of churn severity, still not a net-headcount figure.

series.label: Reference headcount LEVELS (not net-change series) — for calibrating the denominators above

points.source: ESA 2024 Economic Impact Report (theesa.com): US industry 'supporting nearly 350,000 U.S. jobs'; direct+indirect ~429,000
points.note: US, DIRECT+INDIRECT SUPPORTED jobs (not direct-only). Direct core employment much smaller — e.g. ~36,000 in studios/dev teams + ~32,000 dev/publisher ops per report. Do not confuse with global dev headcount.

points.source: CESA Game Industry Report 2024 (renamed from White Paper), presented at Tokyo Game Show Sept 2024
points.note: JAPAN, game-industry workforce INCLUDING surrounding industries ~200,000. Avg annual income ¥7.08M. Level, not net-change. CESA report timing = calendar-year reporting.

points.source: Amir Satvat via PocketGamer.biz 'hiring velocity trending upward' (2025): ~243,000 estimated worldwide DIRECT game-dev employees
points.note: GLOBAL DIRECT core game-dev headcount ~243,000 (narrower basis than the ~750k broad-industry figure). ~13,500 annual hires now vs ~25,000 at COVID peak.

data.summary:
The central finding: GROSS layoffs and NET headcount change tell opposite stories, and conflating them is the main error to avoid. Two independent datasets converge. (1) S&P Global Market Intelligence (aggregate headcount of ~75-90 of the largest listed gaming companies, dataset back to 2010) shows the industry GREW every year 2010-2023, PEAKED in 2023, and posted its FIRST-EVER net annual contraction of -2% in 2024. (2) Amir Satvat's ASGC dataset (broader ~750,000-person games-industry base) shows global NET workforce essentially FLAT across the whole downturn: +4,000 jobs / +0.6% over the four years 2022→2026 (~750,000 → ~754,000), even though ~48,000 roles were eliminated (gross) against ~52,500 hires. So despite headline gross layoffs of ~8,500 (2022), ~10,500 (2023), 15,631 (2024, the peak), and 9,053 (2025), the global total barely moved — heavy churn/re-hiring, not a collapse. The ONE year gross cuts overwhelmed hiring enough to move net negative was 2024 (S&P -2%); by Jan 2025 hiring reached breakeven with layoffs on a 6-week trailing basis and quarterly layoffs fell below 700 (from >6,000 in a single quarter in early 2024). The 'rollback vs structural' verdict is REGIONAL, not global: globally it looks like a rollback (net near-flat), but North America is genuinely STRUCTURAL — a 4-year NET decline of -11.5%, 19.2% of the regional workforce cut, and an 11:1 job-seeker-to-hire ratio (127,000 seekers vs 12,000 hires). Europe (-6.7% gross cut share) and Asia (-2%) are far milder. Survey data (GDC State of the Game Industry) measures churn PREVALENCE, not net level: 11% of devs laid off in the year to Jan 2025, rising to 28% laid off over the prior two years by Jan 2026 (33% for US respondents) with 48% of the laid-off still jobless — high individual churn coexisting with a flat aggregate.</summary>
<findings>["S&P Global: video game industry cut ~2% of its workforce in 2024 — the FIRST net staffing contraction in a dataset going back to 2010; headcount had PEAKED in 2023 (basis: aggregate of ~75-90 largest listed gaming firms).","ASGC/Satvat: global games workforce NET change 2022→2026 was only +4,000 jobs (+0.6%), from ~750,000 to ~754,000 — essentially FLAT despite the downturn. ~48,000 roles eliminated vs ~52,500 hired = churn, not collapse.","GROSS layoffs by year (Satvat): 2022 ~8,500; 2023 ~10,500; 2024 15,631 (peak); 2025 9,053 (-42% YoY); 2026F ~7,500-11,580. Only 2024 pushed global NET negative.","The flat-NET-despite-huge-GROSS paradox is sharpest in 2023-2024: 2023 saw ~10,500 gross cuts yet was the PEAK headcount year (fully absorbed by hiring); 2024 saw the worst gross cuts (>6,000 in one quarter) and was the only net-negative year.","Regional split is where 'structural' beats 'rollback': North America NET -11.5% over 4 years (19.2% of regional workforce cut; 11:1 seeker-to-hire ratio, 127k seekers vs 12k hires) versus global +0.6%. Europe -6.7% and Asia -2% (gross cut shares) are milder.","Hiring reached BREAKEVEN with layoffs in Jan 2025 on a 6-week trailing basis; quarterly layoffs have been below 700 since Q4 2024, down from >6,000 in a single quarter in early 2024 — supports a churn/re-hiring recovery narrative over structural collapse (globally).","Survey prevalence (GDC, NOT net level): 11% of devs laid off in year to Jan 2025; 28% over two years by Jan 2026 (33% for US), 50% of employers ran layoffs in past 12 months, 48% of laid-off workers still jobless — high personal churn under a flat aggregate.","Reference LEVELS (different bases, don't blend): global DIRECT core game-dev ~243,000 (Satvat, 2025); US direct+indirect SUPPORTED ~350,000, ~429,000 with indirect (ESA 2024); Japan ~200,000 incl. surrounding industries (CESA 2024)."]</findings>
<caveats>["GROSS vs NET vs LEVEL kept strictly separate per instructions: S&P '-2% in 2024' and Satvat '+0.6% over 4 years' are NET; the 8,500/10,500/15,631/9,053 figures are GROSS layoffs; the 243k/350k/200k/750k figures are LEVELS at a date. They must not be added or compared across categories.","Different denominators: S&P's percentage change covers only ~75-90 large LISTED companies (its -2% is not the same universe as Satvat's ~750,000 broad-industry base). The two agree directionally (flat-to-slightly-down 2024) but are not arithmetically interchangeable.","Year-by-year NET percentages for 2020-2023 in the S&P series are from-memory directional estimates (isEstimate=true) — S&P published the -2% for 2024 and the qualitative peak/boom narrative, but not a full public year-by-year percentage table.","Interpolated LEVEL points (2023, 2024, 2025) in the Satvat series are my distribution of his stated four-year +4,000/+0.6% net figure across years, flagged isEstimate=true; only the endpoints (~750k→~754k) and the annual gross-layoff counts are directly sourced.","Gross-layoff counts diverge between sources: Satvat 2024=15,631 / 2025=9,053 vs the independent Game Industry Layoff Tracker 14,600 / 5,300. These are voluntary/press-aggregated tallies, likely undercounts of small studios and non-Western firms.","GDC survey figures are self-reported prevalence among a self-selected ~2,300-3,000 respondents (English-language, Western-skewed) — they measure how many INDIVIDUALS experienced a layoff, NOT net headcount, and cannot be converted into a workforce level.","JP (CESA) and US (ESA) figures use calendar-year / association methodologies with 'surrounding industries' or 'direct+indirect' multipliers that inflate them well above core-dev headcount; they are context anchors, not part of the net-change trend.","S&P primary page (spglobal.com) and the Variety/ESA PDFs returned 403/402 to automated fetch; their figures here are taken from S&P's own published summary text and reputable secondary reporting (PC Gamer, BusinessWire, GamesBeat, PocketGamer.biz) quoting them directly.","2026 values are forecasts/partial-year (ASGC live tracker), not settled actuals."]</caveats>

data.topic: Geographic reallocation of game-dev jobs 2015-2026: US vs Asia vs Europe workforce share, labor-cost arbitrage (Poland/Eastern Europe/India/Vietnam/LatAm), Canada/Quebec incentives, and the growth of contract/outsourcing/co-dev

series.label: Share of new games job openings — North America (BASIS: ASGC/Satvat open-req dataset, share of global postings)
series.unit: percent of global open game jobs

points.note: Mid-2022 baseline. Satvet: NA was ~40% of global new openings before the contraction. BASIS: open-req share, not headcount.
NA share of new openings fell 40%->25%. Cross-checked by Sept 22 2025 snapshot: NA=3,248 of 14,162 open jobs = 22.9%.

series.label: Open game jobs by continent, Sept 22 2025 snapshot (BASIS: ASGC live open-requisition count)

points.note: ASIA = 4,601 (largest). Asia+Europe+NA = 84% of all openings. Data date Sept 22 2025.
EUROPE = 4,008 open jobs.
NORTH AMERICA = 3,248 open jobs (now 3rd, behind Asia and Europe).

series.label: Global games workforce headcount, ~2022 vs ~2026 (BASIS: ASGC/GICC estimate, all game-industry employees)

points.note: Approx 750k baseline. Over the window ~48,000 roles eliminated (6.4% of workforce) and ~52,500 filled -> net +0.6%.
points.source: Amir Satvat / ASGC via GamesBeat ('grew 0.6% in 4 years' article)

points.note: Net +0.6% (~+4,000) globally over 4 yrs — but reallocation, not stasis: NA down, Asia/EU roughly flat, LatAm down. Basis: whole industry, not developers-only.

series.label: Regional workforce % change ~2022->2026 (BASIS: ASGC net headcount change by region)

points.note: NORTH AMERICA -11.5% (the outlier); within NA an internal ~19.2% churn/loss cited. >50% of ALL international games layoffs occurred in California alone; ~70-75% in North America.
EUROPE -6.7%.
SOUTH AMERICA ~-11% (gave back much of prior boom; Brazil had been +210% in 2017-2022).
ASIA -2% (smallest decline; most resilient region -> its share of global workforce rose relative to NA/EU).

series.label: 2017-2022 hiring-boom growth by country (BASIS: ASGC/LinkedIn headcount growth, pre-contraction)

points.note: BRAZIL +210% — biggest boom-era gainer (LatAm nearshore/co-dev).
POLAND +150% — Eastern-Europe cost-arbitrage + CD Projekt/Techland/People Can Fly ecosystem.
SWEDEN +88%.
AUSTRALIA +59%.
UK/ENGLAND +58%.
CANADA +48% (Quebec/BC tax-credit-driven).

series.label: Top open-job cities Sept 2025 — shows Asia/EE hubs displacing US metros (BASIS: ASGC open-req count by city)

points.note: Shenzhen 946 (#1 city globally).
Shanghai 629.
Montreal 515 (Quebec incentive hub, #3).
Warsaw 239 (Poland).
Bangalore 202 (India offshore hub) — ahead of Vancouver (209 tie) and above Istanbul 181.

series.label: Per-developer annual cost by country 2024-2025 — labor-cost arbitrage (BASIS: market salary surveys, avg game/software dev, ESTIMATE)

points.note: USA ~$114k avg (Qubit Labs/ERI). Reference point. ~$88.9k junior, ~$108k mid.
points.source: Qubit Labs game developer salary guide; ERI SalaryExpert

points.note: POLAND ~$43.2k -> US ~2.6x Poland. (Senior remote-for-West Polish devs command EUR80-115k.)
points.source: Qubit Labs; Polish Gamedev Salary Report 2024

points.note: VIETNAM ~$31.7k (Unreal devs ~$25.2k) -> US ~4.5x. Cheapest major hub.
INDIA ~$15k-$30k (midpoint ~$22.5k) -> US ~3.8x-7.6x. Studios cite 50-70% lower cost than US/EU for equivalent 3D/AAA-support production.
points.source: Qubit Labs; Juego Studio / India gamedev reports

series.label: Game outsourcing / co-dev services market size (BASIS: analyst market reports — sources disagree, range given)

points.note: 2024 market ~$1.9B-$4.2B depending on report scope (DataHorizzon/DHR $4.2B; market.us $1.87B; MRFR $3.07B). Wide disagreement on boundary of 'outsourcing' vs 'co-dev'.
Projected ~$15.5B by 2033 (CAGR ~14%). Driver: ~68-70% of developers now use outsourcing in some form; AAA publishers run parallel co-dev lines across continents.

series.label: Keywords Studios — largest pure outsourcing/co-dev vendor headcount (BASIS: company/consolidated, art+QA+localization+co-dev, Asia-heavy)

points.note: ~12,340 staff Dec 2023 across 70+ facilities in 26 countries; large Manila (Philippines), India, China footprints — the offshored 'support layer' for Western AAA.
points.source: Keywords Studios FY2023 results; Revelio Labs

points.note: ~14,735 staff 2024 (Revelio); FY2024 revenue $879.2m. Taken private Nov 2024 (EQT/CPPIB/Temasek). Acquired US AAA studio Certain Affinity Oct 2024. NOTE: acquisitions inflate headcount — not purely organic.
points.source: Keywords Studios FY2024 results (MarketScreener); Revelio Labs

series.label: Poland games workforce — Eastern-Europe hub level (BASIS: EGDF/PARP 'Game Industry of Poland', all game-industry specialists)

points.note: ~15,000 professionals across ~500 producing/publishing companies (roughly doubled in a decade). 16 international studios each 200+ staff. 456 open jobs (5th globally) as of Sept 2025.
points.source: Trade.gov.pl; The Game Industry of Poland Report 2025 (EGDF/PARP)

points.note: 824 studios employing 14,568 specialists (Report 2025 count). Women 24.5%, foreign workers ~15%.
points.source: The Game Industry of Poland Report 2025 (en.parp.gov.pl PDF)

series.label: Quebec games hub — incentive-driven concentration (BASIS: provincial industry stats + CTMM tax credit)

points.note: 15,000+ game developers, 250+ studios (200+ in Montreal). CTMM tax credit = 37.5% of eligible labour; costs treasury ~$350m/yr (15 firms take 75%). 2024-25 budget trimmed credit (first ~$18k of salary no longer eligible) — first cracks in the incentive model.
points.source: The Logic; Investissement Quebec; NPR Planet Money

series.label: Remote-role share of open games jobs — offshoring vs remote distinction (BASIS: ASGC open-req flag)
series.unit: percent of open roles that are remote

points.note: Mid-2022: 25-30% of openings were remote (RTO not yet enforced).
points.source: Amir Satvat / ASGC via GamesBeat + GameDevReports

points.note: 2025: only 14% remote (1,980 of 14,162); 86% on-site. Key point: Western firms cut cost via OFFSHORING to lower-cost geographies + RTO, NOT via domestic remote. GDC 2024: 40% of AAA devs under mandatory RTO.
points.source: Amir Satvat / GamesBeat; GDC State of the Industry 2024

data.summary: Between 2015 and 2026 game-dev jobs did not vanish globally — they RELOCATED. The single best time-series is Amir Satvat's ASGC dataset. It shows the global workforce essentially flat (~+0.6% over 2022-2026, ~750k employees, ~48k roles cut vs ~52.5k filled) but sharply reallocated by region: North America -11.5%, Europe -6.7%, South America -11%, Asia only -2%. The clearest single metric is that North America's share of NEW job openings collapsed from ~40% (mid-2022) to ~25% (2025); by the Sept-2025 snapshot Asia (4,601 openings) and Europe (4,008) both out-post North America (3,248), and Shenzhen/Shanghai/Montreal/Warsaw/Bangalore now sit among the top job cities. This is heavily a story of (1) labor-cost arbitrage and (2) layoff concentration in expensive US metros: >50% of ALL international games layoffs hit California alone and ~70-75% hit North America. Cost gaps are the engine: a US game dev averages ~$114k vs ~$43k Poland (US ~2.6x), ~$32k Vietnam (~4.5x) and ~$15-30k India (~3.8-7.6x); Indian studios advertise 50-70% cost savings on equivalent 3D/AAA-support work. The 2017-2022 boom already pre-loaded these hubs (Poland +150%, Brazil +210%, Sweden +88%). The offshored 'support layer' is institutionalized through outsourcing/co-dev: the services market is ~$1.9-4.2B (2024), projected ~$15.5B by 2033, with ~68-70% of developers using outsourcing; the largest vendor, Keywords Studios (~14.7k staff, Asia/Manila/India-weighted, FY24 rev $879m), is the archetype. Incentive geographies (Quebec's 37.5% CTMM labour credit, ~15k devs/250 studios; Poland ~15k) anchor the reallocation, though Quebec's 2024-25 budget began clawing the credit back. Remote work is NOT the mechanism — remote openings fell from ~27.5% (2022) to 14% (2025) amid AAA return-to-office mandates; Western firms are shifting cost by moving seats to cheaper countries, not by going remote domestically.
findings: Global games workforce is roughly flat (~+0.6% over 2022-2026, ~750k) but REALLOCATED: North America -11.5%, Europe -6.7%, South America -11%, Asia only -2% (ASGC/Satvat). Asia gained relative share.

North America's share of NEW game-job openings fell from ~40% (mid-2022) to ~25% (2025). By Sept-2025, Asia (4,601 open jobs) and Europe (4,008) both exceed North America (3,248); the three = 84% of all openings.

Layoffs were geographically concentrated in expensive US markets: >50% of ALL international games layoffs occurred in California alone; ~70-75% in North America. Layoffs 8,098 (2022) -> 10,137 (2023) -> 15,631 (2024) -> 9,053 (2025).

Labor-cost arbitrage is the engine: US dev ~$114k/yr vs Poland ~$43k (2.6x), Vietnam ~$32k (4.5x), India ~$15-30k (3.8-7.6x). Indian studios advertise 50-70% savings on equivalent AAA-support/3D production.
The 2017-2022 boom pre-built the cheaper hubs: Brazil +210%, Poland +150%, Sweden +88%, Australia +59%, UK +58%, Canada +48% headcount growth — the reverse of the 2022-2026 NA contraction.

Outsourcing/co-dev is now structural: services market ~$1.9-4.2B in 2024 (sources disagree), projected ~$15.5B by 2033 (~14% CAGR); ~68-70% of developers use outsourcing. Keywords Studios (~14.7k staff, Asia/Manila/India-weighted, FY24 rev $879m) is the archetype offshored support layer for Western AAA.

Incentive geographies anchor the shift: Quebec's CTMM = 37.5% refundable labour credit, ~15,000 devs / 250+ studios (200+ in Montreal), costs treasury ~$350m/yr; Poland ~15,000 specialists across ~500-824 studios. Montreal (515) and Warsaw (239) rank among top open-job cities; Bangalore (202) now beats several US metros.

Offshoring — not domestic remote — is the cost mechanism: remote openings fell from ~27.5% (2022) to 14% (2025); GDC 2024 found 40% of AAA developers under mandatory return-to-office. Western firms move seats to cheaper countries rather than let workers stay remote at home.
caveats: The single richest time-series (regional % change, share-of-openings, boom-era growth) is Amir Satvat's ASGC dataset, built from LinkedIn/open-requisition scraping + community data since mid-2022 — it is the best available but is NOT an official filing; treat all ASGC figures as isEstimate=true. Its 'workforce' basis is whole-game-industry employees, not developers-only, and pre-2022 points are reconstructed.

GROSS layoffs, HEADCOUNT LEVELS, and NET change are kept separate above and must not be blended: e.g. the ~48k roles-eliminated figure (gross-ish) coexists with a +0.6% NET change because ~52.5k were filled. California '>50% of layoffs' is a gross-layoff share, not a headcount share.

Salary/cost figures are market-survey averages (Qubit Labs, ERI, Talent JDI, national salary reports), not payroll disclosures; they vary by seniority, engine (Unreal vs Unity), and whether the role is nearshore-for-West (senior Polish devs reach EUR80-115k, compressing the arbitrage at the top end). Treat all as estimates.

Outsourcing/co-dev market-size numbers come from commercial analyst reports (market.us, DataHorizzon, MRFR, Kings/Verified) whose scope definitions differ wildly (2024 base ranges $1.87B-$4.2B); the boundary between 'outsourcing', 'co-dev' and 'services' is not standardized. Use the range, not a point estimate.

ACQUISITION distortion: Keywords Studios headcount jumps partly reflect roll-ups (e.g. Certain Affinity Oct 2024) and its Nov-2024 take-private (EQT/CPPIB/Temasek) — not purely organic offshoring growth.

Poland headcount sources disagree (~15,000 'over 500 companies' trade.gov figure vs 14,568 across 824 studios in the EGDF/PARP Report 2025) due to different studio-inclusion thresholds and survey years.

Quebec/Poland/India totals are game-industry-wide (all roles incl. QA/art/support), not developers-only; comparability across sources is limited by differing basis. calendarYear used throughout for comparability, but many underlying company FYs (EA, Take-Two, Ubisoft, most JP firms) end 31 Mar — not directly reconciled here because the workforce dataset is calendar-based.

No clean 2015 anchor exists in the primary jobs dataset (Satvat's collection starts mid-2022); the 2015->2022 direction is inferred from the 2017-2022 boom growth rates, which is why 2015 is not given as a hard point.

data.region: Global (headquartered in Redwood City, California, USA; majority of workforce outside the US)
data.summary: Electronic Arts' consolidated headcount grew steadily from ~8,400 (FY2015) to a peak of ~14,600 (FY2026), reported each year as of March 31 in the 10-K "Employees"/"Human Capital" section. Growth was organic plus M&A: the largest jump (FY2021 ~11,000 to FY2022 ~12,900, +1,900) was distorted by acquisitions closing in FY2022 — Glu Mobile (closed 29 Apr 2021, ~800+ staff) and Playdemic (20 Sep 2021); Codemasters closed 18 Feb 2021, just inside FY2021. Despite growth in the reported year-end totals, EA ran repeated GROSS layoff rounds that were offset by hiring/M&A: ~800 cut in Mar 2023 (6%), ~670 in Feb 2024 (5%, plus cancellation of Respawn's Star Wars FPS and closure of Ridgeline Games), a BioWare downsizing in Jan 2025 (dozens laid off, studio shrunk to under 100 from ~200 after Dragon Age: The Veilguard missed sales by ~50%, with a skeleton core team kept on Mass Effect 5), a 300-400-person cut in late Apr 2025 (~100 at Respawn, Titanfall extraction shooter and other incubation projects cancelled), and a reported ~300 across Battlefield studios (DICE, Criterion, Ripple Effect, Motive) in Mar 2026 despite Battlefield 6's record launch. Note the year-end headcount RISING while layoffs occur is not a contradiction: EA hires and acquires faster than it cuts, so gross layoffs must be tracked separately from the net year-end level.

headcount.note: 71% international (rising offshore share). Only +100 net YoY despite the Apr 2025 300-400 cut in-year; Mar 2026 Battlefield-studio cuts (~300) fell just after this year-end. Filed 11 May 2026, amid pending $55B PIF/Silver Lake/Affinity Partners take-private (announced 2025, expected to close ~Q1 2027).

layoffs.scope: Company-wide restructuring, ~6% of workforce; also reduced office/real-estate footprint. CEO Andrew Wilson framed it as focusing on 'biggest opportunities' (owned IP, live services). Restructuring charges $170M-$200M ($55M-$65M severance).
layoffs.source: CNBC / Variety / Game Developer; EA 8-K restructuring disclosure
layoffs.note: 'about 800 jobs, or 6% of workforce.' Restructuring targeted 'substantially complete' by 30 Sep 2023. Fell at FY2023 year-end.

layoffs.scope: Company-wide restructuring, ~5% of workforce. Cancelled Respawn's untitled Star Wars first-person shooter (Mandalorian-themed, led by Peter Hirschmann) and closed Ridgeline Games (after founder/Halo co-creator Marcus Lehto's departure). Strategy: move away from licensed third-party IP toward owned brands, sports, live-service communities. Charges $125M-$165M.
layoffs.source: Game Informer / GamesBeat / GamesRadar; EA 8-K (restructuring plan approved 27 Feb 2024)
layoffs.note: 'approximately 670 employees, roughly 5% of workforce.' Also 'sunsetting' some games and cancelling future licensed IP.

layoffs.scope: BioWare studio-team downsizing after Dragon Age: The Veilguard underperformed (EA: sales ~50% below expectation). Dozens laid off (exact figure not disclosed by EA; press: 'dozens', at least ~30, possibly more), dozens more permanently reassigned to other EA studios. Studio shrank to UNDER 100 from ~200 at Veilguard peak; a core/skeleton team kept on Mass Effect 5. Senior Dragon Age/Mass Effect veterans (Trick & Karin Weekes) departed.
layoffs.source: Bloomberg (Jason Schreier) via Kotaku/PC Gamer/Forbes/GameShub; EA spokesperson statement
layoffs.note: COUNT IS AN ESTIMATE — EA declined to give a number ('not sharing numbers... the right number of people in the right roles for Mass Effect'). Studio-level, not company-wide. Value 30 is a conservative lower-bound proxy for 'dozens'; true figure uncertain (blends layoffs + internal transfers).

layoffs.scope: Company-wide, 'between 300 and 400' roles, concentrated in EA Experiences/Entertainment segment. ~100 at Respawn (publishing, QA, Star Wars Jedi and incubation teams). Cancelled two Respawn early-stage incubation projects, incl. a Titanfall-universe extraction shooter (codenamed R7) and a multiplayer FPS. Apex Legends and Star Wars Jedi continued.
layoffs.source: Bloomberg (Jason Schreier) / GamesRadar / GameSpot / Kotaku / Gematsu; Respawn memo
layoffs.note: COUNT IS MIDPOINT ESTIMATE of the reported '300-400' range; EA did not confirm an exact number. ~100 of these were at Respawn. First reported 29 Apr, confirmed 30 Apr 2025.

layoffs.scope: Layoffs across all four Battlefield Studios (DICE, Criterion, Ripple Effect, Motive) despite Battlefield 6's record launch (~7M copies in first days). EA framed it as a 'realignment' around live-service support for Battlefield 6. All four studios remained operational. EA said the cuts were unrelated to the pending PIF/Silver Lake buyout.
layoffs.source: Shacknews / Slashdot / mxdwn / VPEsports / TechPortal (industry sources; EA did not issue an official headcount)
layoffs.note: COUNT IS PRESS-REPORTED ESTIMATE (~300), NOT EA-CONFIRMED. Reported ~9-10 Mar 2026. Study cross-check advised; EA traditionally does not confirm such figures.

acquisitionDistortions: FY2021 to FY2022 headcount jump (+1,900, ~11,000 to ~12,900) is largely ACQUISITION-DRIVEN, not organic: Glu Mobile closed 29 Apr 2021 (early FY2022, ~800+ staff) and Playdemic closed 20 Sep 2021.

Codemasters (closed 18 Feb 2021, ~700-800 staff across UK studios incl. Slightly Mad) landed inside FY2021, partly explaining the FY2020 (~9,800) to FY2021 (~11,000) +1,200 jump alongside organic growth.

EA's year-end reported headcount RISING across FY2023-FY2026 masks large gross layoff rounds each year — hiring plus M&A (and EA Sports FC/Battlefield ramp) offset cuts, so net level grew while thousands of gross roles were eliminated. Do NOT read rising year-end totals as absence of layoffs.

No mega single-acquisition distortion comparable to Take-Two/Zynga or Microsoft/Activision; EA made no large-cap studio acquisition in this window (its attempted 2022 mergers, e.g. with NBCUniversal/others, did not complete).

extras.expansion: Organic + bolt-on M&A: Respawn (2017), Codemasters (Feb 2021), Glu Mobile (Apr 2021), Playdemic (Sep 2021), Metalhead Software, Ridgeline (opened 2022, closed Feb 2024). Headcount roughly doubled FY2015 (~8,400) to FY2026 (~14,600).
extras.hiringPlans: Heavy ongoing investment in EA Sports FC (post-FIFA rebrand, FY2024+), Battlefield (Battlefield 6 record launch 2025), Apex Legends and Madden NFL live services — franchise ramps drove net hiring that offset layoff rounds. Offshore share of workforce rose from 64% (FY2021) to 71% (FY2026).
extras.salaryTrend: Not disclosed as a company-wide figure in 10-K human-capital sections; EA discloses pay-equity commitments and equity compensation in proxy statements rather than average salary. No reliable public average-salary time series.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed in 10-K. EA references retention/engagement programs but does not publish a numeric annual turnover/attrition rate.

keyFindings: Every FY2015-FY2026 headcount figure is a PRIMARY SEC 10-K disclosure (as-of Mar 31, consolidated group). Basis shifted wording in FY2021 from 'regular, full-time employees' (FY2015-FY2020) to 'people globally' under the newer Human Capital item — treat as broadly comparable but note the definitional change.
Peak reported headcount is ~14,600 (FY2026); trough in window ~8,400 (FY2015). Net growth ~74% over 11 years.
FY2022's +1,900 spike is the single biggest year and is acquisition-distorted (Glu Mobile + Playdemic), NOT organic.

Five distinct layoff waves: Mar 2023 (~800/6%, EA-confirmed), Feb 2024 (~670/5%, EA-confirmed + Star Wars FPS cancel + Ridgeline closure), Jan 2025 BioWare (dozens, studio to <100, EA-unconfirmed), Apr 2025 (300-400 total, ~100 Respawn, Titanfall shooter cancel, EA-unconfirmed exact), Mar 2026 Battlefield studios (~300, press-reported/EA-unconfirmed).
Only the 2023 and 2024 gross layoff counts are officially confirmed via EA 8-K/statements; 2025-2026 figures are trade-press/Bloomberg estimates — flagged isEstimate=true.
BioWare downsizing after Dragon Age: The Veilguard (EA: ~50% below sales expectation) left a skeleton core team on Mass Effect 5; studio fell from ~200 to under 100.

Rising year-end headcount coexists with recurring layoffs — hiring and franchise ramps (EA Sports FC, Battlefield 6, Apex, Madden) offset gross cuts. Never blend gross layoffs with the net year-end level.
Context: EA agreed in 2025 to a ~$55B take-private by Saudi PIF, Silver Lake, and Affinity Partners (expected close ~Q1 2027); EA states the Mar 2026 Battlefield cuts were unrelated to the deal.
