data.region: United States (NASDAQ: TTWO; HQ New York). Global workforce across North America, Europe, Asia-Pacific, Middle East.
data.fiscalYearEnd: March 31 (fiscal year labeled "FY" ends 31 Mar; e.g. FY2023 = year ended 31 Mar 2023).

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees globally
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2015 10-K (Item 1, Employees), filed 22 May 2015
headcount.note: "As of March 31, 2015, we had approximately 2,840 full-time employees, of which approximately 1,360 were employed outside of the United States." R&D staff ~2,120.

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees globally
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2016 10-K, filed 19 May 2016
headcount.note: ~2,933 employees globally; R&D staff 2,179.

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees globally
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2017 10-K, filed 24 May 2017
headcount.note: ~3,707 employees globally; R&D staff 2,818. Rise partly reflects Social Point acquisition (closed Mar 2017).

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees globally
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2018 10-K, filed 17 May 2018
headcount.note: ~4,492 employees globally; R&D staff 3,533.

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees globally
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2019 10-K, filed 14 May 2019
headcount.note: ~4,894 employees globally; R&D staff 3,784.

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees globally
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2020 10-K, filed 22 May 2020
headcount.note: ~5,800 employees globally; R&D staff 4,488. Playdots acquisition closed later (Dec 2020).

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees as of 31 Mar 2021
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2021 10-K (Human Capital), filed 19 May 2021
headcount.note: "With 6,495 full-time employees as of March 31, 2021, of which 3,341 were employed outside of the United States." R&D staff 5,079.

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees as of 31 Mar 2022 (PRE-Zynga)
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2022 10-K (Human Capital), filed 17 May 2022
headcount.note: "With 7,799 full-time employees as of March 31, 2022, of which 3,812 were employed outside of the United States." R&D staff 6,042. NOTE: FY end 31 Mar 2022 is ~7 weeks BEFORE the Zynga close (11 May 2022), so this figure does NOT yet include Zynga's ~3,000-strong workforce.

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees as of 31 Mar 2023 (POST-Zynga)
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2023 10-K (Human Capital), filed 26 May 2023
headcount.note: "With 11,580 full-time employees as of March 31, 2023, of which 7,064 were employed outside of the United States." R&D staff 8,894. The ~7,799 -> 11,580 jump (+3,781, ~+48%) is INORGANIC: Zynga acquisition closed 11 May 2022 (first full FY consolidating Zynga).

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees as of 31 Mar 2024
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2024 10-K (Human Capital), filed 22 May 2024
headcount.note: "With 12,371 full-time employees as of March 31, 2024, of which 7,621 were located outside of the United States." R&D staff 9,639. This snapshot predates most of the April-2024-announced '2024 Plan' cuts.

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees as of 31 Mar 2025
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2025 10-K (Business / Human Capital), filed 20 May 2025
headcount.note: "Our headcount includes 12,928 full-time employees as of March 31, 2025, including 10,096 in development studios." Sales/marketing staff 1,418. Up ~557 YoY DESPITE the 2024 Plan cuts, reflecting Gearbox acquisition (closed 20 Jun 2024, ~1,000+ staff) plus GTA VI ramp; net figure blends organic hires, an acquisition, and layoffs.

headcount.basis: consolidated group, full-time employees as of 31 Mar 2026
headcount.source: Take-Two FY2026 10-K (Business / Human Capital), filed 22 May 2026
headcount.note: "Our headcount includes 12,909 full-time employees as of March 31, 2026, including 9,998 in development studios." Sales/marketing staff 1,456. Essentially flat YoY (-19). Geography: 48.3% North America, 29.8% Europe, 17.7% Asia-Pacific, 4.2% Middle East; 9,998 (77%) focused on product development.

layoffs.scope: Corporate operations and label publishing, notably Private Division; some Zynga integration cuts
layoffs.source: GamesIndustry.biz / Game Developer (Mar 2023); tied to $14.6M FY2023 business reorganization expense in FY2024 10-K
layoffs.note: Take-Two confirmed job cuts in March 2023 but never disclosed an official number; ~200 is a rough press-based estimate (isEstimate=true). Reflected in the $14.6M business-reorganization charge booked in FY2023 (year ended 31 Mar 2023) per the FY2024 10-K reorg note. This is the ONLY workforce action in the FY2023/late-2023 window; there was NO separate '~5%' cut announced in November 2023 (the Nov 8 2023 Q2 FY2024 release contained no workforce-reduction announcement).

layoffs.scope: ~5% of global workforce; company-wide, 'streamlining organizational structure' plus cancellation of several in-development titles (the '2024 Plan')
layoffs.source: Take-Two 8-K/press release 16 Apr 2024; Variety, GamesIndustry.biz, Game Developer, GeekWire
layoffs.note: Company stated '~5% of workforce' but gave NO official absolute headcount. 5% of the ~11,580 (FY2023) base ~=580; media estimates range ~400 (off 8,894 R&D base) to ~600. Midpoint ~550 (isEstimate=true). Part of the '2024 Plan': estimated total charges $160M-$200M ($120-140M title cancellations, $25-35M severance, $15-25M office closures); ~$165M targeted annual savings; expected largely complete by 31 Dec 2024. GeekWire: 70 Seattle-area workers in a WARN filing. Collateral studio closures/cuts: Roll7 and Intercept Games (Kerbal Space Program 2) shut in 2024; Private Division heavily gutted (later divested Nov 2024).

layoffs.scope: Extension of the '2024 Plan': additional title/project cancellations and office closures; NOT primarily a new mass layoff
layoffs.source: Take-Two FY2025 10-K Note 21 Business Reorganization, filed 20 May 2025
layoffs.note: The Feb-2025-era actions were an EXPANSION of the same '2024 Plan' (Take-Two treats it as one program), not a distinct new '5%' round. FY2025 business-reorganization expense = $106.5M: employee-related costs $51.5M, title cancellations $35.1M, loss on divestitures $11.4M (incl. Private Division sale), ROU/office-closure impairment $3.9M, other $4.6M. No official incremental headcount number was published; count set to 0 to avoid double-counting the ~5% already captured under Apr 2024. The 2024 Plan was 'largely completed as of March 31, 2025.'

acquisitionDistortions: ZYNGA (primary distortion): closed 11 May 2022 for ~$12.7B. FY2022 headcount of 7,799 (as of 31 Mar 2022) is PRE-close and excludes Zynga; FY2023 headcount of 11,580 (as of 31 Mar 2023) is the first year-end fully consolidating Zynga. The +3,781 (~+48%) jump from 7,799 to 11,580 is overwhelmingly INORGANIC (Zynga added ~3,000+ employees). Do NOT read this as organic growth.

GEARBOX ENTERTAINMENT: acquired from Embracer, closed 20 Jun 2024 (~$460M). Added ~1,000+ developers; contributes to FY2025 headcount rising to 12,928 (as of 31 Mar 2025) even though the 2024 Plan was cutting ~5% in the same period. FY2025 net change blends organic hires + Gearbox + layoffs.
SOCIAL POINT: closed ~Mar 2017; contributes to the FY2016->FY2017 rise (2,933 -> 3,707).
PLAYDOTS (Dec 2020), Nordeus (Jun 2021), Popcore, Zynga-era studios: smaller inorganic additions across FY2021-FY2022.
PRIVATE DIVISION DIVESTITURE: sold in Nov 2024; the 'loss on divestitures' ($11.4M in FY2025) and associated headcount removal are a NEGATIVE inorganic adjustment offsetting Gearbox in FY2025/FY2026.

extras.expansion: Zynga (mobile/social, closed May 2022) is by far the largest expansion, roughly doubling group headcount and shifting the mix toward mobile. Gearbox (closed Jun 2024) added the Borderlands studio. Product-development staff is ~77-78% of the company (9,998-10,096 of ~12,900) across FY2025-FY2026.
extras.hiringPlans: Post-2024-Plan the company held roughly flat: FY2025 12,928 -> FY2026 12,909 (-19). Headcount is being sustained rather than expanded, dominated by the multi-year Rockstar Games GTA VI development effort (GTA VI is the primary near-term growth driver; launch slipped to 2026). No public numeric hiring target disclosed.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed as a hard number in 10-Ks.
extras.salaryTrend: Not separately disclosed; stock-based compensation is a large and rising cost (e.g. FY2025 SBC ~$398.5M), reflecting heavy equity-based pay for developers/executives.
extras.layoffTotal: No single official cumulative layoff number. The '2024 Plan' (announced Apr 2024, extended into FY2025, completed ~Mar 2025) is the material program: ~5% of workforce plus multiple title cancellations, with combined FY2024+FY2025 business-reorganization expense of $93.3M + $106.5M = ~$199.8M, near the top of the originally guided $160-200M range.

data.summary: Take-Two's full-time headcount (all figures from 10-K filings, fiscal year ending 31 Mar) grew steadily from ~2,840 (FY2015) to 7,799 (FY2022, PRE-Zynga), then jumped INORGANICALLY to 11,580 (FY2023) as the first fiscal year-end fully consolidating the Zynga acquisition (closed 11 May 2022). It rose to 12,371 (FY2024), 12,928 (FY2025, aided by the Gearbox acquisition despite ongoing layoffs), and was essentially flat at 12,909 (FY2026). CRITICAL: the ~6,000-to-11,000+ jump around FY2023 is the Zynga deal, NOT organic growth — FY2022 (7,799) predates the close, FY2023 (11,580) is the first post-close year-end. On layoffs: the material action is the 'Cost Reduction Program' / '2024 Plan' announced 16 Apr 2024 — ~5% of the workforce (~550, no official absolute number given) plus cancellation of several in-development titles, estimated $160-200M in total charges; it was extended in early 2025 and largely completed by 31 Mar 2025 (combined FY2024+FY2025 business-reorganization expense ~$199.8M). The brief's 'November 2023 ~5%' does not correspond to a distinct filing-confirmed event — the Nov 8 2023 Q2 FY2024 release announced no workforce cut; the earlier action was the March 2023 Private Division/corporate cuts (~$14.6M FY2023 reorg charge, no official headcount). Rockstar Games (GTA VI, dev scale is the dominant driver of the ~10,000-strong development-studio headcount, ~77% of the company) and 2K anchor the development organization.
keyFindings: ZYNGA is the key distortion: the +3,781 (~+48%) jump from FY2022 (7,799, as of 31 Mar 2022, PRE-close) to FY2023 (11,580, as of 31 Mar 2023) is inorganic. Zynga closed 11 May 2022 — after the FY2022 year-end but within FY2023.
Development/R&D staff grew from ~2,120 (FY2015) to 8,894 (FY2023) to 10,096 (FY2025) and 9,998 (FY2026); ~77-78% of total headcount is product development.

The material layoff event is the '2024 Plan' announced 16 Apr 2024: ~5% of workforce (no official absolute count; ~550 midpoint, press range 400-600) plus multiple title cancellations. Guided total charges $160-200M; actual FY2024 $93.3M + FY2025 $106.5M ~= $199.8M. Largely complete by 31 Mar 2025.

FY2025 severance/employee-related costs were $51.5M (vs only $3.2M in FY2024), showing the bulk of the human cost landed in FY2025 as the plan was extended in early 2025 — this is the same program, not a separate February-2025 '5%' round.

No filing-confirmed 'November 2023 ~5%' layoff exists; the Nov 8 2023 Q2 FY2024 earnings release announced no workforce cut. The pre-2024-Plan action was March 2023 (Private Division / corporate; ~$14.6M FY2023 reorg charge, no official headcount).

Gearbox acquisition (closed 20 Jun 2024) added ~1,000+ developers, pushing FY2025 headcount UP to 12,928 even while the company was cutting ~5% — the net figure blends organic hires, an acquisition, and layoffs and must not be read as any single trend.

Collateral to the 2024 Plan: studios Roll7 and Intercept Games (Kerbal Space Program 2) closed in 2024; Private Division was gutted and then divested in Nov 2024 (loss on divestiture $11.4M in FY2025).

FY2026 headcount is essentially flat (12,909, -19 YoY), consistent with the company sustaining rather than expanding as it concentrates resources on Rockstar's GTA VI (development scale is the primary driver of the ~10,000 development-studio headcount).

acquisitionDistortions: FY2016 jump ~7,190 -> ~9,400: driven almost entirely by the King Digital Entertainment acquisition ($5.9B, closed 23 Feb 2016, ~2,000 staff), NOT organic hiring.

13 Oct 2023: Microsoft closed its $68.7B acquisition of Activision Blizzard King. This is a HARD DISCONTINUITY — ABK stops filing its own 10-K; headcount folds into 'Microsoft Gaming' (~22,000 total at Jan 2024, which also contains legacy Xbox Game Studios and ZeniMax/Bethesda). No ABK-standalone headcount is published thereafter.

Post-2023 layoff figures (1,900 in Jan 2024; 650 in Sep 2024; the gaming slice of the ~9,000 Jul 2025 and ~4,800 Jul 2026 rounds) are Microsoft Gaming / Microsoft-WIDE numbers, NOT ABK-only. Only King (~200 in Jul 2025) and Blizzard (~100 in Jul 2025) portions are cleanly attributable to ex-ABK studios.
keyFindings: Last independent-company headcount: ~13,000 employees at 31 Dec 2022 (ATVI FY2022 10-K, consolidated, ~three-quarters North America). This is the cleanest final ABK figure.

The ~9,800 the brief cites as the 2015 starting point actually corresponds to the 2018-2021 plateau (FY2018 ~9,760; FY2021 ~9,800). The true 2015 figure was ~7,190; the 2016 step-up to ~9,400 is the King acquisition.

2015->2022 trajectory (consolidated): 7,190 -> 9,400 (King) -> 9,625 -> 9,760 -> 9,080 (2019 restructuring, -800 roles) -> 9,500 -> 9,800 -> 13,000. The 2022 surge (+3,200) was organic development hiring.
13 Oct 2023 Microsoft close = headcount discontinuity; ABK folded into Microsoft Gaming (~22,000 at Jan 2024).
Jan 2024: 1,900 Microsoft Gaming cuts (~8.6%) — cancelled Blizzard's Project Odyssey survival game; Mike Ybarra and Allen Adham left Blizzard.
Sep 2024: +650 Microsoft Gaming cuts (corporate/support), cumulative ~2,550 since close.
Jul 2 2025: ~9,000 Microsoft-wide layoff; King ~200 (10%), Blizzard ~100; Everwild and Perfect Dark cancelled, The Initiative closed.
Blizzard franchises intact through the cuts: World of Warcraft, Diablo (IV, 2023), Overwatch 2, Hearthstone; the cancelled title was the Odyssey survival/MMO game (Jan 2024).

extras.expansion: Pre-acquisition growth 2020-2022 was development-led (Call of Duty franchise, Diablo IV, WoW expansions, King mobile). Post-2023 the trajectory reversed under Microsoft — four gaming layoff rounds in ~18 months plus studio divestitures.
extras.hiringPlans: No ABK-specific plans post-acquisition; subsumed under Microsoft Gaming strategy which since 2024 has been net contraction (cuts + studio sales) amid AI-driven restructuring.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed; senior Blizzard departures (Ybarra, Adham Jan 2024) notable.

data.summary: Activision Blizzard's consolidated headcount ran ~7,190 (FY2015) to ~13,000 (FY2022) per its annual 10-K disclosures. The 2016 step-up to ~9,400 was the King Digital acquisition (closed Feb 2016), after which staffing plateaued around 9,600-9,800 (dipping to ~9,080 in FY2019 after an ~800-role restructuring), then surged +3,200 to ~13,000 in 2022 on organic development hiring. The ~9,800 the task cites as a 2015 start actually maps to the 2018-2021 plateau, not 2015. Microsoft's $68.7B acquisition closed 13 Oct 2023, creating a hard reporting discontinuity: ABK no longer files its own 10-K and its staff fold into 'Microsoft Gaming' (~22,000 at Jan 2024). Subsequent cuts are Microsoft Gaming / company-wide figures, not ABK-only: Jan 25 2024 (1,900, ~8.6% of gaming; cancelled Blizzard's Odyssey survival game; Ybarra and Adham left), Sep 12 2024 (650), Jul 2 2025 (~9,000 Microsoft-wide, with King ~200 and Blizzard ~100), and a near-date Jul 2026 round (~4,800 Microsoft-wide / ~3,200 Xbox, still settling in trade press). Blizzard's core franchises — WoW, Diablo, Overwatch, Hearthstone — remain in service; the marquee casualty was the cancelled Odyssey survival/MMO.

data.region: United States / Global (studios in US, Europe, incl. King in Barcelona/London)
data.fiscalYearEnd: June 30 (Microsoft consolidated FY ends June 30; company does not disclose a separate gaming-segment headcount in its 10-K)
data.summary: Microsoft does not break out a gaming/Xbox headcount in its SEC filings — the 10-K reports only total company FTEs (~221,000 as of June 2023, ~228,000 as of June 2024). Therefore ALL gaming-specific headcount figures below are press/management estimates, NOT filings. The widely-cited framework: Microsoft Gaming reached ~22,000 employees immediately after the Activision Blizzard deal closed on Oct 13, 2023 (ABK added ~13,000 people to Microsoft's pre-existing ~8,000–9,000 Xbox/ZeniMax gaming staff). This is an ACQUISITION-driven jump, not organic growth. Four gross layoff waves followed: Jan 2024 (1,900), Sep 2024 (650), plus the May 2024 studio closures (undisclosed count), and the large Jul 2, 2025 wave (~9,000 company-wide, with gaming taking 'less than half' of that). By mid-2025 the division's baseline was described in reporting as roughly '20K people,' i.e. ~20,000 — a net figure after cumulative cuts, still an estimate. Layoffs (people cut, dated) are kept strictly separate from headcount level (staff at a date) below.

headcount.basis: estimate — Xbox + ZeniMax gaming staff before Activision Blizzard close; Microsoft does not disclose segment headcount
headcount.note: Range ~8,000–9,000 commonly cited for Xbox Game Studios + ZeniMax + platform/services teams pre-acquisition. Not in any filing.
headcount.source: Trade press synthesis (GamesIndustry.biz / Kotaku background)

headcount.basis: estimate — Microsoft Gaming total immediately after Activision Blizzard acquisition closed 13 Oct 2023
headcount.note: ACQUISITION DISTORTION: jump is entirely due to ABK adding ~13,000 employees (Activision, Blizzard, King), NOT organic hiring. The ~22,000 figure is derived from the Jan 2024 layoff math: 1,900 = ~8.6–9% of the division. Microsoft never published this number in a filing.
headcount.source: CNBC / Variety (derived from Phil Spencer memo, 1,900 = ~9% of gaming)

headcount.basis: estimate — approximate Microsoft Gaming headcount reported as 'the gaming division's 20K people' around the Jul 2025 layoffs
headcount.note: NET level after cumulative cuts and attrition; a rounded press estimate, not a filing. Directionally consistent with ~22,000 post-ABK minus ~1,900 (Jan 2024) minus ~650 (Sep 2024) minus prior organic attrition.
headcount.source: Aftermath / Massively Overpowered (Jul 2025 coverage)

headcount.basis: consolidated group — TOTAL Microsoft FTEs (all divisions), from 10-K; NOT gaming-only
headcount.note: Provided only to separate company-wide scale from gaming. Microsoft reported ~221,000 FTEs Jun 2023 and ~228,000 Jun 2024 in its Form 10-K human-capital disclosure. No gaming breakout exists in the filing.
headcount.source: Microsoft FY2024 Form 10-K (human capital); via PBS/GamesRadar summaries

layoffs.scope: Microsoft Gaming (Xbox + Activision Blizzard + ZeniMax) — company-wide impact <1% of Microsoft total
layoffs.note: GROSS cut. ~8.6–9% of the ~22,000-person gaming division. Announced in Phil Spencer memo. Blizzard's unannounced survival game 'Odyssey' cancelled; Blizzard president Mike Ybarra and co-founder/chief design officer Allen Adham departed.
layoffs.source: CNBC / Game Informer / Variety

layoffs.scope: Microsoft Gaming — 'mostly corporate and supporting functions'
layoffs.note: GROSS cut. Third gaming layoff round of 2024. Phil Spencer memo said it did NOT signal cancellation of any additional games/devices. Brought cumulative post-ABK gaming cuts to ~2,550 (1,900 + 650) excluding the undisclosed May 2024 studio-closure headcount.
layoffs.source: GeekWire / Fortune / Bloomberg

layoffs.scope: COMPANY-WIDE (~4% of Microsoft's ~228,000 FTEs) — gaming took 'less than half'; not gaming-only
layoffs.note: GROSS company-wide figure — DO NOT read as gaming-only. Microsoft stated Xbox cuts were 'less than half' of the 9,000 (implies roughly up to ~4,000 gaming, exact gaming number never disclosed = estimate). Within gaming: King (Barcelona) ~200 people / ~10% of studio; Turn 10 (Forza Motorsport) ~50% cut; ZeniMax/Bethesda 'significant overhaul' incl. London marketing + Rockville staff; Raven, High Moon, Sledgehammer, Halo Studios, Undead Labs impacted. The Initiative studio CLOSED. Games cancelled: Perfect Dark (The Initiative), Everwild (Rare), an unannounced ZeniMax Online MMORPG (codename Blackbird). Blizzard ended Warcraft Rumble support; Romero Games lost Microsoft funding.
layoffs.source: Aftermath / Windows Central / Massively Overpowered / Game Developer / PBS

layoffs.scope: Microsoft/Bethesda studio CLOSURES — headcount not disclosed (recorded here for completeness; count=0 means undisclosed, not zero people)
layoffs.note: STUDIO CLOSURES, not a numbered layoff. Closed: Arkane Austin (Redfall), Tango Gameworks (Hi-Fi Rush — later acquired by Krafton to keep the studio/IP alive), Alpha Dog Games (Mighty Doom, sunset 7 Aug 2024). Roundhouse Games/Studios merged into ZeniMax Online Studios (not closed). Microsoft cited 'reprioritization of titles and resources' toward 'high-impact' games. Affected employee count was never published.
layoffs.source: Game Developer / Kotaku / Windows Central / Pure Xbox

acquisitionDistortions: The ~22,000 Microsoft Gaming headcount appears ONLY after the Activision Blizzard acquisition closed 13 Oct 2023 — the jump from ~8,000–9,000 to ~22,000 is entirely the ~13,000 ABK employees (Activision, Blizzard, King), NOT organic Xbox hiring.
ABK deal value: ~$68.7B headline / $75.4B including Activision's net cash. Closed 13 Oct 2023 after ~20 months of regulatory review (FTC, EC, UK CMA).

Because the acquisition folded ABK, ZeniMax (acquired 2021), and legacy Xbox Game Studios into one 'Microsoft Gaming' org, subsequent layoffs (Jan 2024, May 2024, Sep 2024, Jul 2025) are largely post-merger overlap elimination and portfolio reprioritization — not headcount trimming of a stable organic workforce.
The Jul 2, 2025 figure of ~9,000 is COMPANY-WIDE across all Microsoft; conflating it with gaming overstates gaming cuts. Microsoft's own framing: Xbox share was 'less than half.'
keyFindings: No official gaming-segment headcount exists: Microsoft's 10-K discloses only total company FTEs (~221k Jun 2023, ~228k Jun 2024). Every gaming-specific number here is a press/derived estimate.
Best-supported gaming headcount anchors: ~22,000 immediately post-ABK (Oct 2023, acquisition-driven) and ~20,000 by mid-2025 (net, after cuts) — both estimates.

Four distinct reductions: Jan 25 2024 (1,900 gross, ~9% of gaming); May 7 2024 (studio closures, headcount undisclosed); Sep 12 2024 (650 gross, corporate/support); Jul 2 2025 (~9,000 COMPANY-WIDE, gaming 'less than half').
Studios closed: Arkane Austin, Tango Gameworks, Alpha Dog (all May 2024); The Initiative (Jul 2025). Roundhouse merged into ZeniMax Online (May 2024, not closed).
Games cancelled across waves: Blizzard 'Odyssey' (Jan 2024); Perfect Dark, Everwild, ZeniMax Online MMORPG 'Blackbird' (Jul 2025).
Cumulative dated gaming layoffs Jan 2024 + Sep 2024 = ~2,550 (excludes undisclosed May 2024 closures and the un-broken-out gaming portion of the Jul 2025 company-wide 9,000).

extras.expansion: Growth was inorganic — ZeniMax/Bethesda acquired Mar 2021 (~$7.5B, +~2,300 staff) and Activision Blizzard closed 13 Oct 2023 (~$69B, +~13,000 staff), taking Microsoft Gaming to ~22,000. No comparable organic hiring.
extras.hiringPlans: Post-2024 posture is contraction/reprioritization, not expansion — repeated 'sustainable cost structure' / 'high-impact games' framing in Spencer memos. Note: a further large wave hit in July 2026 (~4,800 jobs / reported ~20% of Xbox per GeekWire) — adjacent to but outside the requested through-mid-2025 scope and flagged here only for context.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed; heavy involuntary attrition via four layoff rounds 2024–2025.

data.fiscalYearEnd: December 31 (calendar year; Epic is private, no public 10-K)
data.summary: Epic Games is privately held (Tencent ~40% economic stake; Sony, KIRKBI, Disney minority investors) and files no SEC 10-K, so all headcount figures are self-reported via memos or third-party estimates. The single most reliable anchor is CEO Tim Sweeney's Sept 28, 2023 memo: ~830 layoffs equal to ~16% of the workforce, which implies an Epic-employee basis of ~5,190-5,200 at that date. On top of the 830 layoffs, ~250 people left Epic via divestitures (Bandcamp sold to Songtradr; SuperAwesome ad business spun out as an independent company), for ~1,080 total departures in that event. NOTE: the user's brief figure of '~870 people leaving total' is not supported by primary sources — Epic's own numbers are 830 layoffs PLUS ~250 divestiture departures. The user's '~6,500-7,000 by 2023' figure is higher than Epic's own implied ~5,200 basis; the larger number reflects contractor/all-affiliated headcount from data aggregators (e.g. Revelio Labs shows ~12,800 counting all associated profiles, clearly inflated vs. Epic's own basis). Post-2024: no evidence of net rehiring or a return to growth. On March 24, 2026 Epic announced a second, larger round — ~1,000 layoffs (~20%), leaving ~4,000 employees, alongside >$500M in identified cost savings, citing a Fortnite engagement downturn that began in 2025. The trajectory 2023→2026 is net contraction, not recovery.

headcount.basis: Epic employees (implied): 830 layoffs / 0.16 ≈ 5,190-5,200; the basis Epic's own memo used
headcount.source: Derived from Tim Sweeney memo via Variety/CNBC/PC Gamer (830 = ~16%)
headcount.note: Back-calculated from the 830=16% figure. This is Epic's own 'employees' basis, NOT the contractor-inclusive aggregator counts.

headcount.basis: Epic employees (implied): ~5,190 minus 830 layoffs minus ~250 divestiture departures ≈ 4,110
headcount.source: Derived from Sweeney memo (830 layoffs + ~250 Bandcamp/SuperAwesome)
headcount.note: Estimate. Reflects ~1,080 total departures from the Sept 2023 event.

headcount.basis: Estimate, all-employees (third-party aggregator, methodology undisclosed)
headcount.source: Statista / aggregator estimate
headcount.note: Roughly consistent with the ~4,100 implied post-2023 Epic-employee basis; no evidence of net rehiring in 2024-2025.

headcount.basis: Epic employees (implied): ~1,000 layoffs = ~20%, leaving ~4,000 → base ~5,000
headcount.source: PBS / Engadget / Variety reporting on Sweeney's March 24, 2026 memo
headcount.note: Implies core headcount was roughly flat 2024→2026; no confirmed rehiring surge.

headcount.basis: Epic employees remaining after March 2026 cuts (reported)
headcount.source: PBS News / Engadget (citing Sweeney memo, March 24, 2026)
headcount.note: '~4,000 employees after layoffs' per reporting. Estimate — Epic gives round numbers, not exact filings.

layoffs.scope: Company-wide (~16% of workforce); ~two-thirds in teams outside core development
layoffs.source: Tim Sweeney memo 'Layoffs at Epic', reported by Variety, CNBC, Game Informer, PC Gamer
layoffs.note: Severance: 6 months base pay + 6 months healthcare (US/Canada/Brazil) + accelerated option vesting through end-2024. This is the GROSS layoff count, distinct from the ~250 divestiture departures.

layoffs.scope: Divestiture departures (NOT layoffs): Bandcamp sold to Songtradr; SuperAwesome ad business spun out as independent company
layoffs.source: Sweeney memo; Variety/CNBC ('around 250 people leaving Epic through divestitures')
layoffs.note: Separate from the 830 layoffs. Combined Sept 2023 departures ≈ 1,080. Correcting the brief's '~870 total' — primary sources give 830 + ~250.

layoffs.scope: Company-wide (~20-25% of workforce); cited Fortnite engagement downturn since 2025
layoffs.source: Sweeney memo, reported by PBS, Engadget, Variety, Fox Business, GeekWire
layoffs.note: 'over 1,000' laid off; leaves ~4,000. Paired with >$500M identified cost savings (contracting, marketing, closing open roles). Severance: min 4 months base pay (more by tenure) + 6 months healthcare (US) + option vesting through Jan 2027. GeekWire: 82 affected at Seattle-area office via WARN.

acquisitionDistortions: Bandcamp divestiture (Sept 2023): ~acquired by Epic March 2022, sold to Songtradr Sept 2023 — the departing Bandcamp staff are a DIVESTITURE outflow, not organic attrition or a layoff. Reduces Epic headcount without being a workforce 'cut'.

SuperAwesome divestiture (Sept 2023): SuperAwesome (acquired by Epic 2020) ad business spun out as an independent company under CEO Kate O'Loughlin — another divestiture outflow bundled into the Sept 2023 ~250 figure. Not a layoff.

Historical hiring/acquisition inflation (2020-2022): Epic's rapid headcount growth to ~5,000+ was partly acquisition-driven (Bandcamp, SuperAwesome, Mediatonic/Tonic Games, Harmonix, Cubic Motion, Capturing Reality, ArtStation, Sketchfab, etc.), so pre-2023 headcount jumps were NOT purely organic hiring.

Aggregator over-count: third-party datasets (e.g. Revelio Labs ~12,800) count all associated LinkedIn/contractor profiles and vastly exceed Epic's own 'employee' basis (~5,200 per the 16%=830 math). Do not blend aggregator totals with Epic's self-reported basis.
keyFindings: Epic is private (no SEC 10-K); the only firm anchor is Sweeney's Sept 28, 2023 memo — 830 layoffs = ~16% → implies ~5,190-5,200 Epic employees at that date (Epic's own basis).
Sept 2023 event = 830 LAYOFFS + ~250 DIVESTITURE departures (Bandcamp→Songtradr, SuperAwesome spun out) = ~1,080 total departures. The brief's '~870 leaving total' is not supported; use 830 + ~250.
The brief's '~6,500-7,000 by 2023' exceeds Epic's own implied ~5,200 basis and reflects contractor/all-affiliated aggregator counts — flagged as an over-count.
No evidence of meaningful net rehiring post-2024; estimates hold core headcount roughly flat at ~4,000-5,000 through early 2026.

SECOND major layoff round: March 24, 2026 — ~1,000 cut (~20%), leaving ~4,000, plus >$500M cost savings, citing a Fortnite engagement downturn since 2025. Net trajectory 2023→2026 is contraction, not recovery.
About two-thirds of the 2023 cuts hit non-core-development teams; Epic protected engineering/core lines.

extras.expansion: Rapid 2020-2022 growth to ~5,000+ was heavily acquisition-fueled (Bandcamp, SuperAwesome, Mediatonic/Tonic Games, Harmonix, ArtStation, Sketchfab, Cubic Motion, Capturing Reality). Sweeney's 2023 memo admitted Epic had been 'spending way more money than we earn' funding the Fortnite metaverse/creator ecosystem, a lower-margin model than the original Battle Royale.
extras.hiringPlans: No public rehiring/expansion plans after the cuts. March 2026 memo explicitly includes 'closing some open roles' among >$500M cost savings — signaling continued hiring freeze/contraction, not growth.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed (private company). Involuntary reductions: ~16% (Sept 2023) and ~20% (Mar 2026) of workforce in the two named rounds.
extras.avgTenureYears: Not disclosed; severance in both rounds was tenure-scaled (2026: minimum 4 months base pay, more with tenure).

data.summary: Riot Games is a privately held US studio, wholly owned by China's Tencent (Tencent took a majority stake in 2011 and acquired 100% in December 2015). As a private subsidiary it files no SEC reports, and parent Tencent's annual/interim reports do not break out Riot's headcount, so ALL headcount levels below are estimates derived from Riot's own public statements and press coverage rather than filings. The only hard, dated numbers are gross layoff counts announced by Riot itself. Riot's leadership (CEO Dylan Jadeja) stated the company "doubled the size of Riot in just a few years" since 2019 — from roughly 2,000 employees (2019) to a peak of roughly 4,500-4,800 by the start of 2024. On 22 January 2024 Riot cut 530 roles = ~11% of workforce (this 530/11% ratio mathematically implies a ~4,800 total FTE base at that date), shutting its external-publishing arm Riot Forge (wound down after Bandle Tale, Feb 2024), gutting the Legends of Runeterra team (refocused onto Path of Champions PvE), and pulling back on incubation/"big bets." Smaller cuts followed: ~32 in Oct 2024 (mostly LoL devs + publishing), and in Feb 2026 ~80 on the 2XKO fighting-game team (about half that team) plus ~12 in the publishing division. CRITICAL DATA CAVEAT: third-party headcount trackers report far higher numbers (e.g. 7,436 for 2023; Revelio ~6,740 in Dec 2025; Crustdata ~8,158) because they count contractors, agency/QA/esports/partner staff and stale LinkedIn profiles. Those figures should NOT be blended with Riot's own ~4,500-4,800 core-FTE framing against which the 11% cut was calculated.

headcount.basis: estimate — company-wide FTE, per Riot CEO statement that Riot 'doubled in size' since 2019 from ~2,000
headcount.source: Riot Games official announcement 'An Important Update about Riot's Future' (22 Jan 2024); Game Developer
headcount.note: Approximate baseline; Jadeja framed 2019 as ~2,000 before doubling. Not a filing.

headcount.basis: estimate — company-wide FTE; back-calculated from 530 cuts = 11%, and consistent with Riot's 'doubled since 2019' and '4,500+ Rioters' impact-report language
headcount.source: Riot Games 2022 Impact Report ('4,500+ Rioters'); Riot 22 Jan 2024 announcement (530 = 11%)
headcount.note: Range ~4,500-4,800. 530/0.11 = 4,818 implied FTE base. Riot's own '4,500+' branding is a rounded floor. NOT the ~7,436 third-party figure, which includes contractors/esports/partner staff.

headcount.basis: estimate — company-wide FTE remaining immediately after the Jan 2024 layoff (~4,800 minus 530)
headcount.source: Derived: Riot 22 Jan 2024 announcement
headcount.note: Simple net of gross layoff from estimated peak; does not account for backfills/attrition/new hires through 2024.

layoffs.scope: Global, ~11% of total workforce. Primarily teams outside core live-game development. Closed Riot Forge (external-studio publishing, wound down after Bandle Tale released Feb 2024); Legends of Runeterra team drastically reduced and refocused on Path of Champions PvE; pullback on incubation/'big bets'. Refocus onto League of Legends, VALORANT, Teamfight Tactics.
layoffs.source: Riot Games official ('An Important Update about Riot's Future', CEO Dylan Jadeja) + TechCrunch, PC Gamer, Game Developer
layoffs.note: HARD dated figure from Riot itself. Severance: 6+ months pay plus a cash bonus equal to 2023 performance bonus.

layoffs.scope: League of Legends and publishing — reported ~27 active LoL developers + ~5 from publishing division
layoffs.source: Game Developer; press coverage
layoffs.note: Smaller, targeted cut; exact count reported ~32.

layoffs.scope: 2XKO fighting-game team — ~80 roles, reported as roughly half the global 2XKO dev team, after the game failed to attract a wide audience
layoffs.source: Game Developer; TechPowerUp; press coverage (Feb 2026)
layoffs.note: Count is an approximate press figure (~80). Separate from the publishing cut same month.

layoffs.scope: Publishing division — ~12 roles across three internal publishing teams
layoffs.source: Game Developer ('Riot Games confirms layoffs within publishing division'); TechPowerUp
layoffs.note: Approximate press figure (~12).

acquisitionDistortions: Ownership, not organic events, drives Riot's corporate structure: Tencent took a majority stake in 2011 and acquired 100% of Riot in December 2015. Riot's headcount is therefore consolidated inside Tencent but NOT separately disclosed in Tencent's filings — there is no organic-vs-acquired split to flag for Riot itself.

No headcount step-changes from M&A: Riot's growth 2019-2023 (~2,000 to ~4,800) was ORGANIC hiring to become a 'multi-game, multi-experience' company, not acquisition-driven. The subsequent declines are layoffs/attrition, not divestitures.

Third-party tracker inflation is the main distortion to guard against: Revelio Labs (~6,740, Dec 2025), Crustdata (~8,158), and '7,436 for 2023' figures count contractors, agency/QA/esports/partner personnel and stale LinkedIn profiles. These are 2-4x higher than the ~4,500-4,800 core-FTE base Riot used to compute its own 11% layoff and must NOT be blended with it.

extras.expansion: Roughly doubled 2019->2023 (~2,000 -> ~4,500-4,800 FTE) to build a multi-game portfolio (VALORANT 2020, TFT, Wild Rift, Legends of Runeterra, esports, Riot Forge publishing, an MMO, fighting game 2XKO). Post-2024 strategy is contraction/refocus on core live games (League of Legends, VALORANT, TFT).
extras.hiringPlans: Post-Jan-2024 messaging emphasized sustainability and refocus, not net growth. Riot later stated the League of Legends team would become 'even larger' in future despite cuts elsewhere (PC Gamer). No public net-hiring target.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed (private company).

keyFindings: Riot is 100% Tencent-owned (since Dec 2015) and private — NO SEC filings; Tencent does not break out Riot headcount. Every headcount LEVEL here is an estimate; only the dated gross layoff counts are hard numbers.
Peak workforce ~4,500-4,800 FTE at start of 2024 (Riot's own '4,500+ Rioters' branding; '530 = 11%' implies ~4,818). This matches the user's ~4,500-5,000 peak estimate.

Jan 2024 layoff = 530 people = ~11%, confirmed directly by Riot (CEO Dylan Jadeja, 22 Jan 2024). Closed Riot Forge; gutted Legends of Runeterra team (refocus to Path of Champions); publishing/incubation pullback. This is the load-bearing hard number.
Growth was organic (2,000 in 2019 -> ~4,800), not acquisition-driven; no M&A distortions for Riot itself.
Follow-on cuts: ~32 (Oct 2024, mostly LoL devs); ~80 on 2XKO team + ~12 in publishing (Feb 2026). Cumulatively 600+ roles cut across 2024-2026.

DO NOT blend third-party tracker headcounts (Revelio ~6,740; Crustdata ~8,158; '7,436' for 2023) with Riot's ~4,500-4,800 FTE base — trackers include contractors/esports/partner staff and inflate 2-4x.

PC Gamer — 'Riot lays off 530 employees, will close Riot Forge': https://www.pcgamer.com/riot-games-layoffs-2024/

Game Developer — 'Riot Games confirms layoffs within publishing division' (2026): https://www.gamedeveloper.com/production/riot-games-confirms-layoffs-within-publishing-division

data.summary: Roblox is a platform/technology company, NOT a game studio: it builds the engine, app and creator tools, while the games ("experiences") are made by tens of millions of third-party creators who are NOT Roblox employees. Full-time employee headcount is therefore small relative to the ecosystem and grew from ~555-830 at IPO time (2019-2020, per S-1) to 3,065 at Dec 31, 2025 (per 10-K). Growth was steep through FY2022 (roughly +525 in 2022), then nearly flat in 2023-2024 (2,457 -> 2,474) as the company deliberately slowed hiring and cut its recruiting team, before re-accelerating +24% in 2025 (to 3,065). Consistently ~75% of staff are in product & engineering functions. All headcount figures are consolidated group full-time employees, as-of Dec 31 of each year, taken directly from Roblox's SEC 10-K "Human Capital" sections (FY2021-FY2025) and the November 2020 S-1 (for 2019-2020). Note: Roblox also uses "thousands of trust & safety agents" (2020 S-1: over 1,700) who are contractors/vendor staff, NOT counted in the full-time employee figure.

headcount.basis: consolidated group / full-time employees (as-of Sept 30, 2019)
headcount.note: Not stated directly. Derived from S-1: 'over 830 full-time employees [at Sept 30, 2020], which represented an increase of over 275 full-time employees from September 30, 2019' => ~555 at Sept 30, 2019. Approximate. Roblox had no fiscal-2019 10-K (direct listing was March 2021).
headcount.source: Roblox S-1 (filed Nov 19, 2020), 'Our People' / risk-factor sections

headcount.basis: consolidated group / full-time employees (as-of Sept 30, 2020)
headcount.note: S-1 states 'over 830 full-time employees' (a floor, not exact) of which ~80% engineering & product. No fiscal-2020 10-K exists; company IPO'd via direct listing March 10, 2021. Separately had 'over 1,700 trust & safety agents' (contractors, not employees).
headcount.source: Roblox S-1 (filed Nov 19, 2020), 'Our People'

headcount.basis: consolidated group / full-time employees (as-of Dec 31, 2021)
headcount.note: First 10-K. Stated as 'roughly 1,600 full-time employees' (company's own rounding, not an exact count). 1,190 in product & engineering (~75%); 124 employees outside the U.S.
headcount.source: Roblox FY2021 Form 10-K, 'Human Capital'

headcount.basis: consolidated group / full-time employees (as-of Dec 31, 2022)
headcount.note: Exact figure from 10-K: '2,128 full-time employees.' Over 1,600 in product & engineering (75%); over 100 outside the U.S. Largest single-year jump (~+525 vs FY2021).
headcount.source: Roblox FY2022 Form 10-K, 'Human Capital'

headcount.basis: consolidated group / full-time employees (as-of Dec 31, 2023)
headcount.note: Exact figure from 10-K: '2,457 full-time employees.' Over 1,800 in product & engineering (~75%); over 100 outside U.S. Net +329 vs FY2022 despite a Sept 2023 talent-acquisition-team cut, i.e. still net hiring.
headcount.source: Roblox FY2023 Form 10-K, 'Human Capital'

headcount.basis: consolidated group / full-time employees (as-of Dec 31, 2024)
headcount.note: Exact figure from 10-K: '2,474 full-time employees.' Over 1,800 in product & engineering (~77%); over 100 outside U.S. Essentially flat vs FY2023 (net +17) — the hiring-slowdown year. Company completed its return-to-office (San Mateo by July 2024) in 2024.
headcount.source: Roblox FY2024 Form 10-K, 'Human Capital'

headcount.basis: consolidated group / full-time employees (as-of Dec 31, 2025)
headcount.note: Exact figure from 10-K filed Feb 11, 2026: '3,065 full-time employees.' Over 2,300 in product & engineering (~75%); over 200 outside U.S. Hiring re-accelerated: net +591 vs FY2024 (~+24%).
headcount.source: Roblox FY2025 Form 10-K, 'Human Capital'

layoffs.scope: Talent Acquisition (recruiting) team only — a targeted role cut, not a company-wide layoff
layoffs.note: Roblox confirmed to press it cut ~30 people from its talent-acquisition org on Monday Sept 25, 2023, citing 'adjusted hiring needs' after a reduction in hiring targets to bring cash-comp growth in line with bookings growth by end of Q1 2024. This is a GROSS cut; total headcount still rose net in 2023 (2,128 -> 2,457) and 2024, so it did not reduce the year-end level. Roblox has not disclosed any company-wide RIF.
layoffs.source: TechCrunch / Game Developer / GamesIndustry (Sept 2023)

acquisitionDistortions: No material acquisition-driven headcount distortions. Roblox's employee growth (2019-2025) is essentially organic hiring; the company has not made a large workforce-adding acquisition comparable to Take-Two/Zynga or Microsoft/Activision. Its acquisitions (e.g., Guilded 2021, Speechly 2023, Bytedance/Hamster/others) were small and did not visibly move the reported full-time count.

The far larger distortion for a naive reader is the platform-vs-studio confusion: Roblox reports only ~2,100-3,100 employees, yet its ecosystem involves millions of third-party creators. Those creators are independent participants in the DevEx/creator economy, NOT employees, and must never be added to headcount.

extras.hiringPlans: Deliberate slowdown 2023-2024 (net +329 then +17), described by management as aligning cash-compensation growth with bookings growth; re-accelerated in 2025 (net +591, ~+24%, to 3,065). Roblox is heavily engineering-driven; expects continued investment in product/engineering and AI.
extras.salaryTrend: Stock-based compensation is very large relative to headcount (a key expense line); company targeted bringing cash-comp growth in line with bookings growth by end of Q1 2024. Roblox states it maintains competitive Silicon Valley engineering pay.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed in 10-K.
extras.expansion: Consistently ~75% of full-time employees in product & engineering across all years (75% FY2021-2023 & FY2025; ~77% FY2024). Majority based at San Mateo, CA HQ; international employees grew from 124 (2021) to over 200 (2025). Completed return-to-office (remote employees asked to relocate to San Mateo by July 2024, announced Oct 2023).

keyFindings: Full-time employees grew from ~555 (Sept 2019, S-1-derived) to 3,065 at Dec 31, 2025 (10-K) — roughly a 5.5x increase, essentially all organic.
Exact year-end 10-K figures: FY2022 2,128 | FY2023 2,457 | FY2024 2,474 | FY2025 3,065. FY2021 was self-rounded to 'roughly 1,600.'
2023-2024 was a deliberate hiring plateau (2,457 -> 2,474, net +17), NOT a headcount decline; 2025 re-accelerated ~+24%.
The only disclosed role cut was ~30 people in the talent-acquisition team on Sept 25, 2023 — a targeted recruiting reduction, not a company-wide layoff, and it did not lower the year-end total.
The 'October 2023' event often mis-cited as a layoff was actually a return-to-office mandate (remote staff asked to move to San Mateo by July 2024), not job cuts.

Platform, not studio: ~75% of employees are product/engineering; the game-making 'developers' are millions of independent third-party creators (over 24,500 in the DevEx cash-out program in 2024), who are NOT employees.
Creator economy scale (for contrast, not headcount): Roblox paid creators roughly $930 million in 2024, spread across ~20,000-24,500 DevEx participants — an ecosystem, not payroll.
Trust & safety 'agents' (over 1,700 per the 2020 S-1, 'thousands' in later 10-Ks) are contractors/vendor staff and are explicitly excluded from the full-time employee count.
sources: Roblox FY2025 Form 10-K (filed 2026-02-11), Human Capital: 3,065 full-time employees at Dec 31, 2025 — https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1315098/000131509826000024/rblx-20251231.htm
Roblox FY2024 Form 10-K (filed 2025-02-18), Human Capital: 2,474 full-time employees at Dec 31, 2024 — https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1315098/000131509825000033/rblx-20241231.htm
Roblox FY2023 Form 10-K (filed 2024-02-21), Human Capital: 2,457 full-time employees at Dec 31, 2023 — https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1315098/000131509824000026/rblx-20231231.htm
Roblox FY2022 Form 10-K (filed 2023-02-28), Human Capital: 2,128 full-time employees at Dec 31, 2022 — https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1315098/000131509823000035/rblx-20221231.htm
Roblox FY2021 Form 10-K (filed 2022-02-25), Human Capital: roughly 1,600 full-time employees at Dec 31, 2021 — https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1315098/000131509822000058/rblx-20211231.htm

Roblox S-1 (filed 2020-11-19), 'Our People': over 830 full-time employees at Sept 30, 2020, up over 275 from Sept 30, 2019 — https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1315098/000119312520298230/d87104ds1.htm
TechCrunch, 'Roblox cuts 30 on talent acquisition team as hiring slows' (Sept 21, 2023) — https://techcrunch.com/2023/09/21/roblox-cuts-30-on-talent-acquisition-team-as-hiring-slows/

Game Developer, 'Roblox cutting 30 members of talent acquisition team' (Sept 2023) — https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/roblox-cutting-30-members-of-talent-acquisition-team-due-to-adjusted-hiring-needs-
Tubefilter, 'Roblox paid creators almost $1 billion in 2024' (Feb 2025) — creator-economy context — https://www.tubefilter.com/2025/02/07/robloxs-latest-payout-to-creators-280-million/

data.summary: Ubisoft's global consolidated headcount roughly doubled over the 2010s (from ~9,000 team members in FY2014-15 to a peak of 20,665 at 31 March 2022), then entered a sustained, multi-year decline. By 31 March 2025 the group reported 17,782 team members (official FY24-25 figure), and at 31 March 2026 it stood at 16,590 (official FY25-26 figure) — down ~4,075 (~20%) from the March 2022 peak. The contraction has been driven mainly by hiring discipline and voluntary attrition, layered with targeted restructurings and outright studio closures (San Francisco, Osaka, Sydney ramp-down, Leamington, Reflections/Düsseldorf/Stockholm cuts in FY24-25; Winnipeg, Belgrade, Halifax, further Stockholm and Barcelona restructuring in 2025-26). Ubisoft's official year-end counts (17,782 / 16,590) are HIGHER than third-party aggregators like stockanalysis.com (16,625 / 16,590) because the aggregator uses a narrower basis; the official filing figures are used here as primary. A major 2025 structural event is the Tencent-backed carve-out of ~2,300 developers into a new subsidiary, Vantage Studios (Assassin's Creed, Far Cry, Rainbow Six), which remains consolidated within Ubisoft (Ubisoft ~75%, Tencent ~25%) and so does not by itself reduce group headcount, but reorganizes it.

headcount.basis: consolidated group / team members worldwide — estimate
headcount.note: Kotaku (Mar 2014) reported Ubisoft had 'over 9,000' employees; company 'Facts & Figures' Feb 2016 cited ~10,000 worldwide. No clean official 31-Mar-2015 total located; ~9,000-9,900 estimate.
headcount.source: Kotaku / Ubisoft Facts & Figures Feb 2016

headcount.basis: consolidated group / team members worldwide — estimate
headcount.note: Ubisoft 'Facts & Figures' Feb 2016 stated approximately 10,000 employees worldwide. Round figure, treat as ~10,000.
headcount.source: Ubisoft Facts & Figures (Feb 2016)

headcount.basis: developers worldwide — estimate
headcount.note: Bloomberg/Wikipedia: 'nearly 16,000 developers' by mid-2019. Refers to development staff; total group team members were of similar order. Exact 31-Mar-2019 official total not machine-extractable from the compressed URD PDF.
headcount.source: Wikipedia (citing Bloomberg Businessweek)

headcount.basis: consolidated group — estimate (derived)
headcount.note: Derived: Statista states 2024 (17.78k) was down ~2.54k vs 2020, implying ~20,320 at the 2020 reference date. Approximate; official FY19-20 year-end total not separately confirmed.
headcount.source: Statista (derived)

headcount.basis: consolidated group (31 March 2022) — PEAK
headcount.note: All-time peak year-end headcount. Wikipedia cites 20,279 for 2022; aggregators (stockanalysis, tradingeconomics) cite 20,665 (20.66K) at 31 Mar 2022. Peak of the growth cycle before the multi-year decline.
headcount.source: stockanalysis.com / TradingEconomics (Ubisoft filings)

headcount.basis: consolidated group (31 March 2023)
headcount.note: stockanalysis.com: 20,133 at 31 Mar 2023 (-532 YoY). Wikipedia cites 20,279 for 2023. Interim: 19,410 team members at 30 Sep 2023 (Ubisoft-confirmed via Daniel Ahmad/ZhugeEX).
headcount.source: stockanalysis.com; Daniel Ahmad (interim Sep-2023)

headcount.basis: consolidated group (31 March 2024)
headcount.note: ~19,011 at 31 Mar 2024 (stockanalysis, -1,122/-5.6% YoY); Statista/other trackers cite ~18,719 for CY2024. Ubisoft FY24-25 PR describes prior year as ~19,000. Interim: 18,666 at 30 Sep 2024.
headcount.source: stockanalysis.com; Ubisoft FY24-25 PR

headcount.basis: consolidated group / total headcount (31 March 2025) — OFFICIAL
headcount.note: Official Ubisoft FY2024-25 earnings (14 May 2025): total headcount 17,782 at end March 2025, down ~1,230 YoY and ~3,000 since Sept 2022. NOTE: aggregator stockanalysis.com shows 16,625 for FY2025 on a narrower basis — official 17,782 used here. Interim: 17,097 at 30 Sep 2025.
headcount.source: Ubisoft FY2024-25 Earnings (official)

headcount.basis: consolidated group / total headcount (31 March 2026) — OFFICIAL
headcount.note: Official Ubisoft FY2025-26 earnings (20 May 2026): total headcount 16,590 at end March 2026, down ~1,200 YoY. Attrition near record lows; 155 former senior talents returned. ~20% below the March 2022 peak. Occurs alongside the Vantage Studios carve-out (still consolidated).
headcount.source: Ubisoft FY2025-26 Earnings (official); Daniel Ahmad

layoffs.scope: Studio closures: Ubisoft San Francisco + Ubisoft Osaka closed; Ubisoft Sydney ramped down. Also XDefiant shut down.
layoffs.note: ~277-300 roles affected (reported 'close to 300'). Part of cost-reduction/prioritization drive.
layoffs.source: Game Developer / PC Gamer

layoffs.scope: Ubisoft Leamington closed; targeted layoffs at Ubisoft Reflections, Düsseldorf, Stockholm.
layoffs.note: Company confirmed permanent closure of Leamington plus restructurings at Reflections/Düsseldorf/Stockholm; 185 jobs.
layoffs.source: GamesIndustry.biz / PC Gamer

layoffs.scope: Ubisoft Winnipeg closed (~65) and Belgrade closed (~100); further layoffs; Barcelona restructured toward Rainbow Six only.
layoffs.note: June 2026 wave; ~380 total jobs cited across Winnipeg/Belgrade/Barcelona and related cuts. Range overlaps with the Jan-2026 wave in some reports.
layoffs.source: Game Developer / This Week in Video Games / Game Informer

layoffs.scope: Ubisoft Halifax closed (also Stockholm office shuttered).
layoffs.note: Halifax ~71 jobs; closure came weeks after Halifax staff voted to unionize (Ubisoft said unrelated). Stockholm office also closed Jan 2026.
layoffs.source: Game Developer / Nintendo Life

acquisitionDistortions: No large ACQUISITION-driven headcount JUMP in the period — Ubisoft's rise to the 2022 peak was largely ORGANIC hiring/studio expansion, unlike Take-Two (Zynga May 2022) or Microsoft (Activision Oct 2023).

MAJOR STRUCTURAL DISTORTION (carve-out, not acquisition): Oct 2025 creation of Vantage Studios, a Tencent-backed subsidiary housing Assassin's Creed, Far Cry and Rainbow Six with ~2,300 developers (Montreal, Quebec, Sherbrooke, Saguenay, Barcelona, Sofia). Tencent invested €1.16-1.2bn (~$1.25bn) for ~25%; Ubisoft retains ~75%. Because Vantage stays CONSOLIDATED within Ubisoft, the ~2,300 staff remain in group headcount — the carve-out reorganizes, and does not by itself cut, the reported total. Watch for future deconsolidation risk if Tencent's stake or governance changes.

Interim vs year-end basis mismatch: widely-cited figures 20,279/19,410 (Sep 2023), 18,666 (Sep 2024), 17,097 (Sep 2025) are 30-September INTERIM counts, NOT 31-March fiscal year-end totals — do not blend with the year-end series (20,665 / 19,011 / 17,782 / 16,590).

Aggregator basis conflict: stockanalysis.com reports 16,625 (FY2025) vs Ubisoft's official 17,782 — a ~1,150 gap from differing consolidation scope. Official filing figures are treated as primary here.
keyFindings: Peak-to-trough: from 20,665 at 31 Mar 2022 to 16,590 at 31 Mar 2026 — a net reduction of ~4,075 (~20%) over four fiscal years.

The shrink is primarily ATTRITION- and hiring-discipline-led (Ubisoft repeatedly cites voluntary attrition near record lows plus targeted restructurings), NOT a single mass layoff. Confirmed dated GROSS cuts total only ~750-850 across FY24-25/FY25-26 studio closures — far less than the ~4,000 net headcount drop, meaning most of the decline is non-replacement of leavers.

Distinct GROSS-layoff events (keep separate from net headcount): Dec 2024 (~300, San Francisco/Osaka/Sydney), Jan 2025 (185, Leamington/Reflections/Düsseldorf/Stockholm), Jan 2026 (~71, Halifax), June 2026 (~300-380, Winnipeg/Belgrade/Barcelona).
Oct 2025 Vantage Studios carve-out (~2,300 devs, Tencent ~25% / €1.2bn) is the defining structural event — a consolidated subsidiary, so it reorganizes rather than reduces group headcount.

Data-quality caveat: Ubisoft historically reports year-end team-member totals in Universal Registration Documents (URDs) whose PDFs are not machine-extractable here; FY2015-FY2021 exact year-end totals are therefore estimates/derived, while FY2024-25 (17,782) and FY2025-26 (16,590) are official press-release figures.

extras.avgTenureYears: n/a — not disclosed in reviewed sources
extras.expansion: Growth phase ~2014-2022 was organic (new/expanded studios across Montreal, Quebec, Sofia, Barcelona, Bordeaux, Berlin, Mumbai, etc.). Post-2022 shift is contraction + reorganization; Oct 2025 Vantage Studios (Tencent-backed) concentrates the flagship AC/Far Cry/Rainbow Six franchises (~2,300 devs) into an autonomous 'creative house' model Ubisoft plans to extend to other studios/franchises.
extras.hiringPlans: Net negative through FY25-26: continued recruitment discipline and portfolio rationalization. Ubisoft emphasizes retaining/rehiring senior talent (155 former top talents returned in FY25-26) rather than net expansion.
extras.salaryTrend: n/a — not quantified in reviewed sources
extras.turnoverRate: Voluntary attrition described by Ubisoft as at/near record-low levels in FY24-25 and FY25-26, especially for senior profiles — cited as a key mechanism enabling headcount reduction without mass layoffs.

data.region: Sweden (STO: EMBRAC B); studios/operations global (Europe, North America, Asia)
data.summary: Embracer is the definitive games-industry balloon-and-collapse case. From FY2019/20 the company used ~USD 8bn of debt-funded M&A (Saber, Gearbox, Asmodee, Dark Horse, Crystal Dynamics/Eidos, ~100+ bolt-ons) to inflate consolidated total headcount from 3,109 (Mar 2020) to a peak of 16,601 (Mar 2023) — the ~17,000 figure often cited. The trigger for collapse: a verbally-agreed ~USD 2bn strategic investment (later confirmed to be Savvy Games Group / Saudi PIF) walked away in May 2023, leaving Embracer over-leveraged. A restructuring program (announced Jun 2023, completed 31 Mar 2024) cut headcount by 4,532 in one fiscal year to 12,069 (Mar 2024), cancelled ~80 game projects and closed/divested 40+ studios (Volition, Free Radical Design, Campfire Cabal, Pieces Interactive, Piranha Bytes and others). NOTE: of that 4,532 reduction, ~2,950 was the DIVESTMENT of Saber Interactive (a sale, not layoffs); ~3,727 of the departures were game developers. After restructuring, Embracer executed a three-way split: Asmodee spun off and listed Feb 2025 (which mechanically removed thousands of board-game staff from the consolidated count, driving the drop to 7,180 at Mar 2025), and Coffee Stain Group spun off/listed Dec 2025-Jan 2026 (remaining parent renamed Fellowship Entertainment), leaving 6,090 employees at Mar 2026. So the post-peak decline is a BLEND of genuine layoffs/closures (FY23-FY24) and de-consolidation via spin-offs (FY25-FY26) — these must not be read as pure organic cuts.

headcount.basis: consolidated group / total headcount (incl. contracted staff), 31 Mar 2020
headcount.source: Embracer Annual Report 2020/21 'year in brief' (states growth 'from 3,109 to 6,325')
headcount.note: Base year before the acquisition balloon. Loose press phrasings say '4,000-5,000 incl. contracted'; 3,109 is Embracer's own reported total headcount figure.

headcount.basis: consolidated group / total headcount, 31 Mar 2021
headcount.source: Embracer Annual Report 2020/21 'year in brief'
headcount.note: Roughly doubled in one year via Saber, Gearbox, DECA and bolt-ons. 60 internal studios.

headcount.basis: consolidated group / total headcount, 31 Mar 2022
headcount.source: Embracer Annual Report 2021/22 'year in brief'
headcount.note: ACQUISITION-DRIVEN: doubled again. Asmodee (~2,300+), Dark Horse, Easybrain, Crystal Dynamics/Eidos, plus 31 bolt-ons. 118 internal studios. This is the biggest single-year organic-plus-M&A jump.

headcount.basis: consolidated group / total headcount, 31 Mar 2023 (PEAK)
headcount.source: Embracer Annual Report 2022/23 'year in brief'
headcount.note: PEAK. The '~17,000' commonly cited. 138 internal studios, 221 projects under development (165 unannounced). The May 2023 collapse of the ~USD 2bn Savvy/PIF investment happened days after this year closed.

headcount.basis: consolidated group / total headcount, 31 Mar 2024
headcount.source: Embracer Q4 FY23/24 report; Game Developer
headcount.note: -4,532 (-27%) vs prior year, marking completion of the restructuring program (31 Mar 2024). CAUTION: this net drop blends layoffs/studio closures with the ~2,950-person Saber Interactive divestment. ~3,727 of departures were game developers.

headcount.basis: consolidated group / total headcount, 31 Mar 2025 (post Asmodee spin-off)
headcount.source: Embracer Q4 FY24/25 interim report
headcount.note: SPIN-OFF DISTORTION: Asmodee was demerged and separately listed on 7 Feb 2025, de-consolidating thousands of board-game staff. The FY24->FY25 drop from 12,069 to 7,180 is therefore mostly structural (spin-off) plus divestments/Easybrain sale, not primarily layoffs. Of the 7,180, ~5,378 were game developers.

headcount.basis: consolidated group / total headcount, 31 Mar 2026 (post Coffee Stain spin-off; parent renamed Fellowship Entertainment)
headcount.source: Embracer Q4 FY25/26 full-year report; Icon Era; Wikipedia
headcount.note: Coffee Stain Group spun off/listed (Nasdaq First North) Dec 2025-13 Jan 2026, further de-consolidating staff. Remaining Embracer parent renamed Fellowship Entertainment. Note stockanalysis.com/S&P shows 4,747 at this date on a different (narrower/FTE) basis — sources disagree; 6,090 is the widely-reported group total-headcount figure.

layoffs.scope: Group-wide early wave (~5% of workforce) as restructuring began
layoffs.source: Wikipedia / trade press
layoffs.note: Approximate; early tranche of the Jun-2023-announced program.

layoffs.scope: Total consolidated headcount REDUCTION over FY2023/24 (16,601 -> 12,069). NOT all layoffs.
layoffs.source: Embracer Q4 FY23/24 report / Game Developer
layoffs.note: CRITICAL: this is a NET headcount reduction, not gross layoffs. It blends terminations + studio closures + the ~2,950-person Saber Interactive DIVESTMENT (a sale). Embracer's own framing: since the Jun-2023 program start ~1,583 people were laid off and ~3,000 left via the Saber divestment. ~3,727 of total departures were game developers. Restructuring cost ~SEK 3.38bn (~USD 317m).

layoffs.scope: Studio CLOSURES (people count not disclosed per studio): Volition (Aug 2023), Free Radical Design (Dec 2023), Campfire Cabal (2023), Pieces Interactive (Jun 2024), Piranha Bytes (~Jun 2024)
layoffs.source: Wikipedia / trade press
layoffs.note: count=0 is a placeholder — these are studio-closure events, not a people-count. 40+ studios closed or divested in total during the FY2023/24 program.

acquisitionDistortions: UP-SLOPE is almost entirely M&A, not organic hiring: 3,109 (Mar 2020) -> 16,601 (Mar 2023) driven by ~USD 8bn of acquisitions — Saber Interactive (2020, ~USD 525m), Gearbox (2021, ~USD 1.3bn), Asmodee (2021/22, ~USD 3.1bn incl. earnouts, added 2,300+ board-game staff), Dark Horse, Easybrain, Crystal Dynamics/Eidos-Montreal (2022), plus 100+ bolt-ons. Never read the climb as organic growth.
FY2021/22 doubling (6,325 -> 12,760) is dominated by first full consolidation of Asmodee (board games) — a different labor pool from game dev.

FY2023/24 drop (16,601 -> 12,069, -4,532) is NOT all layoffs: ~2,950 of the reduction is the DIVESTMENT (sale) of Saber Interactive to management; the rest is terminations + studio closures. Gearbox was also sold (to Take-Two, closed 2024).
FY2024/25 drop (12,069 -> 7,180) is dominated by the ASMODEE SPIN-OFF (demerged & separately listed 7 Feb 2025) plus the Easybrain divestment — de-consolidation, not workforce cuts.

FY2025/26 drop (7,180 -> 6,090) reflects the COFFEE STAIN GROUP spin-off (separately listed Dec 2025 / trading 13 Jan 2026), after which the remaining Embracer parent was renamed Fellowship Entertainment — again structural de-consolidation.

Data-provider figures diverge from group total headcount: YCharts/S&P-derived sources show ~4,874 (2023) and ~4,747 (Mar 2026) on a narrower FTE/legal-entity basis; Embracer's own 'total headcount' (incl. contracted) is 16,601 (2023) and 6,090 (2026). Use the primary Embracer figures for the trajectory and do not mix bases.
keyFindings: Peak consolidated total headcount: 16,601 at 31 Mar 2023 (the widely-cited '~17,000').

Trough of organic collapse: 12,069 at 31 Mar 2024 after the restructuring program — a 4,532 (-27%) one-year reduction, but this NET figure blends layoffs, ~40+ studio closures, and the ~2,950-person Saber Interactive divestment.
Trigger: the verbally-agreed ~USD 2bn investment (later confirmed Savvy Games Group / Saudi PIF, agreed Oct 2022) collapsed in May 2023, forcing the restructuring.
~80 game projects cancelled and 40+ studios closed or divested during FY2023/24; ~3,727 of departures were game developers.

Post-restructuring the group was split three ways: Asmodee (spun off / listed Feb 2025), Coffee Stain Group (spun off / listed Dec 2025-Jan 2026), with the remaining parent renamed Fellowship Entertainment. Post-2024 headcount declines are therefore mostly de-consolidation via spin-offs, not fresh layoffs.
End state: 6,090 employees at 31 Mar 2026 — about 37% of the 16,601 peak, and roughly double the 3,109 pre-balloon base of Mar 2020.

extras.expansion: Balloon phase FY2019/20-FY2022/23: 3,109 -> 16,601 (+13,492, ~5.3x) via ~USD 8bn of acquisitions and 100+ deals; internal studios grew from ~26 to 138.
extras.turnoverRate: Restructuring FY2023/24: -4,532 (-27%) net; program cost ~SEK 3.38bn (~USD 317m). ~80 projects cancelled, 40+ studios closed/divested.
extras.hiringPlans: No net growth mode post-2023; strategy is deleveraging and separation into independent listed companies (Asmodee, Coffee Stain, Fellowship Entertainment / Embracer).

data.summary: Nintendo's consolidated workforce grew steadily and organically from 5,120 (FY2015, ended 31 Mar 2015) to 8,666 (FY2026, ended 31 Mar 2026) — a ~69% increase over 11 years with no year-over-year decline except a small FY2016 dip (5,120 to 5,064). Growth is entirely organic (no headcount-distorting acquisitions; Nintendo does very few and small ones). All figures below are from Nintendo's own securities reports (有価証券報告書) filed with the Kanto Local Finance Bureau, and the corporate profile / FY2026 results — i.e. PRIMARY sources. Nintendo publishes two figures each year: CONSOLIDATED (連結会社の状況, group total including subsidiaries and regular part-timers, excluding people seconded out / including people seconded in) and PARENT-ONLY (提出会社の状況, Nintendo Co., Ltd. Japan only). Parent-only rose 2,009 to 3,084 over the same period. No layoffs. Extremely low turnover (1.9% at the Japanese parent in FY2025), long tenure (~14 years and rising), and high pay (~9.7-9.8M JPY average at the parent). Nintendo raised base pay +10% in April 2023 and made further undisclosed increases in April 2026. The much-discussed large new Kyoto development building is NOT yet open — it is the 'Corporate Headquarters Development Center,' with completion pushed to ~March 2029; the Nintendo Museum (a separate facility in Uji) opened Oct 2024.

acquisitionDistortions: None material. Nintendo's headcount growth FY2015-FY2026 is ORGANIC. Unlike Take-Two (Zynga, May 2022) or Microsoft (Activision Blizzard, Oct 2023), Nintendo has made no large workforce-adding acquisitions in this window. Its acquisitions (e.g. Next Level Games fully acquired Mar 2021, SRD Co. acquired Apr 2022, Dynamo Pictures/Nintendo Pictures Oct 2022) are small studios adding at most low-hundreds of staff each and do not create step-changes in the consolidated total.

Note a definitional consistency point (not a distortion): the consolidated figure includes 'regular part-timers' (常用パートタイマー) and people seconded in, and excludes people seconded out; the parent-only figure is Nintendo Co., Ltd. Japan alone. Do not blend the two series.

extras.turnoverRate: Very low. Nintendo Co., Ltd. (Japanese parent) turnover ~1.9% in FY2025 (per Game Developer's analysis of Nintendo disclosure). Regional subsidiaries run higher but still moderate: Nintendo of America ~5.1%, Nintendo of Europe ~6%, Nintendo Australia ~16.7% (all as of ~Mar 2025). These regional figures cover Nintendo's ~5,630 permanent employees. Nintendo does not publish a single group-wide turnover rate in its securities report; the ~1.9% headline refers to the Japanese parent.
extras.avgTenureYears: ~14 years at the Japanese parent and rising: 13.5 (FY2019) → 13.9 (FY2020) → 14.4 (FY2025) → 14.6 (FY2026). Parent-only average age likewise ~39-40.5. Regional subsidiary tenure is lower (NoA ~10 yrs, NoE ~11.1 yrs, Australia ~8.5 yrs). All parent-only tenure figures are from the securities reports (有価証券報告書).
extras.salaryTrend: Rising. Parent-only average annual salary (税込; includes bonuses & non-standard wages): 9,126,281 JPY (FY2019) → 9,350,972 (FY2020) → 9,666,256 (FY2025) → 9,824,708 (FY2026, +1.64% YoY). So ~9.1M climbing to ~9.8M JPY over the period, i.e. broadly the '~9-10M JPY' band. Structural driver: Nintendo raised BASE pay +10% effective April 2023 (announced by president Shuntaro Furukawa; note: 2026 press reports of a 'new' 10% raise were a misread — the 10% was 2023). Nintendo then implemented further (undisclosed-percentage) salary and starting-salary increases in April 2026.
extras.hiringPlans: Continuous net hiring; no announced targets or freezes. Consolidated net adds ran +481 (FY2025) and +461 (FY2026). Nintendo emphasizes stable long-term employment and low attrition rather than aggressive scaling; it raised starting salaries in April 2026 partly to stay competitive for new-graduate recruitment in Japan. No layoffs or restructuring in the period.
extras.expansion: Facilities: The large new Kyoto R&D facility — the 'Nintendo Corporate Headquarters Development Center' (previously 'Second Development Building') adjacent to the Kyoto HQ, on land bought in 2022 — is NOT yet open. Completion was pushed back (originally ~2027/2028) to roughly March 2029 after Nintendo up-scaled the design (~9 floors + basement, ~49,305 sqm total floor area, ~¥121bn cost). Separately, the Nintendo Museum (a visitor facility in Uji, Kyoto, at the former Uji Ogura plant) opened 2 Oct 2024 — this is a museum, not an R&D/dev building. An earlier 'Nintendo Development Center' next to HQ was completed in the mid-2010s. Corporate: majority-owned/expanded studios in the window include Next Level Games (fully acquired Mar 2021), SRD Co. (Apr 2022), and Dynamo Pictures → Nintendo Pictures (Oct 2022).

keyFindings: Consolidated headcount rose organically from 5,120 (FY2015) to 8,666 (FY2026), ~+69%, with only one small YoY dip (FY2016). No layoffs anywhere in the series.
Two distinct series must not be blended: CONSOLIDATED group (5,120 → 8,666) vs PARENT-ONLY Nintendo Co., Ltd. Japan (2,009 → 3,084). The parent is ~35-40% of the group.

Growth is fully organic — no Take-Two/Zynga- or Microsoft/Activision-style acquisition step-change. Nintendo's studio acquisitions in the window are small (Next Level Games, SRD, Dynamo/Nintendo Pictures).
Textbook labor stability: Japanese-parent turnover ~1.9% (FY2025), average tenure ~14.4-14.6 years, average age ~40, average pay ~9.7-9.8M JPY.
The +10% base-pay raise was APRIL 2023 (not 2026); a further undisclosed raise landed April 2026. 2026 press reports of a 'new' 10% raise were a misattribution of the 2023 event.

CAUTION on the user's premise about a 'new Kyoto R&D building opened ~2024': the big HQ development center is delayed to ~March 2029 and is NOT open. What opened in Oct 2024 was the Nintendo Museum in Uji, a visitor/exhibition facility, not a dev building.
All headcount, salary, age and tenure figures are from PRIMARY sources: Nintendo securities reports (有価証券報告書) and the official corporate profile — not estimates.

data.summary: Capcom's consolidated headcount grew organically and steadily from 2,681 (FY2015, ended Mar 2015) to 3,976 (FY2026, ended Mar 2026) — a ~48% increase over 11 years, with the pace accelerating sharply after FY2021. Growth is entirely organic developer hiring, NOT acquisition-driven: Capcom made no material headcount-adding M&A over this window, so its series is a clean signal of deliberate in-house R&D expansion (development personnel rose from 2,285 in FY2021 to 3,011 targeted/3,011-area by FY2026, and the company publicly commits to hiring at least ~100 new developers annually). This expansion is backed by an aggressive HR investment strategy: a ~30% average base-salary increase from April 2022, a more performance-linked bonus, an employee stock-grant (ESOP) plan, a new-graduate starting salary raised to 300,000 yen/month in April 2025, and a new/expanded R&D building/development facilities. Retention is strong (turnover 2.8-5.4%, latest 2.8%; average tenure ~10 years) and per-employee economics are exceptional (consolidated operating income per employee rose from 10,975k yen in FY2021 to 17,466k yen in FY2025). All headcount figures FY2015-FY2020 come directly from Capcom's Japanese securities reports (yuho / 有価証券報告書), FY2021-FY2025 from the official reference table in the FY2025 securities report, and FY2026 from Capcom's own corporate-information IR page.

headcount.note: As of Mar 31, 2026. Consolidated 3,976; non-consolidated 3,593. +210 YoY. Development personnel ~3,011 (per Capcom's mid-term hiring roadmap).

acquisitionDistortions: NONE material. Capcom's headcount growth is fully organic in-house developer hiring, not M&A. Unlike Take-Two (Zynga, May 2022) or Microsoft (Activision Blizzard, Oct 2023), Capcom did not make headcount-adding acquisitions across FY2015-FY2026; the +48% rise reflects deliberate R&D staffing under its human-capital investment strategy (target ~100+ new developers/year). The one notable divestiture direction (Capcom is a net hirer, not divester) does not distort the series.
Minor caution: the two small YoY dips — FY2017 (2,811, -28) and FY2019 (2,832, -120) — are organic attrition/normalization, NOT layoffs. No mass layoff events are on record for Capcom in this window.

extras.expansion: Development personnel grew from 2,285 (FY2021) to 2,846 (FY2025), ~+561 developers (+24.5%) in four years, and ~3,011 targeted by FY2026. Company built/expanded R&D and development facilities ('organizing and improving the working environment ... to expand its development environment and facilities to accommodate the increasing scale of development') including a new R&D building in Osaka. Formed a Chief Human Resources Officer function coordinating an R&D Human Resources Dept, People Strategy Team, and Health & Productivity Management Dept.
extras.hiringPlans: Public commitment to hire at least ~100 new developers every year; new-graduate starting monthly salary raised to 300,000 yen from April 2025 to strengthen recruitment of top talent. Aggressive developer-focused hiring is the explicit growth lever.
extras.salaryTrend: ~30% average base-salary increase for full-time employees effective Apr 1, 2022, plus a more performance-linked bonus and an employee stock-grant (ESOP) plan (established Jun 2022). Non-consolidated average annual salary rose ¥6,034k (FY2021) → ¥7,127k (FY2022) → ¥7,660k (FY2023) → ¥8,328k (FY2024) → ¥9,185k (FY2025), ~+52% cumulative over four years. Earlier baseline: ¥5,712k (FY2017), ¥5,998k (FY2020).
extras.turnoverRate: Low. Non-consolidated turnover rate: 3.9% (FY2021), 5.4% (FY2022), 3.5% (FY2023), 2.9% (FY2024), 2.8% (FY2025) — latest 2.8%, well below Japanese industry norms.
extras.avgTenureYears: ~10 years average continuous service (non-consolidated): 10.0 years (FY2017), 10.2 years (FY2020); average age ~36-37. Long tenure reflects strong retention.

keyFindings: Consolidated headcount: 2,681 (FY2015) rising to 3,976 (FY2026), +48% over 11 years, all organic — no acquisition distortion.
Growth accelerated post-FY2021: from ~2,900-3,150 plateau (FY2016-FY2021) to +150-235 net adds per year FY2022-FY2026, driven by developer hiring.
Development personnel (R&D staff) is the growth engine: 2,285 (FY2021) → 2,846 (FY2025), ~+561 (+24.5%); Capcom pledges ~100+ new devs/year and ~75% of revenue is Digital Contents.
~30% average base-salary raise (Apr 2022) plus ESOP and higher new-grad pay; average annual salary +52% cumulative FY2021-FY2025 (¥6,034k → ¥9,185k).
Exceptional per-employee profitability: consolidated operating income per employee ¥10,975k (FY2021) → ¥17,466k (FY2025), +59%.
Very low attrition (turnover 2.8% latest, 2.8-5.4% range) and long tenure (~10 years) — hallmark of a healthy, retention-strong studio, in contrast to Western layoff waves.
No layoffs on record FY2015-FY2026; the small FY2017 and FY2019 YoY dips are organic attrition, not workforce cuts.

Two headcount bases to keep distinct: consolidated group (3,976 FY2026) vs non-consolidated / parent-only (3,593 FY2026); salary, tenure and turnover metrics are reported on the non-consolidated basis.

data.summary: Square Enix Holdings is a Japanese holding company whose consolidated group headcount peaked at 5,637 (FY ended Mar 2022) and has declined every year since to 4,290 (Mar 2026) — a ~24% (~1,347-person) drop over four years. The single largest cut was NOT an organic layoff but a DIVESTITURE: on 26 Aug 2022 Embracer Group completed the ~$300M purchase (announced 2 May 2022) of Crystal Dynamics, Eidos-Montreal and Square Enix Montreal plus 50+ IPs (Tomb Raider, Deus Ex, Thief, Legacy of Kain), removing ~1,100 Western developers from the group and driving the -925 net drop between Mar-2022 (5,637) and Mar-2023 (4,712). The holding company itself (submitting-company / non-consolidated basis) employs only 26 people; the operating headcount sits in subsidiaries, overwhelmingly in the Digital Entertainment segment (3,527 of 4,770 at Mar-2024). After the Embracer exit, a Japan-first 'multiplatform' strategy triggered a review of the HD-games pipeline: FY2024/3 booked a ~¥22.0B loss on disposal / abandonment of content (~$140.8M) from cancelled DE titles. This was followed by explicit organic restructuring of the WEST: on 8 Nov 2025 Square Enix confirmed layoffs across North American and European publishing (UK: ~137 positions at risk; US cuts also), consolidating HD-games publishing from 11 divisions to 4 and 'consolidating development functions in Japan' — a ¥11.8B FY2026/3 restructuring charge for >¥3B/yr savings. Net effect: a Japanese corporate reset exported to the West — Tokyo development is being retained and reinforced while Western studios and mobile/publishing ops absorb the cuts.
