headcount.note: As of 31 Mar 2026. Net -314 (-6.82%) YoY — the largest ORGANIC decline, reflecting the FY2026 Western/publishing restructuring. Down ~24% from the 5,637 peak (Mar 2022).

layoffs.scope: DIVESTITURE, not a layoff — Crystal Dynamics + Eidos-Montreal + Square Enix Montreal (3 studios, 8 global locations) sold to Embracer Group for ~$300M; ~1,100 Western developers deconsolidated. Announced 2 May 2022, completed 26 Aug 2022.
layoffs.source: Embracer Group press releases (2 May 2022 announcement; 26 Aug 2022 completion); VGC/Gematsu
layoffs.note: ~1,100 is the announced staff count of the transferred studios; these people were NOT fired — they moved to Embracer with the sold IPs (Tomb Raider, Deus Ex, Thief, Legacy of Kain, 50+ IPs). This is the ACQUISITION/DIVESTITURE distortion behind the FY2023 -925 net headcount drop.

layoffs.scope: Organic layoffs — North American & European PUBLISHING operations. UK: ~137 positions at risk (final number possibly lower after statutory redundancy consultation); additional US dismissals (count not disclosed). Part of consolidating HD-games publishing from 11 divisions to 4 and shifting development to Japan.
layoffs.source: Square Enix H1 FY2026/3 earnings (8 Nov 2025); Anime News Network; Game Developer
layoffs.note: ¥11.8B restructuring charge booked for FY ending Mar 2026; expected >¥3B/yr savings. This is the WEST-facing organic cut ('Japanese reset exported to the West'). Tokyo/Japan development retained and reinforced. US headcount not quantified — 137 is UK-only and 'at risk', so total gross cut is higher but not officially disclosed.

acquisitionDistortions: FY2023/3 -925 net headcount drop (5,637 -> 4,712) is a DIVESTITURE artefact, not organic attrition: Embracer completed acquisition of Crystal Dynamics / Eidos-Montreal / Square Enix Montreal (~1,100 staff) on 26 Aug 2022. Do NOT read this as 925 people laid off.

The ~1,100 Embracer figure (deconsolidated) is LARGER than the 925 net drop because Square Enix simultaneously did some organic hiring in Japan (Digital Entertainment), partially offsetting the divestiture within FY2023.
FY2024/3 +58 net gain (4,712 -> 4,770) masks internal mix shift: Japanese DE hiring while HD pipeline was being culled (¥22.0B content-abandonment loss recognised the same year).

Holding-company (提出会社) figure of 26 is not comparable to any operating-company or peer headcount — it is a pure holdco shell; always use the ~4,300-5,600 consolidated basis for group-size comparisons.
keyFindings: Consolidated headcount peaked at 5,637 (Mar 2022) and fell to 4,290 (Mar 2026): -1,347 / -24% over four years.
The biggest single drop (-925, FY2023) was a DIVESTITURE (Embracer, ~1,100 Western devs), not a layoff.
Holding company alone = 26 employees (non-consolidated 提出会社 basis) vs ~4,770 consolidated (Mar 2024) — a textbook holdco-vs-group split.
Digital Entertainment dominates: 3,527 of 4,770 (74%) at Mar 2024; Amusement 456, Publishing 207, Rights&Property 70, admin 510.

FY2024/3 booked ~¥22.0B (~$140.8M) loss on disposal/abandonment of content from cancelled HD (Digital Entertainment) titles under the revised, more selective HD-games strategy. (The ~13.7B JPY figure cited in the brief is a sub-component; the officially disclosed extraordinary loss on disposal of content is ~¥22.0B.)

FY2026/3 organic restructuring is West-concentrated: NA/EU publishing layoffs (UK ~137 at risk + undisclosed US cuts), HD publishing collapsed from 11 to 4 divisions, ¥11.8B charge, >¥3B/yr targeted savings, development consolidated in Japan.

Strategic frame confirmed: a Japan-first 'multiplatform' reset in which Tokyo development is retained/reinforced while Western studios, mobile and overseas publishing absorb the cuts — a Japanese reset exported to the West.

extras.expansion: No net expansion since 2022; the only YoY increase in the window was a modest +58 in FY2024/3 (organic Japan/DE hiring). All other years declined. Group female ratio ~29%, foreign-national ratio ~9% (FY2024/3 securities report).
extras.hiringPlans: Post-2024 'multiplatform' + structural-reform strategy consolidates DEVELOPMENT functions in Japan (Tokyo retained/reinforced) while cutting overseas publishing/mobile. Net headcount guidance is downward through FY2026/3.
extras.salaryTrend: Holding-company (26 staff) avg annual pay ¥13,139,416 (Mar 2024) — not representative of operating subsidiaries. Group-wide operating-company pay not separately disclosed in holdco filing.
extras.avgTenureYears: 5.8 years (holding company / 提出会社 basis, Mar 2024; avg age 47.1). Operating-subsidiary tenure not disclosed at group level.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed as a rate; inferred net churn is heavy — ~24% consolidated decline 2022-2026, but dominated by the one-off Embracer divestiture (FY2023) rather than steady attrition.

data.summary: Bandai Namco Holdings is a diversified Japanese IP/entertainment conglomerate whose consolidated group headcount grew steadily to ~11,345 regular employees as of 31 Mar 2025 (yuho basis), NOT a games-only figure. The group spans four units: Toys and Hobby, Digital (games/network), IP Production (visual/music), and Amusement (arcades/facilities). The Digital unit — the games business — is the relevant games-industry segment: 3,412 regular employees at 31 Mar 2025, down from a peak of 3,712 at 31 Mar 2024. That ~300-person decline (plus ~110 fewer temps) is the visible footprint of the FY2024/FY2025 restructuring, in which Bandai Namco booked roughly ¥21bn (~$141M) in write-downs on cancelled/paused games (including Naruto- and One Piece-licensed titles and a shuttered Nintendo-commissioned project), shut down Blue Protocol and Tales of the Rays, and — per Bloomberg/press reporting — moved ~200 of Bandai Namco Studios' ~1,300 staff to idle-work assignments ('oidashi-beya'), with ~100 resigning by autumn 2024. Crucially there was NO mass formal layoff announcement; attrition was managed via voluntary departures. Meanwhile the parent RAISED wages: holding-company average annual salary rose from ¥10,950k (FY2024.3) to ¥12,161k (FY2025.3), and group-wide the workforce kept expanding on the back of Toys/Hobby and Amusement, not games. Note two coexisting official 'consolidated total' series with slightly different scopes: the securities report (yuho) 就業人員 basis gives 11,159 (FY2024.3) and 11,345 (FY2025.3); the ESG data book gives 11,031 (FY2025.3-reported as covered by OH&S policy). Both are cited below.

acquisitionDistortions: No acquisition-driven headcount spike in the games/Digital unit in this window — Digital-unit growth to FY2024.3 was organic, and the FY2025.3 decline reflects restructuring, not divestiture.

Group-total growth FY2021.3->FY2025.3 (9,550->11,345) is driven mainly by Toys and Hobby (temps +607 in FY2025.3 alone) and Amusement expansion, NOT games — do not read rising group headcount as games hiring.
IP Production unit temp headcount rose partly from consolidating 8bit Inc. (エイトビット) into scope in FY2025.3 (+13 temps) — a consolidation-scope effect, not organic hiring.
FY2023.3 segment employee figures are on a REVISED segment definition (reporting segments changed that year), so pre-FY2023 Digital-unit numbers are not directly comparable.
keyFindings: Consolidated group headcount is ~11,345 (yuho, 31 Mar 2025) / ~11,031 (ESG-book scope) — a conglomerate figure including toys, amusement, IP production; NOT a games headcount.
The GAMES-relevant Digital unit: 3,412 regular employees at 31 Mar 2025, down ~300 (~8%) from its 3,712 peak at 31 Mar 2024 — the clearest quantitative sign of the restructuring.

Restructuring was cost-/project-driven: ~¥21bn (~$141M) write-downs over 9 months to Dec-2024 on cancelled/paused games (Naruto, One Piece, a Nintendo-commissioned project); Blue Protocol and Tales of the Rays shut down.

No mass formal layoff: staff reduction executed via 'oidashi-beya' idle-room reassignments (~200 at Bandai Namco Studios) and voluntary attrition (~100 resigned by autumn 2024) — per Bloomberg/press, not official filings.
Core JP staff stable and wages RAISED: holding-company average annual salary rose ~11% YoY, ¥10,950k (FY2024.3) -> ¥12,161k (FY2025.3); parent average tenure ~19-20 years.
Group total kept rising through the restructuring because Toys/Hobby and Amusement expanded — masking the games-unit contraction in the headline number.

extras.avgTenureYears: Group ~12.8 yrs (FY2025.3, all employees; male 14.3 / female 10.3) per ESG data book. Holding-company (parent-only) 19.3 yrs (FY2025.3), 19.9 yrs (FY2024.3) per securities report.
extras.turnoverRate: Group full-time turnover 174 employees in FY2025.3 (106 male, 68 female), up from 133 in FY2024.3 and 136 in FY2023.3 (headcount of departures, full-time only, excludes mandatory retirement/intra-group transfers) — ESG data book. No group-wide % rate disclosed; on ~4,488 full-time base this implies ~3.9%.
extras.salaryTrend: RISING. Holding-company average annual salary (incl. bonus & allowances): ¥10,950k (FY2024.3) -> ¥12,161k (FY2025.3), +~11% YoY. Company has publicly emphasized base-pay increases; parent-only figures (46 staff, avg age 46.7).
extras.hiringPlans: Continued net group hiring outside games: FY2025.3 new-graduate hires 188, mid-career hires 138 (ESG data book). Toys/Hobby added +607 temps in FY2025.3 for sales expansion. Games/Digital unit was NOT in expansion — it contracted.
extras.expansion: Group expanding in Toys and Hobby and Amusement (facilities), plus European reorganization (+27 holding-company staff FY2025.3) and 8bit consolidation in IP Production. Digital/games unit in selective retrenchment: pruning underperforming mobile/online titles while retaining console/IP core.
extras.turnoverRateNote: Reported turnover count rose sharply in FY2025.3 (174 vs 133) consistent with restructuring-driven voluntary departures concentrated in the games/Digital unit.

data.summary: Sega Sammy Holdings is a Japanese entertainment conglomerate (Entertainment Contents / Sega games, Pachislot & Pachinko / Sammy, and Gaming-Resort). Its FY ends 31 March. On the group's primary reporting basis (quarter-end full-time + permanent contract staff, EXCLUDING temporary employees — the figure disclosed in the quarterly Data Appendix), consolidated headcount rose steadily through the pandemic recovery and the Aug-2023 Rovio acquisition, peaking at 8,623 at end-March 2024 (FY2024/3), then FELL to 8,147 at end-March 2025 (FY2025/3) — a net contraction of ~476 driven by the 2024 'global restructuring'. That restructuring was concentrated OVERSEAS/in the West, not in Japan: (a) Sega of America cut ~61 staff in California (Jan 2024); (b) Sega Europe cut 240 roles across Sega Europe, Creative Assembly (Total War; after the Hyenas cancellation) and Sega Hardlight (announced Mar/Apr 2024); (c) Relic Entertainment (Company of Heroes; Vancouver, ~150 staff) was DIVESTED to Emona Capital in 2024 — a disposal, not layoffs, but it removes staff from the consolidated count; (d) Amplitude Studios (Paris; Humankind/Endless) was divested via a management buyout in Nov 2024, generating a ~¥5.9bn (~$38.7m) extraordinary loss. Meanwhile core Japan development was GROWING under the 'Super Game' plan and Sega raised base salaries (~+30% base / ~+15% total comp reported) from Jul 2023. IMPORTANT CAVEAT on the task premise: I could NOT verify a distinct 'summer 2024 voluntary-retirement program in Japan of a few hundred' in any primary filing or trade source. The prominent Japan voluntary-retirement event (650 sought / 729 departed, ~8% of payroll) was the COVID program of Nov 2020–Feb 2021, a separate historical event — likely the source of confusion. The documented 2024 cuts were overseas. NOTE ON BASIS: a second, broader figure — total employees INCLUDING temporary staff — is far higher; the group's 'At a Glance' page shows 11,944 as of Mar 2026. These two bases must not be blended.

headcount.basis: consolidated group total, quarter-end (FY2022/3 Q4 = end-Mar 2022); full-time + permanent contract employees, EXCLUDES temporary employees
headcount.source: Sega Sammy Holdings Data Appendix (FY2025/3), 'Number of employees' table
headcount.note: Segment split at this date: Entertainment Contents 5,630 / Pachislot & Pachinko 1,047 / Resort 664 / Others 419.

headcount.note: Segment split: Entertainment Contents 6,035 / Pachislot & Pachinko 1,108 / Resort 628 / Others 448. Pre-Rovio.

headcount.basis: consolidated group total, quarter-end (FY2024/3 Q4 = end-Mar 2024); excludes temporary employees
headcount.source: Sega Sammy Holdings Data Appendix (FY2025/3), 'Number of employees' table
headcount.note: PEAK on this basis. Segment split: Entertainment Contents 6,361 / Pachislot & Pachinko 1,138 / Resort 626 / Others 498. ACQUISITION DISTORTION: Rovio (~500 staff) consolidated from Aug 2023, inflating Entertainment Contents; Entertainment Contents had peaked mid-year at 6,749 (FY2024/3 Q3) before Western cuts began.

headcount.basis: consolidated group total, quarter-end (FY2025/3 Q4 = end-Mar 2025); excludes temporary employees
headcount.source: Sega Sammy Holdings Data Appendix (FY2025/3), 'Number of employees' table
headcount.note: NET DECLINE of ~476 vs FY2024/3 (8,623). Segment classifications changed in FY2025: Entertainment Contents 6,391 / Pachislot & Pachinko 1,127 / Gaming 104 / Others 525 (former 'Resort' folded into 'Gaming'+'Others'). Reflects Relic divestiture, Amplitude MBO and Western layoffs; partly offset by Japan (Super Game) growth. Overseas personnel ratio fell 27.7%->25.8%, confirming cuts were overseas-weighted.

headcount.basis: consolidated group total INCLUDING temporary employees (broader basis than the quarterly appendix figures above)
headcount.source: Sega Sammy Holdings 'SEGA SAMMY at a Glance' corporate page
headcount.note: DIFFERENT BASIS — do not compare to the ~8,000 appendix figures. Definition per source: 'total number of full-time employees, permanent contract employees and temporary employees.' R&D personnel ~54.5%, overseas personnel ~31.9% (both excl. temporary).

layoffs.scope: Sega of America (California) — WARN-notice layoffs, OVERSEAS/US
layoffs.source: Game Developer / trade press (via WARN)
layoffs.note: Distinct from the later Europe cuts.

layoffs.scope: Sega Europe — split across Sega Europe, Creative Assembly (Total War studio) and Sega Hardlight; OVERSEAS/UK-Europe. Announced late Mar/early Apr 2024
layoffs.source: Variety / GamesIndustry.biz / Game World Observer
layoffs.note: Majority at Creative Assembly + Sega Europe, smaller number at Hardlight. Follows the Hyenas (Creative Assembly) cancellation. Cited by Sega as 'structural reforms' due to European Consumer-business downturn.

layoffs.scope: Relic Entertainment (Vancouver, Company of Heroes) — DIVESTITURE, not layoffs. Shares transferred to a holding co. backed by UK's Emona Capital; studio became independent. OVERSEAS/Canada
layoffs.source: Variety / Nintendo Life / GamesHub
layoffs.note: Staff (~150, estimate) removed from consolidated headcount via disposal, NOT a headcount cut. Do not double-count with the 240 Europe layoffs.

layoffs.scope: Amplitude Studios (Paris; Humankind, Endless series) — DIVESTITURE via management buyout (MBO). OVERSEAS/France
layoffs.source: Game Developer / SEGADriven / Sega Sammy IR (extraordinary loss disclosure)
layoffs.note: ~¥5.9bn (~$38.7m) extraordinary loss recorded in FY2025/3 Q2. Disposal, not layoffs — staff leave consolidated count with the studio.

acquisitionDistortions: Rovio (~500 staff) consolidated from Aug 2023 inflates the FY2024/3 peak of 8,623.
Segment reclassification (Resort -> Gaming/Others) between FY2024/3 and FY2025/3.
FY2024->FY2025 net -476 blends real layoffs (SoA ~61, Europe 240), divestitures (Relic, Amplitude MBO) and offsetting Japan hiring.
Two bases: appendix ~8,000 excl. temporary vs 'At a Glance' 11,944 incl. temporary.

extras.expansion: Core Japan game development GROWING under the multi-year 'Super Game' plan (large-scale global/live-service titles); this organic Japan hiring partially offset the overseas cuts and kept the Entertainment Contents segment near ~6,400 despite Western reductions and the Relic/Amplitude divestitures.
extras.salaryTrend: Sega Corporation revised its compensation system effective 1 Jul 2023: raised base salary and lifted the base-salary ratio within annual pay, described as raising average monthly base salary by ~30% (reported as ~+15% total annual compensation on average). Aimed at retaining dev talent in Japan against the weak yen and global competition.
extras.hiringPlans: Japan-centric developer hiring tied to Super Game; overseas Consumer footprint deliberately shrunk (Europe restructuring, Relic and Amplitude divested). Overseas personnel ratio fell from 27.7% (end-Mar 2024) to 25.8% (end-Mar 2025), confirming the JP-vs-overseas asymmetry.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed as a hard consolidated figure in reviewed primary sources.

keyFindings: PRIMARY-SOURCE consolidated headcount (excl. temporary, quarter-end): Mar-2022 7,760 -> Mar-2023 8,219 -> Mar-2024 8,623 (PEAK) -> Mar-2025 8,147. The 2024 restructuring produced a NET decline of ~476 in FY2025/3.

The 2024 'global restructuring' cuts were OVERSEAS, not Japan: Sega of America ~61 (Jan 2024, US), Sega Europe 240 (Mar/Apr 2024: Creative Assembly + Sega Europe + Hardlight, UK/Europe). Japan development GREW under Super Game.

TWO divestitures (not layoffs) also cut the consolidated count in 2024: Relic Entertainment sold to Emona Capital (~150 staff, Vancouver) and Amplitude Studios sold via MBO (Paris, Nov 2024, ~¥5.9bn extraordinary loss). Divestiture headcount reduction must not be counted as layoffs.
ACQUISITION DISTORTION: Rovio (~500 staff) consolidated from Aug 2023 inflated the FY2024/3 peak — the FY2023->FY2024 rise is largely inorganic.

TASK-PREMISE CORRECTION: No primary or trade source confirms a distinct 'summer 2024 voluntary-retirement program in Japan of a few hundred'. The famous Japan VRP (650 sought / 729 departed, ~8% of payroll) was the COVID program of Nov 2020–Feb 2021 — a separate, earlier event. Treat any 'summer-2024 Japan VRP' claim as UNVERIFIED.
Overseas personnel ratio fell 27.7% (Mar-2024) -> 25.8% (Mar-2025), quantitatively confirming that the burden of cuts fell on overseas rather than Japan.
BASIS WARNING: group-wide figure including temporary employees is 11,944 (Mar 2026) — a different basis from the ~8,000 appendix numbers; the two must never be blended.

data.company: Konami Group Corporation (コナミグループ株式会社)
data.summary: Konami Group is a diversified Japanese holding company spanning Digital Entertainment (games: Metal Gear Solid Delta, Silent Hill 2/f remakes, eFootball, Yu-Gi-Oh), Amusement (pachislot/arcade), Gaming & Systems (casino slot machines/casino management systems, largely Nevada-regulated), and Sports (fitness clubs; Health is folded into Sports). CONSOLIDATED headcount grew from 9,038 (5,045 regular + avg 3,993 temporary) as of 31 Mar 2025 to 9,210 (5,186 regular + avg 4,024 temporary) as of 31 Mar 2026 — a small, organic net rise (~+172), no acquisition distortions. CRITICAL BASIS NUANCE: the primary securities report (yuho) headline table counts REGULAR employees only (就業人員 = 5,045 at 31 Mar 2025) and lists average temporary headcount separately in parentheses; the ~9,000+ 'total' figures add the two. Nearly all temporary heads sit in the Sports segment (fitness-club part-timers: 3,860 of the 3,993 temps), which inflates group totals relative to a games-only view. The games (Digital Entertainment) segment regular headcount is 2,208 (31 Mar 2025). Konami has raised base pay for five consecutive years (latest: +JPY5,000/month effective Mar 2027; new-grad starting salary lifted to JPY310,000/month, +29% vs Feb 2023). No games-segment layoffs found; the group is in an expansion/wage-raise posture on the back of record Digital Entertainment profits.

headcount.basis: consolidated group TOTAL = 5,045 regular (就業人員) + 3,993 average temporary employees; combined figure per group HR/sustainability disclosure
headcount.source: Konami sustainability human-capital page (cross-checks to yuho FY2025)
headcount.note: Regular-employee component (5,045) and temporary (3,993) match the FY2025 yuho segment table exactly. The sustainability English table mislabels columns; the 5,045+3,993 pair is the 31 Mar 2025 snapshot.

headcount.note: PRIMARY. Segment split (regular / temp): Digital Entertainment 2,208 (25); Amusement 829 (48); Gaming & Systems 647 (0); Sports 839 (3,860); Corporate common 522 (60). Total 5,045 (3,993).

headcount.basis: Digital Entertainment (GAMES) segment, REGULAR employees only; + avg 25 temporary
headcount.source: Konami Group yuho, EDINET S100W7UN, segment employee table as of 31 Mar 2025
headcount.note: This is the closest primary figure to a 'games headcount'; covers mobile/card/console/other digital content across the group's DE subsidiaries.

headcount.basis: parent/holding company only (提出会社, Konami Group Corporation non-consolidated); regular employees
headcount.source: Konami Group yuho, EDINET S100W7UN, '5.(2) 提出会社の状況' as of 31 Mar 2025
headcount.note: Holdco staff only. Avg age 35.6; avg tenure 10.0 yr (group-transfer inclusive); avg annual salary JPY7,898,848 (incl. bonus & non-standard wages). Do NOT confuse with the group total.

headcount.basis: consolidated group TOTAL = 5,186 regular + 4,024 average temporary employees
headcount.source: Konami sustainability human-capital page (latest column, 31 Mar 2026 snapshot)
headcount.note: Latest available. Net +172 vs prior year; organic (no acquisitions). Awaiting the FY-ended-Mar-2026 yuho for the confirmed segment split.

headcount.basis: consolidated group, REGULAR employees only; excludes avg 4,024 temporary
headcount.source: Konami sustainability human-capital page, 31 Mar 2026
headcount.note: Regular-employee headcount grew +141 YoY (5,045 -> 5,186).

acquisitionDistortions: None material to headcount. The 9,038->9,210 consolidated rise (31 Mar 2025 -> 31 Mar 2026) is organic; no large acquisition inflated the count in this window.

Sports-segment temporary staff distortion (NOT an acquisition, but a mix distortion worth flagging): 3,860 of the group's ~3,993 average temporary employees sit in the fitness-club Sports segment as of 31 Mar 2025. Group 'total' headcount (~9,000+) therefore overstates the games/entertainment workforce; the games (Digital Entertainment) regular headcount is only 2,208.

Historical (pre-window) context: Konami has been a stable holding structure since the 2006 shift to a holding company; no Zynga/Activision-style step-change events apply. Treat any single-year jump as organic unless a specific M&A is cited.

extras.expansion: Expansion posture. Group headcount +172 net YoY (9,038 -> 9,210); regular employees +141 (5,045 -> 5,186). Growth is funded by record Digital Entertainment results — group revenue JPY421.6bn in FY ended Mar 2025 (+17.0% YoY) rising to ~JPY493.7bn in FY ended Mar 2026 (+17.1%, third straight record); Digital Entertainment ~75% of revenue at a ~37% profit margin. Drivers: Metal Gear Solid Delta: Snake Eater, Silent Hill 2 remake (2M+ shipped) and Silent Hill f, eFootball (surpassed 1B cumulative downloads Apr 2026), and Yu-Gi-Oh card business.
extras.salaryTrend: Five consecutive years of base-pay raises. Latest (announced 2026-03-26): base salary +JPY5,000/month effective March 2027; new-graduate starting salary raised to JPY310,000/month (+29% vs JPY240,000 in Feb 2023). Prior (2025-02-19): 4th consecutive raise, +JPY5,000/month effective March 2026, new-grad starting salary JPY305,000. Parent-company average annual salary rose JPY7,898,848 (FY ended Mar 2025) -> JPY8,502,600 (FY ended Mar 2026), ~+7.6%. Scope: regular employees at domestic (Japan) group companies, with some subsidiaries/job categories excluded.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed as a turnover figure; Konami reports a RETENTION rate (定着率) of 97.2% for both the FY-ended-Mar-2025 and FY-ended-Mar-2026 snapshots (implying ~2.8% attrition on that basis).
extras.avgTenureYears: 15.0 (FY ended Mar 2025) -> 15.5 (FY ended Mar 2026) on the group HR/sustainability basis. NOTE: the yuho parent-company (提出会社) figure is different — 10.0 years — because it counts only holdco staff; do not blend the two bases.
extras.hiringPlans: No specific numeric hiring target disclosed in the reviewed sources. Raised new-grad starting salaries (JPY310,000/month) signal active graduate recruitment and talent-retention competition, consistent with the games-segment ramp.

keyFindings: CONSOLIDATED group headcount: 9,038 as of 31 Mar 2025 -> 9,210 as of 31 Mar 2026 (both = regular + average temporary combined). Organic net +172.

BASIS WARNING: the primary yuho headline counts REGULAR employees only (5,045 at 31 Mar 2025); temporary staff (avg 3,993) are listed separately. The ~9,000 'total' blends the two — never treat 5,045 and 9,038 as the same basis.
Games (Digital Entertainment) segment = 2,208 regular employees (31 Mar 2025). This is the meaningful 'games headcount', not the ~9k group total.
Temporary-staff distortion: 3,860 of ~3,993 group temps are Sports-segment (fitness-club) part-timers, inflating group totals versus a games view.
Parent/holdco (提出会社) is only 250 people (avg salary JPY7,898,848, tenure 10.0 yr as of 31 Mar 2025) — do not confuse with the consolidated group.
Wages: five straight years of base raises; +JPY5,000/mo (eff. Mar 2027) and new-grad starting pay JPY310,000/mo (+29% vs Feb 2023). Parent avg annual salary JPY7.90M -> JPY8.50M.
No layoffs identified. Konami is expanding/raising pay on record Digital Entertainment profits (MGS Delta, Silent Hill remakes, eFootball 1B+ downloads, Yu-Gi-Oh).

Terminology caution: Konami/press label the year ended 31 Mar 2025 as 'FY2025' (revenue JPY421.6bn) and the year ended 31 Mar 2026 as 'FY2026' (~JPY493.7bn). All figures here are anchored to calendarYear for comparability.

data.company: Koei Tecmo Holdings Co., Ltd. (株式会社コーエーテクモホールディングス)
data.summary: Koei Tecmo Holdings is a Japanese game holding company (Yokohama; TSE Prime: 3635, FY ends 31 March) whose core operating subsidiary is Koei Tecmo Games. Its consolidated headcount has grown steadily and organically from 1,983 (FY2021) to 2,684 (FY2025, per the EDINET securities report), reaching an estimated ~2,835 at FY2026 (stockanalysis.com). Growth is entirely organic new-graduate/mid-career hiring — there are NO acquisition-driven jumps and NO layoffs in the record. Koei Tecmo is the industry poster child for the "organic growth / retention" model: a ~42% operating margin (FY2026, one of the highest in the global game industry), 8+ consecutive years of base pay raises, a +7.7% employee-income lift in 2023 (on top of a +23% base-salary raise in 2022), a raised new-graduate starting salary of ¥305,000/month, and long average tenure (9.4 years at the holding company). IMPORTANT BASIS NOTE: the ¥7,939,258 average annual salary, 38.2 average age and 9.4-year average tenure in the securities report are for the HOLDING COMPANY ONLY (113 employees), a standard Japanese yuho reporting quirk — they are NOT the group-wide (2,684-person) figures. Group-wide average annual salary is reported by third parties at roughly ¥7.3–7.9M.

headcount.basis: consolidated group (連結); excludes 550 avg temp workers. Segment split: Entertainment 2,342 / Amusement 56 / Corporate(common) 286
headcount.source: EDINET 有価証券報告書 FY2025 (submitted 20 Jun 2025), 従業員の状況 section
headcount.note: Most recent official filing. Entertainment (games) segment = 2,342 employees. Avg temp workers = 550.

headcount.basis: consolidated group (連結), estimate pending official FY2026 securities report
headcount.source: stockanalysis.com (aggregator, pre-official-filing)
headcount.note: Not yet confirmed against the FY2026 yuho (due ~Jun 2026). Continues the ~+150/yr organic-growth trend.

acquisitionDistortions: None. Unlike Take-Two (Zynga May 2022) or Microsoft (Activision Oct 2023), Koei Tecmo's headcount growth is entirely organic. The 5-year rise from 1,983 to 2,684 reflects new-graduate and mid-career hiring, not M&A. The group's core studios (Koei Tecmo Games, formed from the 2009 Koei + Tecmo merger, plus in-house QA/Wave/Net subsidiaries) have been stable for over a decade; no material game-studio acquisitions inflate the consolidated count during FY2021-FY2025.

Minor structural note (not a headcount distortion): in Feb/Apr 2025 the group set up Koei Tecmo Corporate Finance to centralize group finance — an internal reorganization, not an acquisition, with negligible headcount impact.

extras.salaryTrend: Rising sharply and continuously — the defining feature of Koei Tecmo's retention model. 8+ CONSECUTIVE fiscal years of base-up (ベースアップ). 2022: average +23% base-salary raise (Japan staff). 2023 (effective Apr, announced May 2023): +7.7% average employee annual income, monthly base-up incl. new-grad starting salary raised to ¥305,000/month (~¥3.66M/yr entry). Holding-company average annual salary (yuho basis, 113 ppl, incl. bonus & non-standard wages): ¥7,939,258 in FY2025. Group-wide average reported by third parties ~¥7.3-7.9M. No wage freezes or cuts on record.
extras.turnoverRate: Low (company's explicit selling point). No exact group turnover % is disclosed in the securities report. Proxy: holding-company average tenure of 9.4 years (long for Japan's game sector) and a strong retention narrative. No labor union (組合なし) but 'labor relations extremely good' per the yuho. Third-party salary sites and IR messaging consistently frame low attrition as a competitive advantage; treat any specific % as unofficial/estimate.
extras.avgTenureYears: 9.4 years (holding-company basis, 113 employees, FY2025 yuho). NOTE: this is the parent-company figure, not group-wide; operating-subsidiary tenure is not separately disclosed. Average age 38.2 years (holding-company basis).
extras.hiringPlans: Active, organic new-graduate hiring is the core growth engine — the company runs an annual shinsotsu (new-grad) recruitment cycle and mid-career hiring, adding ~150 net consolidated staff per year. Raised new-grad starting salary to ¥305,000/month to compete for talent. Growth is deliberately in-house rather than via acquisition. Entertainment (games) segment holds 2,342 of 2,684 consolidated staff (FY2025).
extras.turnover: Not officially quantified; framed qualitatively as low.
extras.expansion: Consolidated headcount +701 (+35%) over 5 years (1,983 FY2021 → 2,684 FY2025), all organic. Continued expansion into FY2026 (~2,835 est). Backed by industry-leading profitability: FY2026 operating margin 42.0% (up 3.4pp from 38.6%), revenue ¥88.4bn, operating profit ¥37.2bn, net profit ¥42.8bn — profitability that funds the sustained pay-raise/retention model. Also expanding overseas dev/publishing footprint (Koei Tecmo America/Europe) and a growing strategic securities-investment portfolio that boosts non-operating income.

keyFindings: Consolidated headcount grew organically from 1,983 (FY2021) to 2,684 (FY2025) — a +35% / +701 rise with NO acquisition jumps and NO layoffs, the textbook 'organic growth / retention' model.
FY2026 headcount estimated ~2,835 (aggregator, pre-official-filing) — official confirmation awaits the FY2026 yuho due ~June 2026.

CRITICAL BASIS FLAG: the ¥7,939,258 avg salary, 38.2 avg age and 9.4-yr avg tenure in the securities report are HOLDING-COMPANY-ONLY (113 people), a Japanese yuho reporting convention — NOT group-wide (2,684) figures. Do not blend them.
Games (Entertainment) segment = 2,342 of 2,684 consolidated employees in FY2025; Amusement 56; Corporate/common 286.
Pay: 8+ consecutive years of base-ups; +23% base raise (2022) then +7.7% income lift (2023); new-grad starting pay raised to ¥305,000/month. Directly funds retention.
Profitability underwrites the model: 42.0% operating margin FY2026 (up from 38.6%), among the highest in the global game industry; revenue ¥88.4bn, operating profit ¥37.2bn, net profit ¥42.8bn.
No labor union; 'labor relations extremely good' per the yuho. Low turnover is a stated competitive advantage but is not disclosed as an exact percentage.

data.fiscalYearEnd: March 31 (aligned with parent Kadokawa Corporation, TYO:9468)
data.summary: FromSoftware, Inc. (フロム・ソフトウェア) is a Tokyo-based developer, ~70% owned by Kadokawa Corporation (TYO:9468). Consolidated company-level headcount grew steadily from 288 (Dec 2019) to 456 (May 2025), roughly +58% over 5.5 years — organic hiring, not acquisition-driven. The Elden Ring era (2022+) triggered an explicit public "large-scale recruitment" drive for "multiple new projects." Notably, of ~400 total staff, roughly 300 are development staff, and Elden Ring + Armored Core VI were reportedly made with only ~300 developers (peak of ~200-230 devs on a single title). FromSoftware does NOT publish a standalone yuho/有価証券報告書 (it is a subsidiary, not a separately listed issuer); its headcount figures come from its own corporate "About/Company" page and recruiting materials rather than SEC-equivalent filings, so treat non-official-page numbers as company-reported estimates. Kadokawa's own consolidated filings report group-wide headcount, not a FromSoftware segment breakout. The Aug 2022 Tencent (Sixjoy Hong Kong) and Sony Interactive Entertainment minority equity investments were pure capital stakes — they did NOT change headcount (no consolidation/acquisition distortion). In Oct 2024 FromSoftware announced an ~11.8% average base-salary increase effective April 2025 (new-graduate starting monthly pay raised from ¥260,000 to ¥300,000).

headcount.note: NOT total headcount — this is the development-staff subset. Elden Ring + Armored Core VI were made with ~300 devs; peak ~200-230 devs on a single title. Staff are rotated across projects.

acquisitionDistortions: No acquisition-driven headcount jumps. FromSoftware's growth (288 -> 456) is organic hiring, primarily the post-Elden-Ring recruitment push for multiple new projects.

Aug 2022: Tencent (via Sixjoy Hong Kong Limited, ~16.25%) and Sony Interactive Entertainment (~14.09%) took MINORITY equity stakes via a third-party capital increase; Kadokawa retained ~70% control. These are capital investments, NOT acquisitions — they did not add or remove employees and do not distort the headcount series.

FromSoftware itself is a Kadokawa subsidiary since 2014 (Kadokawa acquired it from Transcosmos). It is NOT separately listed, so it files no standalone yuho/有価証券報告書; figures come from its corporate/recruiting pages, not securities filings. Kadokawa's consolidated group headcount (several thousand) must not be conflated with FromSoftware's company-level ~450.
keyFindings: Headcount grew from 288 (Dec 2019) to 456 (May 2025), ~+58%, entirely organic — driven by the post-Elden-Ring 'large-scale recruitment for multiple new projects' drive.

Only the May 2025 figure (456) is from FromSoftware's own official company page and is treated as non-estimate; 288/349/397/423 are company-reported/trade-press figures flagged as estimates (no yuho exists for this subsidiary).
Of ~400 total staff, roughly 300 are development staff; Elden Ring and Armored Core VI were made with only ~300 devs (peak 200-230 on a single title) — an unusually lean team for AAA output.
Aug 2022 Tencent (Sixjoy Hong Kong ~16.25%) and Sony (SIE ~14.09%) stakes are minority capital investments, NOT acquisitions — zero headcount distortion; Kadokawa keeps ~70%.
Oct 2024: ~11.8% average base-salary increase (effective April 2025); new-graduate starting monthly pay raised from ¥260,000 to ¥300,000 (+~15%) — a retention move amid the hiring push.
Fiscal year ends 31 March (aligned with Kadokawa). No layoffs reported.

extras.expansion: Post-Elden-Ring (Feb 2022) public 'massive/large-scale recruitment' drive for 'multiple new projects' spanning planning, design, art, programming, sound and PR. Headcount rose ~288 (2019) -> 349 (2022) -> 397 (2023) -> 423 (2024) -> 456 (2025).
extras.hiringPlans: Actively recruiting across all disciplines for multiple simultaneous new projects (open positions listed on careers.fromsoftware.jp). Development staff (~300) are rotated across concurrent titles rather than fixed per project.
extras.salaryTrend: Oct 2024 announcement: ~11.8% average base-salary increase effective April 2025; new-graduate starting monthly salary raised from ¥260,000 to ¥300,000 (~+15%), framed as building 'a stable income and rewarding work environment.'
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed; no layoffs reported.

data.company: Sony Interactive Entertainment / PlayStation (Sony Group, TYO: 6758 / NYSE: SONY)
data.region: Global (HQ San Mateo, CA & Tokyo; studios across US, UK, EU, Japan)
data.fiscalYearEnd: March 31 (Sony Group fiscal year; e.g. "FY2023" = year ended 31 Mar 2024)
data.summary: Sony Interactive Entertainment (SIE) is the PlayStation division of Japanese parent Sony Group, and it is the clearest example of a Japanese-owned company running Western-style, project-based games-industry cuts. Two headcount concepts must be kept apart. (1) Sony's OFFICIAL reported figure is for the "Game & Network Services" (G&NS) SEGMENT — a consolidated group-segment basis that is BROADER than SIE (it includes network/PlayStation Network operations and some hardware/business functions, not just game studios). Sony began publicizing this divisional headcount in 2020; it grew every year through FY2022, was FLAT at 12,700 at both 31 Mar 2023 and 31 Mar 2024, then fell for the FIRST time to 12,100 at 31 Mar 2025 — a ~600 net decline reflecting the 2024 cuts. (2) SIE's own "workforce" is NOT separately disclosed in filings; it is inferred from SIE's Feb 2024 statement that the ~900-person layoff equalled "about 8% of our current workforce," implying ~11,250 SIE staff (studio-team + division basis, estimate). GROSS layoffs must not be blended with these levels: SIE cut ~900 (Feb 2024), Bungie cut ~100 (Oct 2023) and ~220 (Jul 2024, 155 of those roles absorbed into SIE rather than eliminated), and Firewalk Studios (~172) plus Neon Koi (~38) were shut down in Oct 2024 (~210 total) after the Concord failure. Bungie was acquired Jul 2022 (~$3.6B) and is progressively folding into SIE; its headcount sits inside G&NS. The Feb 2024 round is the notable hybrid signal — a Japanese parent authorizing simultaneous studio closure (London Studio), across-the-board studio cuts (Insomniac, Naughty Dog, Guerrilla ~40/10%, Firesprite), and game cancellations, in the US/UK "restructure-to-a-target-headcount" style rather than the traditional Japanese lifetime-employment model.

headcount.basis: consolidated group segment — Game & Network Services (BROADER than SIE; includes PlayStation Network ops)
headcount.source: Sony corporate/sustainability data as reported by gamefile.news; exact 2020 figure approximate
headcount.note: First year Sony began publicizing divisional headcount (2020). Value approximate/from-memory ordering; treat as estimate.

headcount.basis: consolidated group segment — Game & Network Services (BROADER than SIE)
headcount.source: GlobalData (Sony Group employee headcount FY2017–FY2022)
headcount.note: GlobalData labels 10,200 as FY2022 G&NS. Period-labeling (Mar 2022 vs Mar 2023) ambiguous across secondary sources; see 12,700 figure below.

headcount.basis: consolidated group segment — Game & Network Services (BROADER than SIE)
headcount.source: Sony corporate data / Sustainability Report, via gamefile.news
headcount.note: gamefile.news reports 12,700 at BOTH Mar 2023 and Mar 2024 ('flat, snapping three years of growth'). Reconciliation note: GlobalData's 10,200 'FY2022' vs Sony's 12,700 at Mar 2023 suggests the two secondary sources use different segment definitions/period labels; verify against Sony's primary IR/Sustainability data.

headcount.basis: consolidated group segment — Game & Network Services (BROADER than SIE)
headcount.source: Sony Group corporate filing (Jun 2024) / Sony Sustainability Report 2024, via gamefile.news
headcount.note: Officially reported G&NS segment headcount. Flat YoY despite Feb 2024 layoffs because cuts were only partly reflected by 31 Mar 2024 and offset by other segment areas.

headcount.basis: consolidated group segment — Game & Network Services (BROADER than SIE)
headcount.source: Sony Group IR / corporate data (updated 3 Jun 2025), via gamefile.news
headcount.note: FIRST annual decline in G&NS divisional headcount since Sony began reporting it in 2020 (down ~600 from 12,700). Reflects Feb 2024 SIE cuts + Firewalk/Neon Koi closures.

headcount.basis: SIE division / studio-team estimate (NOT a filed figure)
headcount.source: Derived from SIE statement '900 ≈ 8% of our current workforce' (Jim Ryan, 27 Feb 2024)
headcount.note: 900 ÷ 0.08 = ~11,250. This SIE-specific number is NARROWER than the 12,700 G&NS segment figure; not disclosed in any Sony filing. Estimate.

layoffs.scope: Bungie (SIE-owned studio; acquired Jul 2022). ~8% of Bungie workforce.
layoffs.source: Kotaku / Game World Observer / Bungie Wiki (Fandom); ~100 'at least'
layoffs.note: Blamed on Destiny 2 revenue ~45% below projections; accompanied delay of 'The Final Shape' from Feb 2024 to Jun 2024. Bungie sits inside G&NS segment. Pre-dates the SIE-wide round.

layoffs.scope: SIE / PlayStation global — ~8% of SIE workforce. London Studio CLOSED entirely; cuts at Insomniac, Naughty Dog, Guerrilla (~40, ~10%), Firesprite, plus Technology/Creative/Support teams; multiple games cancelled (incl. Firesprite live-service Twisted Metal).
layoffs.source: SIE blog 'Difficult News About Our Workforce' (Jim Ryan, 27 Feb 2024); Variety; GameSpot; Game Informer
layoffs.note: The flagship hybrid event: Japanese parent authorizing Western-style project-based cuts + studio closure. Guerrilla's ~40/10% is the only publicly quantified per-studio figure; the 900 is a global total across all affected units.

layoffs.scope: Bungie — ~17% of Bungie's ~1,300 workforce. NOTE: of these, 155 roles were TRANSFERRED/integrated into SIE (not eliminated); ~65 net eliminated at Bungie; one incubation team spun out as a new PlayStation Studios studio. ~850 remained at Bungie.
layoffs.source: Variety; TechCrunch; Game Informer; vgchartz
layoffs.note: Deeper folding of Bungie into SIE. Gross layoff count 220 must not be blended with the 155 internal transfers. CORRECTION vs task brief: this round was JULY 2024, not October.

layoffs.scope: Firewalk Studios shut down (~172) after Concord's failure/sunset (Concord launched 23 Aug 2024, taken offline 6 Sep 2024); Neon Koi (mobile studio) also closed (~38). Total ~210.
layoffs.source: Bloomberg via Engadget/Variety/GameSpot; 172 Firewalk + 38 Neon Koi
layoffs.note: One of the fastest studio closures in industry history — Firewalk shut ~2 months after Concord's launch. Concord permanently sunset with refunds; no relaunch.

acquisitionDistortions: Bungie acquisition (closed Jul 2022, ~$3.6B) added ~1,100–1,300 staff to SIE/G&NS — the ~10,200→12,700 G&NS rise across FY2022–FY2023 is partly INORGANIC (Bungie consolidation), not pure organic PlayStation growth.
Bungie headcount is reported INSIDE Sony's G&NS segment, so Bungie's own layoffs (Oct 2023 ~100; Jul 2024 ~220) reduce the G&NS figure but are studio-specific, not SIE-org-wide cuts.

The Jul 2024 Bungie 'layoff of 220' includes 155 roles TRANSFERRED into SIE — a headcount RESHUFFLE within G&NS, not a net elimination of 220 from the segment. Blending the 220 gross figure with net segment change overstates losses.

Multiple studio acquisitions (Insomniac Aug 2019, Housemarque/Nixxes/Firesprite/Valkyrie/Bluepoint/Haven/Firewalk 2021–2022, Bungie 2022, Savage Game Studios/Neon Koi 2022) inflated the 2020–2023 G&NS growth; disentangling organic vs M&A-driven headcount from public data is not possible with precision.
keyFindings: Sony does NOT disclose an SIE-specific headcount in filings. The only official figure is the broader 'Game & Network Services' (G&NS) segment: 12,700 (31 Mar 2023) → 12,700 (31 Mar 2024, FLAT) → 12,100 (31 Mar 2025) — the first annual decline since Sony began reporting divisional headcount in 2020.

SIE's own workforce is best estimated at ~11,250 as of Feb 2024, derived solely from SIE's statement that the 900-person cut = ~8% of its workforce. This is an ESTIMATE and is NARROWER than the 12,700 G&NS segment figure.

GROSS layoffs 2023–2024 total ~1,430 across SIE + subsidiaries: ~100 Bungie (Oct 2023) + ~900 SIE-wide (Feb 2024) + ~220 Bungie (Jul 2024, of which 155 transferred internally) + ~210 Firewalk/Neon Koi (Oct 2024). These must NOT be summed against the segment net change of only ~600 (12,700→12,100).

HYBRID SIGNAL: A Japanese parent (Sony Group, lifetime-employment culture) authorized Western-style, project-based restructuring — full studio closure (London Studio), across-the-board studio cuts, and game cancellations — the same February 2024 wave that hit US/EU publishers.
TASK-BRIEF CORRECTION: The larger Bungie round (~220) occurred 31 JULY 2024, not October 2024. October 2024 is the Firewalk/Concord shutdown (~210). The ~100 Bungie cut was Oct 2023.
Guerrilla's ~40 (≈10%) is the only publicly quantified per-studio number from the Feb 2024 round; Insomniac, Naughty Dog and Firesprite cuts were confirmed but not individually numbered.
The FY2024 (Mar 2025) decline to 12,100 lagged the Feb 2024 cuts because layoffs weren't fully reflected by 31 Mar 2024 and were offset by other G&NS areas — hence the 'flat' FY2023 reading.

extras.expansion: Aggressive 2019–2022 M&A build-out (Insomniac, Housemarque, Nixxes, Firesprite, Bluepoint, Valkyrie, Haven, Savage/Neon Koi, Bungie, Firewalk) drove G&NS growth from ~9,700 (2020) to 12,700 (2023). Post-2024 posture shifted to consolidation/cuts.
extras.hiringPlans: No net expansion signaled post-Feb 2024; focus on 'future-readiness' and cost discipline. Continued selective live-service investment (Bungie's Marathon) alongside closures.
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed; elevated involuntary attrition in 2024 driven by layoffs/closures.

sources: SIE blog — 'Difficult News About Our Workforce' (Jim Ryan, 27 Feb 2024): https://sonyinteractive.com/en/news/blog/difficult-news-about-our-workforce/ (PRIMARY, company statement — 900 cut ≈ 8%, London Studio closure, Firesprite)

Variety — 'PlayStation to Lay Off 900 Employees, or 8% of Workforce': https://variety.com/2024/gaming/news/playstation-lay-offs-900-employees-sony-1235923839/
GameSpot — 'PlayStation Cutting 900 Jobs...': https://www.gamespot.com/articles/playstation-cutting-900-jobs-in-video-game-industrys-latest-round-of-big-cuts/1100-6521383/ (Guerrilla ~40/10%)

companies.name: Call of Duty franchise & studio network

data.company: Call of Duty franchise (Activision studio network — Activision Publishing, part of Activision Blizzard; acquired by Microsoft Oct 13, 2023)
data.region: Global (primarily US/North America — CA, WA, WI, MN; plus Canada [Beenox, Toronto], Ireland [Demonware/Dublin], China [Shanghai], Spain [Digital Legends])
data.fiscalYearEnd: December 31 (Activision Blizzard reported on a calendar fiscal year; since the Microsoft acquisition closed Oct 2023, results roll into Microsoft's June 30 fiscal year)
data.summary: Call of Duty is the extreme case of a single franchise concentrating a publisher's entire workforce. In its FY2021 annual report (filed/discussed May 2022), Activision Blizzard disclosed that "more than 3,000 people" were working on Call of Duty — equal to ~31% of Activision Blizzard's ~9,800 total employees and nearly HALF of its ~6,800 developers. By the franchise's 20th anniversary (Oct 2023), Activision said 15 studios were contributing to COD, i.e. essentially the ENTIRE Activision Publishing studio network (Infinity Ward, Treyarch, Sledgehammer as the three lead studios; Raven, Beenox, High Moon, Demonware, Activision Shanghai, Toys for Bob, Digital Legends, Solid State, plus central design/QA/localization/Team Ricochet as support). No official per-studio headcount is published; the ~3,000+ figure is the only hard franchise number. Per-studio sizes below are ESTIMATES from a widely-cited Oct 2022 games-industry headcount dataset (LinkedIn-derived), summing to ~2,900 across the six core studios — consistent with the 3,000+ official figure. After Microsoft's acquisition closed (Oct 13, 2023), two waves of cuts hit these studios: Jan 2024 (1,900 gaming-wide cuts; Sledgehammer ~30%, Toys for Bob 89 + Novato office closed) and July 2025 (~9,000 gaming-wide; Sledgehammer ~30% again + office lost, High Moon ~10%, with Infinity Ward, Treyarch, Raven, Beenox, Demonware, Solid State all touched). Toys for Bob spun out to independence Feb 29, 2024 after a "corporate mandate" had reassigned it to COD/Warzone support.

headcount.basis: franchise/team estimate (official floor: 'more than 3,000')
headcount.source: Activision Blizzard FY2021 annual report / 10-K (Dec 31, 2021), reported by TweakTown/Kotaku May 2022
headcount.note: OFFICIAL franchise-wide figure: 'more than 3,000 people are now working on Call of Duty' = ~31% of ATVI's ~9,800 staff and nearly half of ~6,800 developers. This is the only hard number Activision has ever given for total COD headcount. Basis = people across all COD studios, not a single team.

headcount.basis: consolidated group / holding-company (Activision Blizzard, Inc.)
headcount.source: Activision Blizzard 10-K, Human Capital section (Dec 31, 2021)
headcount.note: CONTEXT figure, NOT COD-only: ~9,800 full-time and part-time employees company-wide (68% North America, 25% EMEA, 7% APAC). Of these ~6,800 were developers. COD's 3,000+ = ~31% of the whole company.

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate
headcount.source: Games-industry headcount dataset (LinkedIn-derived), cited via ABGames/NeoGAF Oct 2022
headcount.note: Infinity Ward (Woodland Hills, CA — lead COD studio, Modern Warfare sub-series). Estimate, not a filing.

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate
headcount.source: Games-industry headcount dataset (LinkedIn-derived), Oct 2022
headcount.note: Treyarch (Santa Monica, CA — lead COD studio, Black Ops sub-series). Estimate.

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate
headcount.source: Games-industry headcount dataset (LinkedIn-derived), Oct 2022; cf. Wikipedia 'over 450' Aug 2021
headcount.note: Sledgehammer Games (Foster City, CA — third lead COD studio; also Melbourne ~150 + Toronto). Wikipedia: 'over 450' by Aug 2021. Estimate.

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate
headcount.source: Games-industry headcount dataset (LinkedIn-derived), Oct 2022
headcount.note: Beenox (Quebec City, Canada — COD support: PC ports, Warzone, remasters). Estimate.

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate
headcount.source: Games-industry headcount dataset (LinkedIn-derived), Oct 2022
headcount.note: Raven Software (Madison, WI — primary Warzone live developer; heavy COD support). QA testers unionized (GWA-CWA) May 2022. Estimate.

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate
headcount.source: Games-industry headcount dataset (LinkedIn-derived), Oct 2022
headcount.note: High Moon Studios (Carlsbad, CA — COD support, ports, Warzone). Estimate.

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate
headcount.source: From-memory / industry estimate; no official figure published
headcount.note: Demonware (Dublin, Ireland + Vancouver — online/networking/matchmaking backend for COD). Rough estimate; no filing discloses this. Treat as low-confidence.

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate
headcount.source: From-memory / industry estimate; no official figure published
headcount.note: Activision Shanghai (China — COD art/asset support). Rough estimate, low-confidence; no official disclosure.

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate
headcount.source: From-memory / industry estimate; no official figure published
headcount.note: Toys for Bob (Novato, CA) — reassigned by 'corporate mandate' ~2022 from Crash Bandicoot to COD Warzone support. Pre-2024 spinout est. ~150-200. Spun out to independence Feb 29, 2024. Estimate.

layoffs.scope: Microsoft Gaming-wide (Xbox + Activision Blizzard + ZeniMax) — ~8-9% of ~22,000 gaming staff; not all COD
layoffs.source: Bloomberg (Jason Schreier) / Phil Spencer memo, Jan 25, 2024
layoffs.note: First post-acquisition cut. COD studios directly hit: Sledgehammer (~30% of staff, main office closed), Raven, High Moon per CharlieIntel/Kotaku. CWA-unionized teams at Raven/ZeniMax/Blizzard Albany were shielded on some cuts via bargaining. The 1,900 is gaming-wide, NOT a COD-only figure.

layoffs.scope: Toys for Bob studio (COD Warzone support at the time)
layoffs.source: Wikipedia (Toys for Bob) / PC Gamer, part of the Jan 2024 1,900 cuts
layoffs.note: 89 laid off at Toys for Bob; Novato office closed, survivors moved to WFH. Studio then spun out to independence Feb 29, 2024 (~100 remaining staff, exact count not disclosed). Separates the studio from COD going forward.

layoffs.scope: Microsoft-wide (~4% of global workforce); gaming/Xbox among hardest hit — NOT all COD
layoffs.source: Bloomberg / PBS / Engadget, July 2025 (start of Microsoft FY2026)
layoffs.note: Second wave. COD studios hit: Sledgehammer again (~30% of staff, lost its office, moved remote), High Moon (~10%), plus Infinity Ward, Treyarch, Raven, Beenox, Demonware, Solid State all touched (Infinity Ward IT dept reportedly cut to one person). No official COD-specific tally; these are the reported studio-level impacts, not a discrete COD headcount number.

acquisitionDistortions: MICROSOFT ACQUISITION (closed Oct 13, 2023): Activision Blizzard's ~9,800 staff and all COD studios rolled into Microsoft Gaming (~22,000). After this, COD headcount is no longer separately disclosed in any filing — it disappears into Microsoft's consolidated Xbox segment. All post-2023 studio numbers are trade-press estimates, not filings.

The '3,000+' and per-studio numbers PRE-DATE the Microsoft deal (2021-2022 data) and reflect the pre-acquisition peak. The two Microsoft cut waves (Jan 2024, July 2025) mean current COD headcount is materially below the 3,000+ peak, but no official post-cut total exists.

TOYS FOR BOB spinout (Feb 29, 2024): removes ~100-175 people from the COD network as a corporate-structure change, not organic attrition — the studio left Activision entirely and went independent (with a Microsoft publishing arrangement). Do not read its departure as a pure COD headcount 'cut.'

CONCENTRATION EFFECT (not a distortion but the core finding): unlike a typical publisher, COD's growth did not come from one acquired studio — Activision progressively REASSIGNED its whole studio network (Beenox, High Moon, Raven, Toys for Bob, Shanghai, Demonware) onto COD/Warzone support, so 15 of ~15 studios and ~31% of the company ended up on one franchise. That is a reorganization of existing headcount, not M&A-driven growth.
keyFindings: Only ONE hard franchise-wide number exists: 'more than 3,000 people' on Call of Duty (Activision FY2021 annual report, May 2022) = ~31% of Activision Blizzard's ~9,800 staff and nearly half of its ~6,800 developers.

15 studios worked on COD as of the 20th anniversary (Oct 2023) — effectively the ENTIRE Activision Publishing studio network, with Infinity Ward / Treyarch / Sledgehammer as the three lead studios and the rest (Raven, Beenox, High Moon, Demonware, Shanghai, Toys for Bob, Digital Legends, Solid State, central design/QA/loc, Team Ricochet) as support.

Per-studio Oct 2022 estimates (LinkedIn-derived, NOT a filing): Infinity Ward ~635, Treyarch ~595, Sledgehammer ~532, Beenox ~493, Raven ~382, High Moon ~246 — the six core studios sum to ~2,880, consistent with the official 3,000+ once Demonware/Shanghai/Toys for Bob/QA are added.

Two Microsoft cut waves post-acquisition hit COD studios hardest: Jan 2024 (1,900 gaming-wide; Sledgehammer ~30% + office closed; Toys for Bob 89) and July 2025 (~9,000 gaming-wide; Sledgehammer ~30% again, High Moon ~10%, all major COD studios touched).
Sledgehammer Games was hit ~30% in BOTH waves and lost its physical office both times — the most damaged of the core COD studios.
Toys for Bob was pulled onto COD/Warzone by 'corporate mandate,' then bought its independence and spun out Feb 29, 2024 — leaving the COD network entirely.

After the Microsoft deal closed (Oct 13, 2023), no filing discloses COD headcount separately; all current figures are trade-press estimates. Real 2025 headcount is below the 2021-22 peak but not officially quantified.

extras.expansion: Growth was via REASSIGNMENT, not hiring/M&A: 2015-2022 Activision progressively moved Beenox, High Moon, Raven, Toys for Bob, Shanghai and Demonware onto COD/Warzone support until ~31% of the whole company (3,000+ of ~9,800) worked on one franchise.
extras.hiringPlans: Reversed since Microsoft acquisition — net contraction across all COD studios via Jan 2024 and July 2025 layoff waves; no announced COD hiring expansion. Ongoing annual cadence: COD ships yearly (BO6 in 2024, Black Ops 7 for 2025 by Treyarch+Raven).
extras.turnoverRate: Activision flagged elevated attrition and labor shortages in early 2022 (Axios, Mar 2022) amid workplace-conduct scandals and unionization (Raven QA formed GWA-CWA May 2022) — the first Activision studio union in the US.

data.company: Rockstar Games (Take-Two) / Electronic Arts / Blizzard Entertainment (Microsoft) — franchise & studio team-size estimates
data.fiscalYearEnd: Take-Two & EA fiscal year end 31 Mar; Microsoft (Blizzard parent) fiscal year end 30 Jun; team-size figures below are point-in-time team estimates, not fiscal-year headcount
data.summary: Team-size ("basis = team estimate") figures for the requested franchises. These are DEV-TEAM / STUDIO-TEAM headcounts, deliberately separated from company-wide consolidated employee counts and from gross layoff events. (a) Rockstar: GTA VI and RDR2 each drew on a consolidated ~2,000-person team pooled across all Rockstar studios (RDR2 credited ~2,000 total / ~1,600 developers per Rockstar & Wikipedia); Rockstar entered forced 5-day return-to-office in April 2024 and saw active IWGB unionization, culminating in the firing of ~30-34 workers (Oct 2025) that triggered the Rockstar Games Workers Union / IWGB dispute and an employment-tribunal blacklisting claim. (b) EA: Respawn/Apex Legends team hit by ~23 cuts (Mar 2024, part of EA's ~5% / ~670-role reduction); Battlefield Studios spans four studios (DICE >700, plus Criterion, Ripple Effect, Motive) totalling "almost a thousand" developers per EA/GamesBeat; EA Sports FC built by EA Vancouver + EA Romania. (c) Blizzard franchise teams: WoW core team ~250-300+ (broader ~500+ WoWGG-CWA union bargaining unit), Diablo IV core "Team 3" historically ~100-300 (9,166 total CREDITS incl. outsourcing/audio/localization — NOT core team), Overwatch "Team 4" ~150+.

headcount.basis: team estimate — all Rockstar studios pooled into one team for RDR2; Rockstar co-opted worldwide studios into a single team
headcount.note: RDR2 credited ~2,000 people total, of which ~1,600 were developers. Rockstar consolidated all its global studios into one large team. This is the closest documented analogue for GTA VI scale.
headcount.source: Rockstar Games (RDR2 credits) + Wikipedia 'Development of Red Dead Redemption 2'

headcount.basis: team estimate — dev headcount across all Rockstar studios (Edinburgh, Dundee, Lincoln, Leeds, London, New York/NYC, San Diego, Toronto, India) pooled for GTA VI
headcount.note: GTA VI is developed across Rockstar's global studios on the same all-hands single-team model as RDR2, so ~2,000+ developers is the standard press estimate. Context: forced 5-day return-to-office from April 2024 ('final stages of development, for productivity and security'), sustained IWGB organizing since 2019, and the Oct 2025 firing of ~30 workers that launched the Rockstar Games Workers Union/IWGB dispute. Distinct from company-wide counts below.
headcount.source: Wikipedia (GTA VI) + IWGB / Hypebeast / Kotaku reporting

headcount.basis: company/studio-wide estimate (third-party) — Rockstar Games entity, NOT the GTA VI dev team; NOT an SEC filing
headcount.note: Third-party workforce estimate for the Rockstar Games entity as a whole (all roles, all studios). Third-party sources disagree widely (~3,800 up to ~5,000-6,000 depending on inclusion of contractors). Provided only to contrast company-wide headcount with the ~2,000 dev-team estimate; do not blend. Take-Two consolidated headcount is a separate figure again (Zynga May-2022 acquisition inflated Take-Two group headcount).
headcount.source: Revelio Labs / third-party aggregators (estimate)

headcount.basis: team estimate — Respawn/Apex Legends team; total not officially disclosed
headcount.note: EA/Respawn have NOT disclosed the Apex Legends team headcount. Only the cut is dated: ~23 roles (mostly Apex Legends team) eliminated in March 2024 as part of EA's ~5% / ~670-role reduction. Value set to 0 because no reliable team-level total exists; see layoffs array for the dated gross cut. Apex reported ~18M monthly active players in 2024.
headcount.source: Game Developer / Game World Observer

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate — DICE (Stockholm) only, lead studio for Battlefield 6 multiplayer
headcount.note: 'Over 700 employees at DICE' per EA/GamesBeat coverage of Battlefield Studios. DICE leads multiplayer for Battlefield 6.
headcount.source: GamesBeat / FRVR relaying EA's Battlefield Studios blog + producer Alexia Christofi interview

headcount.basis: team estimate — combined 'Battlefield Studios' banner: DICE + Criterion + Ripple Effect + Motive (formerly Ridgeline, closed 2024)
headcount.note: 'Almost a thousand developers' across the four studios: DICE >700 plus 'hundreds of additional developers at other studios' (Criterion, Ripple Effect, Motive). Role split: DICE = multiplayer; Motive + Criterion = single-player campaign (Motive also multiplayer maps); Ripple Effect = battle royale. Ridgeline Games (single-player) closed 2024. One of the largest teams EA has assembled. A later 2026 'realignment' cut an undisclosed number of Battlefield 6 devs as player counts slumped.
headcount.source: EA 'Battlefield Studios' blog + GamesBeat / Windows Central

headcount.basis: studio-team estimate — EA Vancouver + EA Romania; per-title dev headcount not disclosed
headcount.note: EA Sports FC (FC 24/25/26) is developed primarily by EA Vancouver and EA Romania. EA does not publish a per-title team size; 'hundreds' of developers across the two studios is the qualitative characterization. A LeadIQ third-party profile lists ~900 for the 'EA Sports' entity but that is an aggregator estimate for the label, not the FC dev team. Value set to 0 for lack of a reliable team-level number.
headcount.source: Wikipedia (EA Sports FC / FC 25); LeadIQ (third-party, entity-level)

headcount.basis: team estimate — WoW core dev team ('Team 2'); range ~250-300+
headcount.note: WoW core dev team historically cited at ~100-300; grown via the 2022 Proletariat acquisition (~100 staff folded into the WoW team for Dragonflight). Separately, 500+ WoW workers formed the wall-to-wall WoW Game Makers Guild (WoWGG-CWA) union in 2024 — that ~500+ is a BROADER bargaining unit (designers, engineers, artists, QA), not the narrower core-dev team. Value 300 reflects the upper core-team estimate.
headcount.source: Wowpedia 'Team 2' + Game Developer (WoWGG-CWA union) + Laptop Mag (Proletariat)

headcount.basis: team estimate — Diablo core 'Team 3'; historical range ~100-300
headcount.note: Core Diablo team ('Team 3') historically ~100-300 people (2018 figure); larger during Diablo IV live-service + expansion (Vessel of Hatred) production. Do NOT confuse with the 9,166 people CREDITED on Diablo IV (MobyGames) — that total includes outsourcing, localization, and audio vendors (2,464 audio-related credits alone) and is NOT the core team. Microsoft laid off some Team 3 members end-2024 in a 3% workforce cut.
headcount.source: Diablo Wiki 'Team 3' + MobyGames (total credits) + Twisted Voxel

headcount.basis: team estimate — Overwatch core dev team ('Team 4')
headcount.note: Overwatch 'Team 4' fluctuated from ~40 early dev up to ~75 at beta launch (2016), reaching ~100 at launch and ~150 by 2018. Team 4 also carried Overwatch 2. Affected by Microsoft-era Blizzard cuts (2024 3% workforce reduction and the Jan-2024 ~1,900 Microsoft Gaming layoffs).
headcount.source: Overwatch Wiki 'Team 4'

layoffs.scope: Rockstar Games — ~30-34 staff fired (reported as 31), across UK/global offices; disputed as union-busting
layoffs.note: Rockstar fired ~31 workers (Oct 2025); the company said they had shared confidential info about upcoming games on a public forum, breaching policy. Workers/IWGB launched the Rockstar Games Workers Union and alleged union-busting; an employment tribunal upheld the workers' right to advance a blacklisting claim. Gross event — do not blend with team-size estimates.
layoffs.source: Kotaku / Escapist / Yahoo News / EGW

layoffs.scope: Respawn Entertainment — mostly Apex Legends team; part of EA's ~5% / ~670-role company-wide reduction
layoffs.note: ~23 Respawn roles cut, mostly on the Apex Legends team, alongside cancellation of games at Respawn. Part of EA trimming ~5% of workforce (~670 roles). Gross dated cut, separate from Apex team total (undisclosed).
layoffs.source: Game Developer / Game World Observer / Gayming Magazine

layoffs.scope: Microsoft Gaming (incl. Blizzard) — ~1,900 roles across Xbox/ABK/ZeniMax; hit WoW, Diablo, Overwatch teams among others
layoffs.note: Microsoft cut ~1,900 gaming roles in Jan 2024 after closing the Activision Blizzard acquisition (deal closed Oct 2023 — an ACQUISITION distortion for Microsoft/Xbox headcount). A further ~3% Microsoft/Blizzard cut end-2024 hit Diablo 'Team 3' among others. Company/segment-level gross cuts, not team totals.
layoffs.source: trade press (GamesIndustry.biz / Bloomberg) — from-memory corroboration of dated cut

acquisitionDistortions: Microsoft closed the Activision Blizzard acquisition Oct 2023 — any jump in Microsoft/Xbox Gaming headcount c. late 2023 is the ~+20,000 ABK addition, NOT organic; Blizzard franchise teams (WoW, Diablo IV, Overwatch) rolled under Microsoft Gaming and were subject to Jan-2024 (~1,900) and end-2024 (~3%) Microsoft-level cuts.

Take-Two (Rockstar's parent) acquired Zynga in May 2022 — Take-Two GROUP consolidated headcount jumped by the Zynga workforce (mobile), which is unrelated to Rockstar/GTA VI dev-team size. Do not read Take-Two group headcount as a proxy for Rockstar dev headcount.

EA's Battlefield 'Battlefield Studios' figure aggregates four separate studios (DICE, Criterion, Ripple Effect, Motive) under one banner; Ridgeline Games (single-player) was closed in 2024, so the combined count reflects consolidation/attrition, not a single organic studio.

extras.expansion: Rockstar consolidates all global studios onto one flagship title at a time (RDR2, now GTA VI), so its ~2,000-dev 'team' equals most of the company. Battlefield Studios (EA) is a deliberate 4-studio consolidation ('almost a thousand' devs). Blizzard's WoW team expanded via the 2022 Proletariat acquisition (~100 staff).
extras.hiringPlans: Rockstar forced a 5-day return-to-office from April 2024 for the 'final stages' of GTA VI; EA and Microsoft/Blizzard were in net-contraction mode (2024 EA ~5%/~670 cut; Microsoft ~1,900 gaming cut Jan-2024 + ~3% end-2024).
extras.turnoverRate: Not disclosed at team level; notable involuntary attrition via layoffs (Respawn 23, Microsoft/Blizzard ~1,900) and disputed Rockstar firings (~31).

keyFindings: Rockstar GTA VI dev team ≈ 2,000+ across all studios (team estimate), mirroring RDR2 which credited ~2,000 total / ~1,600 developers — Rockstar pools all global studios onto one title.
Rockstar's ~2,000 dev-team estimate must NOT be conflated with company-wide entity counts (~3,800 third-party) or with Take-Two group headcount (inflated by the May-2022 Zynga acquisition).

Rockstar labor friction is well-documented: forced 5-day RTO from April 2024, IWGB organizing since 2019, and ~31 firings (Oct 2025) that spawned the Rockstar Games Workers Union and an employment-tribunal blacklisting claim.
EA Battlefield Studios ≈ 'almost a thousand' developers across four studios: DICE >700 + Criterion + Ripple Effect + Motive (Ridgeline closed 2024).
Respawn/Apex Legends: EA never disclosed a team total; the only hard number is the dated cut of ~23 roles (Mar 2024, within EA's ~5%/~670 reduction).
EA Sports FC: built by EA Vancouver + EA Romania; no per-title dev headcount disclosed.
Blizzard core franchise teams (team estimates): WoW ~250-300+ (broader ~500+ WoWGG-CWA union bargaining unit), Overwatch 'Team 4' ~150+, Diablo 'Team 3' core ~100-300.
The Diablo IV '9,166 people' figure is TOTAL CREDITS (incl. ~2,464 audio, outsourcing, localization) — it is NOT the core team; core Team 3 is far smaller.
