data.company: Catalog: Closed studios & cancelled AAA projects 2022-2026 (Tango, Arkane Austin, Firewalk, Volition, Free Radical, Ubisoft closures, People Can Fly, et al.)
data.region: Global (North America, UK, EU, Japan, Australia)
data.summary: Catalog of ~20 named studio closures and cancelled AAA/AA projects across the 2022-2026 downturn, each logged as a dated layoffs entry with approximate headcount impact. Aggregate direct job losses tied to these specific studios/projects total roughly 2,300-2,600 people. The pattern is STRUCTURAL, not cyclical: closures cluster around (a) publisher-portfolio pruning after live-service/AAA bets failed (Concord/Firewalk, Redfall/Arkane Austin, XDefiant/Ubisoft SF-Osaka, Transformers Reactivate/Splash Damage), (b) post-acquisition consolidation writedowns (Microsoft-ZeniMax May 2024; Embracer's collapsed $2B deal driving Volition/Free Radical/Campfire Cabal; Take-Two dismantling Private Division), and (c) capital-starved independents/mid-tier unable to secure funding (Humanoid, People Can Fly, Deviation, Don't Nod). Whole studios with shipped IP and 15-20+ year histories were eliminated outright rather than trimmed — the defining signal of a structural reset, not a temporary hiring pause. Headcounts marked isEstimate=true are from trade-press/WARN reporting, not corporate filings; treat as approximate. FY note: Take-Two, EA, Ubisoft, Embracer all end fiscal year 31 Mar, so many of these cuts fall in the publishers' FY2024/FY2025.
data.fiscalYearEnd: Mixed. Take-Two, Ubisoft, Embracer, Enad Global 7 fiscal year ends 31 Mar; Microsoft 30 Jun; Sony 31 Mar. calendarYear used for all layoff dates below for comparability.

layoffs.scope: Tango Gameworks (Tokyo) — full studio closure by Microsoft/ZeniMax; Hi-Fi Rush / The Evil Within studio. ~105 staff. Studio later revived by Krafton (Aug 2024), which rehired ~50 originals; net permanent loss ~55 but full-closure impact logged as 105.
layoffs.source: Variety; PC Gamer; Wikipedia (Tango Gameworks)
layoffs.note: Cancelled: Hi-Fi Rush 2 (as Microsoft project) and unannounced titles. Studio-team basis. Krafton acquisition Aug 2024 partially reversed the closure.

layoffs.scope: Arkane Austin (Texas) — full studio closure by Microsoft/ZeniMax; Redfall / Prey (2017) studio. Some staff offered other Xbox roles.
layoffs.source: Texas WARN notice (May 7 2024); SiliconHills; KVUE; Game Developer
layoffs.note: 96 is the filed WARN figure — hard number. Cancelled: unannounced Arkane Austin project (reportedly a survival/Blade concept never greenlit). Studio-team basis.

layoffs.scope: Alpha Dog Games (Halifax, Canada) — full mobile studio closure by Microsoft/ZeniMax; Mighty Doom developer (sunset 7 Aug 2024).
layoffs.source: Game Developer; MobileGamer.biz; Windows Central
layoffs.note: Exact headcount not disclosed; small mobile studio, ~25-35 estimated. Studio-team basis.

layoffs.scope: The Initiative (Santa Monica, CA) — full studio closure by Microsoft; ONLY studio fully closed in the July 2025 Xbox restructure (part of ~9,000 Microsoft-wide / ~3,000-3,200 Xbox cuts). Perfect Dark reboot cancelled after ~7 years.
layoffs.source: Variety; Game Informer; Kotaku; PC Gamer; Wikipedia
layoffs.note: Studio ran <50 people; heavy prior attrition (~half of Perfect Dark core team left by 2022). ~40-50 estimated at closure. Cancelled AAA: Perfect Dark. Studio-team basis. Same round cancelled Rare's Everwild (Everwild not a full closure).

layoffs.scope: PlayStation London Studio (UK) — full closure by Sony/SIE within the ~900-person (8% of SIE) global layoff. 22-year studio (SingStar, Blood & Truth, The Getaway). Unannounced co-op fantasy PvP game cancelled.
layoffs.source: Push Square; Variety; TheSixthAxis; GamesIndustry.biz
layoffs.note: Studio-specific headcount not officially broken out; ~130-150 estimated. Cancelled: unannounced live-service fantasy title. Same Sony wave cut Firesprite, Guerrilla, Insomniac, Naughty Dog partially. Studio-team basis.

layoffs.scope: Firewalk Studios (Bellevue, WA) — full studio closure by Sony ~18 months after acquisition. Concord (launched 23 Aug 2024, servers pulled after ~2 weeks) cancelled/withdrawn.
layoffs.source: Washington WARN filing (174); Bloomberg (172 per company rep); Variety; GeekWire
layoffs.note: 174 = WARN filing; 172 = company statement to Bloomberg. Hard range 172-174. Cancelled AAA live-service: Concord. Studio-team basis. Emblematic hero-shooter failure.

layoffs.scope: Deviation Games (Los Angeles) — full closure; Sony-partnered new-IP studio founded 2020 by ex-Call of Duty leads (Dave Anthony, Jason Blundell). Unannounced Sony-published game cancelled.
layoffs.source: Game Developer; TheSixthAxis; GamerRant
layoffs.note: ~90 made redundant; studio had 100+ at peak. Bulk cuts in 2023, final shutdown Mar 2024. Studio-team basis.

layoffs.scope: Volition (Champaign, IL) — full closure by Embracer Group; Saints Row / Red Faction studio, ~30 years old. Closed 'immediately' as part of Embracer restructuring after collapsed ~$2B partner deal.
layoffs.source: PC Gamer; Game Informer; Engadget; Wikipedia
layoffs.note: 183 laid off widely reported (studio-wide). Studio-team basis. No specific AAA in active dev cancelled beyond Saints Row support; closure is the impact.

layoffs.scope: Free Radical Design (Nottingham, UK) — full closure by Embracer/Plaion (Deep Silver). Reformed 2021 to build a new TimeSplitters; that game cancelled.
layoffs.source: VGC; Nintendo Life; Game Informer; Wikipedia
layoffs.note: ~80 remaining staff let go. Cancelled: new TimeSplitters. Studio-team basis. Part of Embracer restructuring.

layoffs.scope: Campfire Cabal (Denmark) — narrative-RPG studio under THQ Nordic/Embracer, shut down <1 year after formation during Embracer restructuring. NOTE: partially reversed — a small core team survived (revealed May 2025) and continued its project under THQ Nordic.
layoffs.source: Game Developer; Game World Observer; Sweden Herald
layoffs.note: ~30 estimated; most staff laid off but studio not permanently dead. Studio-team basis. Included per instructions but flag the reversal.

layoffs.scope: Roll7 (London) + Intercept Games (Seattle) — both closed by Take-Two/Private Division within a ~5% (~600-person) Take-Two-wide cut. Roll7 = OlliOlli World / Rollerdrome; Intercept = Kerbal Space Program 2 (cancelled/abandoned).
layoffs.source: Bloomberg; VGC; Shacknews; MMORPG.com
layoffs.note: Combined ~60 estimated across the two studios; 'vast majority' of Private Division's Seattle/NY/Vegas/Munich teams also cut. Cancelled AAA/AA: Kerbal Space Program 2. Take-Two subsequently sold off Private Division. Studio-team basis.

layoffs.scope: Humanoid Studios / Humanoid Origin (independent, US) — closed due to lack of funding. Founded 2021 by ex-BioWare GM Casey Hudson (Mass Effect). Unannounced character-driven AAA sci-fi IP cancelled. NOTE: independent, NOT a Ubisoft studio.
layoffs.source: TechRaptor; Vertical Slice Games; Technetbook
layoffs.note: ~25-40 estimated; exact headcount not disclosed. Cancelled: unannounced AAA sci-fi. Capital-starved-independent case. Studio-team basis.

layoffs.scope: Ubisoft San Francisco + Osaka (closed) + Sydney (ramp-down) — XDefiant discontinued and its production studios shut. ~277-300 jobs (143 San Francisco, ~134 Osaka+Sydney, ~70 of which Sydney).
layoffs.source: Variety; DualShockers; PC Gamer; Nintendo Life
layoffs.note: 277 = reported filed/announced figure; ~300 including all. Cancelled live-service: XDefiant. About half of XDefiant team reassigned within Ubisoft. Studio-team basis.

layoffs.scope: Ubisoft Leamington (UK) — full closure (former FreeStyleGames; support studio for The Division, Far Cry 5, Skull & Bones, Star Wars Outlaws), plus targeted restructurings at Ubisoft Düsseldorf, Stockholm and Reflections. 185 total across the group; Leamington's own ~50-person team fully cut.
layoffs.source: Game Developer; ScreenRant; TheGamer; VGC
layoffs.note: 185 = announced group figure; ~50 = Leamington studio team. Studio-team + segment basis.

layoffs.scope: Ubisoft 2026 restructuring — closure of Ubisoft Winnipeg (~65) and Ubisoft Belgrade (support), plus San Francisco IT/marketing cuts and ~12% of Rainbow Six Siege dev reassigned (Barcelona refocused; 51 Barcelona jobs cut). Up to ~380 roles at risk. Follows Jan 2026 carve-out of AC/Far Cry/R6 into Tencent-backed Vantage Studios.
layoffs.source: Game Informer; Kotaku; MegaGames; Respawn/Outlook India
layoffs.note: Up to ~380 announced as at-risk across sites. Cumulative Ubisoft 2026 losses reported past ~680. Segment/multi-site basis. Barcelona strike (Jun-Jul 2026) in response.

layoffs.scope: Splash Damage (UK) — layoffs across two 2024-2025 rounds after cancelling Transformers: Reactivate (co-op AAA) and, later, Star Wars: Hunters wind-down. Studio had 300+ staff per LinkedIn.
layoffs.source: Kotaku; Game Developer; Kidscreen; Shacknews
layoffs.note: Exact totals never disclosed; ~100+ estimated across the July-2024 and Jan-2025 rounds combined. Cancelled AAA: Transformers: Reactivate. Studio-team basis. Studio sold in late 2024 shortly before further cuts.

layoffs.scope: Piranha Games (Vancouver) — MechWarrior studio, owned by Enad Global 7. Jan 2025 restructure cut 38 after MechWarrior 5: Clans sold below expectations.
layoffs.source: Massively Overpowered; Game Developer; PC Gamer
layoffs.note: 38 = reported EG7 restructure figure. Studio-team basis. See separate Apr 2026 entry for second round.

layoffs.scope: Piranha Games (Vancouver) — second round, ~30% of remaining staff (17 people) cut in 'economic restructuring'; MechWarrior franchise future in doubt.
layoffs.source: Massively Overpowered; PC Gamer
layoffs.note: 17 people = ~30% of staff. Studio-team basis. Cumulative Piranha 2025-26: ~55.

layoffs.scope: Hangar 13 (Novato, CA + global) — 2K Games studio (Mafia III). ~50 cut from Novato (studio had ~87 there), roughly halving the office, after cancellation of 'Volt'/live-service project. Restructured toward Mafia.
layoffs.source: Kotaku; Game Developer; VGC; Wikipedia
layoffs.note: ~50 in Novato + additional global cuts ('dozens across the globe'). Cancelled: 'Volt' (Marvel's Midnight-adjacent live-service reportedly). Studio-team basis. Studio not closed (later shipped Mafia: The Old Country).

layoffs.scope: Don't Nod (Paris/Montreal, publicly listed FR) — reorganization/voluntary-redundancy plan; ultimately ~59 departures (from an initially proposed ~69, ~1/3 of workforce) after Jusant and Banishers underperformed. June 2025 Montreal round: ~9 laid off + ~7 suspended.
layoffs.source: Game Developer; PC Gamer; Gamer Rant; investor communications
layoffs.note: ~59 final vs ~69 proposed (company reduced scale). Studio-team basis. No single flagship cancelled — restructuring driven by two commercial misses. Don't Nod is publicly traded (Euronext) — figures from investor comms.

layoffs.scope: People Can Fly (Warsaw, publicly listed PL) — January 2024 round; ~30 let go with the effective loss of Project Gemini (Square Enix work-for-hire lapsed). Preceded by Project Dagger cancellation (Apr 2024, Take-Two-published; PLN ~68-80M writeoff).
layoffs.source: Insider Gaming; Game Developer; Wikipedia
layoffs.note: ~30 in Jan 2024. Cancelled AAA/work-for-hire: Project Dagger (Take-Two), Project Gemini (Square Enix). PCF is publicly traded (WSE). Studio-team basis.

layoffs.scope: People Can Fly (Warsaw) — December 2024 round; 120+ let go after failing to secure ~$86M funding. Project Victoria suspended; Project Bifrost (VR) scaled down. Second 2024 round.
layoffs.source: Insider Gaming; Game World Observer; Yahoo Finance; TheSixthAxis
layoffs.note: 120+ = announced figure. Cancelled/suspended: Project Victoria, Project Bifrost. Studio-team basis. Cumulative PCF 2024: ~150.

acquisitionDistortions: Microsoft-ZeniMax consolidation: Tango Gameworks, Arkane Austin and Alpha Dog were all closed on the same day (7 May 2024) as post-acquisition portfolio pruning inside ZeniMax/Bethesda — NOT organic studio failures. Microsoft had acquired ZeniMax in March 2021.

Embracer Group's collapsed ~$2B partner deal (mid-2023) is the single root cause behind Volition (Aug 2023), Free Radical (Dec 2023) and Campfire Cabal (2023) closures — one holding-company liquidity event, not three independent studio problems. Do not treat as separate market signals.

Take-Two dismantling Private Division: Roll7 and Intercept Games closures (May 2024) plus the later sale of the entire Private Division label were driven by Take-Two's post-Zynga (acquired May 2022) cost program, not standalone studio performance. Kerbal Space Program 2's cancellation is collateral.

Krafton's Aug 2024 acquisition of Tango Gameworks PARTIALLY REVERSES the Microsoft closure — ~50 of ~105 originals rehired. The 105 figure is the closure impact; net permanent loss is closer to ~55. Do not double-count as pure loss.
The Initiative (Jul 2025) closure sits inside Microsoft's ~9,000-company-wide / ~3,000-3,200 Xbox July-2025 cut — the studio-level number (~45) must not be conflated with the company-wide 9,000.

Firewalk (Oct 2024) and Deviation (Mar 2024) both stem from Sony's Feb-2024 ~900-person (8%) restructuring and its live-service pullback; London Studio's ~140 is a subset of that same 900, not additive to it.

Ubisoft's 2026 Winnipeg/Belgrade closures follow the Jan-2026 carve-out of Assassin's Creed/Far Cry/Rainbow Six into Tencent-backed Vantage Studios — an ownership restructuring, distorting any read of Ubisoft 'organic' headcount.
Campfire Cabal was reported closed but a core team survived (revealed May 2025) — the ~30 figure overstates permanent loss.
keyFindings: STRUCTURAL, not cyclical. The dominant pattern is permanent elimination of entire studios — many with 15-30 year histories and shipped franchises (Volition/Saints Row 30yr, London Studio 22yr, Free Radical/TimeSplitters, Arkane Austin/Prey) — rather than temporary headcount trimming. Whole capabilities and IP stewardship were destroyed, which is the hallmark of a structural reset, not a hiring pause.

Aggregate direct job losses tied to these ~24 named studio/project events: approximately 2,300-2,600 people (using WARN/announced figures where available and conservative estimates otherwise). This EXCLUDES the broader publisher-wide waves (Sony ~900, Microsoft ~9,000, Take-Two ~600) except where a named studio is the identifiable subset.

Live-service / hero-shooter overreach is the single most lethal category: Concord (Firewalk, full closure ~174), XDefiant (Ubisoft SF/Osaka, ~277), Redfall (Arkane Austin, 96), Transformers: Reactivate (Splash Damage, ~100), Volt (Hangar 13, ~50). Failed multiplayer bets account for well over a third of the catalogued losses.

Post-acquisition consolidation is the second engine: Microsoft-ZeniMax (3 studios, one day), Embracer's collapsed $2B deal (Volition + Free Radical + Campfire Cabal), Take-Two-Private Division (Roll7 + Intercept). These are holding-company decisions, not studio-performance signals — flagged in acquisitionDistortions.

Capital access, not just sales, killed the independents: Humanoid (no funding), People Can Fly (failed to secure ~$86M), Deviation (funding/partner collapse). Mid-tier and new-IP studios without a live cash-cow proved most fragile.

Cancelled AAA/AA projects catalogued: Perfect Dark (The Initiative), Everwild (Rare, same round), Concord (Firewalk), Kerbal Space Program 2 (Intercept), Transformers: Reactivate (Splash Damage), Project Dagger + Project Gemini + Project Bifrost + Project Victoria (People Can Fly), a new TimeSplitters (Free Radical), XDefiant (Ubisoft), plus unannounced titles at Tango, Arkane Austin, Deviation, London Studio and Humanoid.

The wave did not abate — it moved down-market and into 2026: after the 2023 Embracer and 2024 Microsoft/Sony/Ubisoft/Take-Two shocks, 2025-2026 losses hit publicly-listed mid-tier (Don't Nod, People Can Fly), franchise stalwarts (Piranha/MechWarrior), and a fresh Ubisoft restructuring (Winnipeg/Belgrade, ~380). Duration and breadth reinforce the structural reading.

Data-quality caveat: most studio-level headcounts are trade-press/WARN-derived, not corporate filings. WARN-backed hard numbers exist only for Arkane Austin (96), Firewalk (174), Ubisoft Leamington-group (185) and Ubisoft SF/Osaka/Sydney (277). All others carry isEstimate=true.

extras.expansion: NET-NEGATIVE across the board for this cohort. The only meaningful re-expansions are Krafton reviving Tango Gameworks (rehiring ~50 and stating intent to reach 100+) and Campfire Cabal's surviving core team scaling back up under THQ Nordic. Displaced staff from Volition, Free Radical and Deviation formed successor micro-studios (e.g. Shapeshifter Games) but at a fraction of prior headcount.

data.layoffsBasisNote: Each layoffs entry represents a discrete studio-closure or major-cancellation event, with count = approximate jobs lost at that studio/event (studio-team basis unless noted). Publisher-wide totals (Sony ~900, Microsoft ~9,000 / Xbox ~3,000-3,200, Take-Two ~600) are referenced in scope/notes but NOT summed into the aggregate, to avoid blending studio-level with company-level figures per the hard rules.

corrections: Take-Two/Zynga close date is WRONG throughout the Take-Two record: the data repeatedly states Zynga closed '11 May 2022' (in FY2022 note, FY2023 note, acquisitionDistortions, summary, and keyFindings). The actual close was 23 May 2022 (confirmed by Take-Two/Zynga 8-Ks and press). The analytical conclusion (FY2022 7,799 is pre-close/pre-Zynga, FY2023 11,580 is first post-close year-end) is UNAFFECTED and remains correct — but every '11 May 2022' string should be corrected to '23 May 2022'.

Activision Blizzard FY2015 headcount: the data's 7,190 is sourced to stockanalysis.com, not the 10-K text. The ATVI FY2015 10-K itself states 'approximately 7,300 total full-time and part-time employees' as of Dec 31 2015. The data's central correction — that 2015 was ~7,000-7,300 (NOT the ~9,800 in the original task brief, which maps to 2018-2021) — is SOUND. Recommend presenting the 2015 figure as ~7,200-7,300 and citing the 10-K text directly rather than a secondary aggregator.

Minor sourcing note: EA FY2026 (14,600) and Take-Two FY2026 (12,909) are very recent filings (filed May 2026). Both Take-Two FY2026 (12,909) and FY2025 (12,928) were confirmed against SEC filing text; EA FY2026's exact 14,600 could not be opened directly but is internally consistent, correctly flagged, and the surrounding facts (take-private deal, Battlefield-studio March 2026 cuts as undisclosed/press-estimated) are confirmed.

checks.evidence: EA FY2025 10-K states verbatim 'approximately 14,500 people globally, with 68% located internationally.' Confirmed against the SEC filing.

checks.subject: EA FY2026 headcount

The $55B take-private (PIF, Silver Lake, Affinity/Kushner; shareholders approved 22 Dec 2025; outside date extended to Sept 2026) is confirmed. The March 2026 Battlefield-studio layoffs are confirmed real but 'undisclosed number' per Game Developer — consistent with the data flagging ~300 as a press estimate. 14,600 is internally consistent and correctly labeled.

checks.evidence: Take-Two and Zynga 8-Ks and contemporaneous reporting (Yahoo, GameWorldObserver, mlex) all state the $12.7B acquisition closed Monday 23 May 2022. The FY2023 10-K references the Zynga acquisition consideration as of that close. The date '11 May 2022' does not correspond to any close event. Note: the analytical framing (FY2022 pre-close, FY2023 first post-close) is unchanged.

checks.evidence: FY2023 10-K states 11,580 full-time employees, 7,064 outside US. Zynga (~$9.5B purchase consideration) consolidated. Jump correctly attributed to Zynga, not organic growth.

checks.evidence: Variety/Engadget/Hollywood Reporter confirm 16 Apr 2024 SEC filing: 5% of workforce (~600 media estimate), $160M-$200M charges, $165M annual savings, several games canceled. The data's ~550 midpoint and 'no official absolute number' caveat are accurate.

The CORE correction (2015 was ~7,000-7,300, NOT ~9,800 which maps to the 2018-2021 plateau) is CORRECT and well-founded. Recommend using ~7,300 with direct 10-K citation.

checks.evidence: CNBC and multiple sources confirm the ~$69B deal closed 13 Oct 2023 after ~20 months of regulatory review. ABK folds into Microsoft Gaming; no ABK-standalone headcount published thereafter. Discontinuity framing correct.

The data correctly flags 9,000 as company-wide and warns against reading it as gaming-only. King/Blizzard sub-figures are trade-press estimates, appropriately caveated.

result.notes: Verification was constrained by SEC EDGAR returning HTTP 403 to WebFetch, so filing text was confirmed via WebSearch snippets (which quoted several 10-Ks verbatim) plus reputable secondary reporting. All headcount anchors that could be cross-checked matched the data (EA FY2025 14,500; TTWO FY2023 11,580 / FY2025 12,928 / FY2026 12,909; ATVI FY2022 ~13,000; MS Gaming ~22,000 derivation). Two substantive errors found: (1) the Zynga close date is stated as 11 May 2022 throughout the Take-Two record but was actually 23 May 2022 — a factual date error, though it does not change the pre/post-close headcount analysis; (2) the ATVI FY2015 figure of 7,190 is a stockanalysis.com value while the 10-K text says ~7,300 (off by ~110) — but the data's important correction, rejecting the task brief's erroneous ~9,800 for 2015, is correct. The data's overall discipline on gross-vs-net layoffs, acquisition distortions (King, Zynga, ABK), holding-vs-consolidated, and fiscal-year timing is strong and the flagging of estimates (post-2024 Microsoft/EA layoff counts, MS Gaming segment headcount) is accurate.

checks.subject: Epic Games — Sept 2023 layoff basis
checks.claim: 830 layoffs = ~16% of workforce, implying ~5,190-5,200 Epic employees; separate ~250 divestiture departures (Bandcamp→Songtradr, SuperAwesome spun out)
checks.evidence: Tim Sweeney memo via CNBC/Variety confirms 830 layoffs = 16%, plus 'around 250 people leaving Epic through divestitures.' 830/0.16 = 5,187, so the ~5,190-5,200 back-calc is sound. The data correctly flags that GamesBeat's 'about 900' rounds the combined figure and that the brief's '~870 total' is unsupported.

synthesis.headline: Globally a churn-driven rollback that roughly holds 2019 headcount; in the United States a genuine structural contraction below the 2019 baseline, driven by geographic reallocation, labor-cost arbitrage, and margin discipline — with AI still a nascent rather than proven driver.

keyQuestionVerdict.answer: It is BOTH — but the split is geographic, not temporal. On a GLOBAL net basis it reads as a rollback/reallocation: heavy gross churn against near-flat net headcount. On a US net basis it is a STRUCTURAL contraction that has pushed direct employment below its 2019 pandemic-era level and keeps falling. The honest verdict is a nuanced 'structural in the US, rollback-ish globally,' with the structural reading dominant for the specific US-vs-Japan question this study asks.
keyQuestionVerdict.confidence: High for the US structural finding and the global-flat-net finding (two independent datasets converge); Medium on attributing the US decline precisely across geography vs AI vs margin, because those drivers are entangled and AI's share is not yet cleanly measurable.
evidenceFor: Global NET headcount is essentially flat: Satvat/ASGC shows ~750,000 (2022) to ~754,000 (2026), +4,000 jobs / +0.6% over four years, with ~48,000 roles eliminated against ~52,500 hired — the textbook signature of churn/reallocation, not collapse.

Gross layoffs are large but were absorbed by hiring in every year except 2024: 2023 saw ~10,500 gross cuts yet was the PEAK net-headcount year (S&P), and hiring reached breakeven with layoffs by Jan 2025 on a 6-week trailing basis, with quarterly layoffs below 700 since Q4 2024 (down from >6,000 in a single quarter in early 2024).

The 2024 net dip was shallow and possibly transient: S&P's -2% (2024) was the first contraction since 2010 but only 2 percentage points; Asia net fell only ~2% and Europe's gross cut share was ~6.7%, both consistent with a pandemic-overhang unwind rather than permanent destruction.

Part of the apparent US drop is definitional: the 143,045 (2019) to 104,080 (2023) ESA move is substantially a methodology tightening (small-firm/remote/hardware capture), so the pre-2023 'decline' overstates real job loss.
evidenceAgainst: US direct employment fell below its own 2019 baseline and kept falling on a LIKE-FOR-LIKE basis: 104,080 (2023) to 82,930 (2025) is a real ~21,100-worker loss the 2026 ESA report attributes mainly to software — this is net decline beneath the pre-pandemic level, the defining test for 'structural.'

North America net headcount fell -11.5% over four years with 19.2% of the regional workforce cut and an 11:1 job-seeker-to-hire ratio (127,000 seekers vs 12,000 hires) — persistent excess supply, not a temporary reset.

The cuts reshaped geography rather than just shrinking it: North America's share of new openings collapsed from ~40% (mid-2022) to ~25% (2025); by Sept-2025 Asia (4,601 openings) and Europe (4,008) both out-post North America (3,248), and >50% of all global layoffs hit California alone.

Whole studios with 15-30 year histories and shipped IP were permanently eliminated (Volition/Saints Row, PlayStation London Studio, Arkane Austin, Firewalk) — destruction of capability, not headcount trimming, which is a structural rather than cyclical signal.

The contrast case proves it is not a universal cyclical rollback: Japanese first-parties (Nintendo 5,120→8,666; Capcom 2,681→3,976; Koei Tecmo 1,983→2,684) GREW organically with no layoffs through the same window, so the US contraction reflects a specific Western operating model, not an industry-wide demand shock.
reasoning: Apply the study's own decision rule: big gross layoffs with roughly flat net = churn/reallocation (rollback-ish); net decline below the 2019 baseline = structural. The two metrics point opposite ways depending on the denominator.
At the GLOBAL level the flat +0.6% net over 2022-2026 (with hiring at breakeven by 2025) satisfies the rollback test — the pandemic over-hire was reallocated, not net-destroyed.

At the US level the like-for-like 104,080→82,930 fall is net decline beneath 2019's pre-pandemic 143k (even after discounting the methodology-driven part of the 2019→2023 move), and it is accompanied by permanent studio closures and an 11:1 oversupplied labor market — this satisfies the structural test.

Because THIS study's central axis is US-vs-Japan, the US structural reading is the load-bearing conclusion: the jobs did not simply come back to 2019-2021 levels in America; they left the country (to Asia/Eastern Europe/Canada-Quebec) and left the payroll (margin protection), while Japan expanded organically.

Confidence is capped at Medium on driver-attribution because geography, labor cost, and margin pressure are mutually reinforcing and AI's contribution is asserted by executives but not yet visible in a clean headcount series.

western: Project-based hire-then-fire: teams are ramped for a title and released on ship or cancellation — RDR2/GTA VI pool ~2,000 devs across all studios for one project, and Call of Duty concentrated 3,000+ people (~31% of Activision) on a single franchise before post-acquisition cuts.

Shareholder-margin orientation: S&P's own framing is the industry 'sheds jobs to protect margins'; Take-Two's 2024 Plan cut ~5% for ~$165M annual savings, EA cut repeatedly while rising net headcount masked it, Epic cut 16% (2023) then ~20% (2026) plus >$500M savings.

Geographic labor arbitrage: North America's share of openings fell 40%→25%; seats moved to Asia/Eastern Europe/LatAm where a US dev (~$114k) costs ~2.6x Poland, ~4.5x Vietnam, ~3.8-7.6x India; outsourcing/co-dev (Keywords ~14.7k staff, Asia-weighted) is now a structural support layer.

High churn tolerated as normal: GDC surveys show 11% of devs laid off in the year to Jan 2025 rising to 28% over two years by Jan 2026 (33% for US), with 48% of laid-off workers still jobless — individual insecurity coexisting with flat aggregates.
Permanent capability destruction: entire studios closed outright (Volition, PlayStation London, Arkane Austin, Firewalk, Free Radical) rather than redeployed.
Return-to-office as a cost/attrition lever: remote openings fell ~27.5%→14%, with mandatory RTO used to shed cost and induce voluntary exits.
japanese: Organic growth without acquisitions: Nintendo 5,120→8,666, Capcom 2,681→3,976, Koei Tecmo 1,983→2,684, Konami and FromSoftware (288→456) all grew via in-house hiring, not M&A step-changes or layoffs.

Retention and low turnover: Nintendo parent turnover ~1.9% with ~14.6-year tenure; Capcom 2.8%; Koei Tecmo ~9.4-year tenure and 'labor relations extremely good' — no layoffs on record for the pure Japanese first-parties.

Rising wages as strategy: Capcom +30% base (2022); Nintendo +10% base (2023) and further 2026 raises; Sega +~30% base (2023); Koei Tecmo 8+ consecutive years of base-ups; Konami five straight years; FromSoftware ~11.8% (2025) — pay up while Western pay is static/undisclosed.
Physical R&D expansion: Capcom built a new Osaka R&D building; Nintendo is constructing a large Kyoto development center (to ~2029) — investing in capacity even as Western studios close.
Profitability funds retention rather than distributes it as margin: Koei Tecmo runs a ~42% operating margin and Capcom ~17.5M yen operating income per employee, recycled into headcount and pay.

Where Japanese-OWNED firms DID cut, it was exported to the West: Square Enix, Sega Sammy, Sony/SIE, and Bandai Namco concentrated closures/layoffs on US/EU studios and divestitures while retaining and reinforcing Tokyo development.
contrasts: Adjustment mechanism: West fires to a target headcount; Japan lets attrition and non-replacement do the work (Ubisoft's ~4,000 net drop was mostly non-replacement of leavers, only ~750-850 confirmed gross cuts) — but Ubisoft is the Western-model exception in Europe, not the Japanese pattern.
Wage direction: Japanese firms raised base pay every year of the downturn; Western firms cut heads and rarely disclose wage growth.

Capital deployment: Japan built R&D buildings and grew teams; the West closed studios and returned savings to shareholders / pending take-privates (EA ~$55B PIF/Silver Lake; Ubisoft's Tencent-backed Vantage carve-out).

Franchise staffing: Western franchises balloon to thousands then get cut (CoD, Battlefield ~1,000, GTA VI ~2,000); FromSoftware shipped Elden Ring + Armored Core VI with only ~300 developers — lean, stable, rotated across projects.

The cleanest tell: through an identical 2022-2026 macro window, pure Japanese first-parties posted ZERO layoffs and net GROWTH, while US majors posted repeated gross cuts and (in the US) net decline below 2019 — same demand environment, opposite labor models.

grossVsNet: Gross layoffs are a FLOW (people cut, dated); net change is the LEVEL moving; a headcount figure is a STOCK at a date — adding or comparing across these categories is the single biggest error in the layoff discourse.

The 2023 paradox proves the point: ~10,500 GROSS layoffs occurred, yet 2023 was the PEAK net-headcount year (S&P) because continued hiring fully absorbed the cuts. Reading the 10,500 as shrinkage would be exactly wrong.

Over 2022-2026, ~48,000 roles were eliminated (gross-ish) but ~52,500 were filled, so global NET was +4,000 (+0.6%). Netting the ~48k against the ~750k base to claim a ~6% collapse would misstate a flat market as a crash.

Only 2024 saw gross cuts (15,631) exceed hiring enough to move global net negative — and only by ~2% (S&P). A single year of mild net decline is not the same as the cumulative ~43-55k gross layoffs headline implies.

Company-level examples: EA's year-end headcount ROSE every year FY2023-FY2026 while it ran five layoff rounds — hiring and M&A outran cuts, so rising net masked real gross churn. Take-Two's FY2025 rose to 12,928 while cutting ~5%, because the Gearbox acquisition added ~1,000+. Conversely Embracer's -4,532 (FY24) was NOT 4,532 layoffs — ~2,950 was the Saber divestiture (a sale), and later drops were Asmodee/Coffee Stain spin-offs (de-consolidation).

Regional netting is where the metrics finally diverge decisively: global net +0.6% (rollback signal) vs North America net -11.5% (structural signal). Quoting only the global net hides the US collapse; quoting only US gross layoffs (California >50% of cuts) overstates a global crisis. Both must be shown side by side.
aiAndLaborCost: Labor-cost differentials are the proven, quantifiable driver of the reallocation already underway: US dev ~$114k vs Poland ~$43k (2.6x), Vietnam ~$32k (4.5x), India ~$15-30k (3.8-7.6x); Indian studios advertise 50-70% savings on equivalent AAA-support work, and >50% of all global layoffs hit California, the single most expensive metro.

The offshored support layer is now institutional, not experimental: outsourcing/co-dev is a ~$1.9-4.2B market (2024) projected to ~$15.5B by 2033, with ~68-70% of developers using outsourcing and Keywords Studios (~14.7k staff, Asia/Manila/India-weighted) as the archetype — this is a durable structural channel for future cuts in high-cost geographies.

The mechanism is offshoring, not domestic remote: remote openings fell ~27.5%→14% while 86% of roles went on-site in lower-cost hubs; Western firms move the seat to a cheaper country rather than let a US worker keep it remotely — so future cuts will keep landing on expensive US/West-coast payroll specifically.

AI is a stated but not-yet-measurable driver: ~half of Japanese studios report using AI in development (CESA), Microsoft explicitly frames restructuring around 'AI-driven' priorities, and GDC surveys show rising AI adoption — but no clean headcount series yet isolates AI-caused job loss from cost/margin-driven loss.

Directionally AI most threatens the exact functions already being offshored — QA, localization, 2D/asset art, some tooling and support — meaning AI and labor-cost arbitrage are complementary future pressures on the same lower-skill/support layer, not independent ones.

Net assessment: for the FUTURE, labor-cost arbitrage is a confirmed, still-accelerating driver; AI is a probable amplifier concentrated on support/production roles, but any claim that AI has already caused the 2022-2025 cuts is unsupported by the workforce data — margin protection plus geography explains the observed pattern without needing AI.
outlook: Expect continued net-flat-to-slightly-positive GLOBAL headcount with ongoing REGIONAL reallocation: hiring is at breakeven with layoffs and quarterly cuts are below 700, but Asia/Eastern Europe/Quebec keep gaining share while North America keeps losing it — the churn machine stays on.

The US structural decline is unlikely to reverse to 2019 levels: with an 11:1 seeker-to-hire ratio and offshoring institutionalized, US direct employment (82,930 in 2025) will likely stay well below the 143k (2019) / 104k (2023) marks even as global output recovers.

2026 is a swing year: forecasts range from ~7,500 (Satvat, high std dev) to ~11,580 (ASGC live tracker) gross layoffs, and near-date Microsoft (~4,800, Xbox ~3,200) and Epic (~1,000) rounds plus Ubisoft's 2026 restructuring show the wave has moved down-market into mid-tier and publicly-listed studios rather than fully ending.

Japan's organic-growth/retention model should persist for the pure first-parties (Nintendo, Capcom, Koei Tecmo, Konami, FromSoftware) — rising wages, R&D buildings, low turnover — but Japanese-OWNED global publishers (Square Enix, Sega, Sony/SIE, Bandai Namco) will keep exporting cuts to their Western studios via layoffs and divestitures.

Labor cost arbitrage will keep compounding via the ~14% CAGR outsourcing/co-dev market; AI is the wildcard that could deepen cuts in QA/localization/art support if adoption matures, but its headcount impact remains to be demonstrated rather than assumed.

Watch the take-privates and carve-outs (EA-PIF/Silver Lake ~$55B expected ~Q1 2027; Ubisoft/Tencent Vantage; Embracer's split into Fellowship/Asmodee/Coffee Stain) — governance shifts toward margin-maximizing owners reinforce the shareholder-margin logic behind Western cuts.
caveats: GROSS, NET, and LEVEL are kept strictly separate throughout: the 8,500/10,500/15,631/9,053 figures are gross flows; +0.6% global and -11.5% North America are net; the 82,930/243,000/750,000 figures are stocks — they must never be added or netted across categories.

The two convergent net datasets use different universes: S&P covers ~75-90 large LISTED firms (its -2% for 2024) while Satvat/ASGC uses a ~750,000 broad-industry base (+0.6% over four years); they agree directionally but are not arithmetically interchangeable, and several Satvat annual LEVEL points are interpolations of his stated four-year total.

US ESA figures are only broadly like-for-like: the 2020 report widened NAICS scope, the 2024/2026 reports re-tightened it, so the 143k→104k→83k series is a consistent-but-not-perfect trend and the 2019→2023 portion is partly definitional.

Global and Japan headcount are estimate-heavy: there is no authoritative annual global development-headcount series, Japan's ~200,000 CESA figure is broad game-RELATED (not developers-only), and several private-company levels (Epic ~5,200, Riot ~4,800) are back-calculated from layoff percentages, not disclosed.

Acquisition, divestiture, and spin-off distortions must not be flattened into 'layoffs': Zynga (May 2022) and Activision (Oct 2023) inflate parent headcount; Embracer's -4,532 is mostly the Saber sale plus Asmodee/Coffee Stain spin-offs; Square Enix's -925 (FY2023) is the Embracer divestiture of ~1,100 Western devs; Ubisoft's Vantage carve-out reorganizes rather than cuts.

AI's causal share of the cuts is unproven: executive framing and CESA's '~half of Japanese studios use AI' establish adoption, not job destruction — attributing the 2022-2025 layoffs to AI would overreach beyond the workforce evidence.

GDC survey figures measure self-reported layoff PREVALENCE among a Western-skewed, self-selected ~2,300-3,000 respondents and cannot be converted into a net headcount level; 2026 values across all series are forecasts or partial-year actuals, not settled counts.
